readings.title: Capital Market Expectations, Part 1 — Framework and Macro Considerations
readings.orientation: Orientation. This reading is mostly conceptual — the macro backdrop for the return forecasting you do in Part 2. The two things you actually calculate are the trend-growth decomposition and the Taylor rule (both worked below). Everything else is about recognizing forecasting errors, reading the business cycle, and mapping the policy mix to the yield curve — so the reference grids in this document are your highest-yield study aids. Note: the source file duplicated the first/last LOS; this is 10 unique LOS.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Asset Allocation
Reading: Capital Market Expectations, Part 1 — Framework and Macro Considerations
Orientation. This reading is mostly conceptual — the macro backdrop for the return forecasting you do in Part 2. The two things you actually calculate are the trend-growth decomposition and the Taylor rule (both worked below). Everything else is about recognizing forecasting errors, reading the business cycle, and mapping the policy mix to the yield curve — so the reference grids in this document are your highest-yield study aids. Note: the source file duplicated the first/last LOS; this is 10 unique LOS.

LOS 1 — The role of, and a framework for, capital market expectations
LOS: discuss the role of, and a framework for, capital market expectations in the portfolio management process
What you must do:  Explain why capital market expectations (CME) matter and walk the 7-step framework in order.
Core idea. CME are expectations for the risk and return prospects of asset classes (not individual securities). They are the raw input to the strategic asset allocation, so they concern beta (systematic, macro-driven return); security selection concerns alpha. Good CME flow from the macro economy to asset-class returns to the IPS-driven allocation, and they must be internally consistent across asset classes.
The 7-step framework
	•	Specify the expectations needed, given the investor's tax status, time horizon, and the asset classes in play.
	•	Research the historical record — understand what actually drives each asset class, not just its average return.
	•	Specify the method(s) and model(s) you will use to forecast.
	•	Determine the best information sources.
	•	Interpret the current investment environment, applying experience and judgment to the data.
	•	Formulate expectations, checking them for consistency across asset classes.
	•	Monitor outcomes and use forecasting errors to refine the process.
Exam focus: A classic "list and briefly explain" prompt. Know the order and that step 6 requires cross-asset consistency — your equity and bond forecasts must imply a sensible equity risk premium, not contradict each other.
LOS 2 — Challenges in developing capital market forecasts
LOS: discuss challenges in developing capital market forecasts
What you must do:  Identify a forecasting pitfall from a described scenario and explain how it biases the estimate.
The nine problems
	•	Limitations of economic data — reporting lags, later revisions, and changing definitions/rebasing.
	•	Data measurement errors and biases — transcription errors, survivorship bias, and appraisal (smoothed) data that understates true volatility and correlations.
	•	Limitations of historical estimates — regime changes make the past a poor guide; data are non-stationary.
	•	Ex post risk as a biased measure of ex ante risk — after a strong run, prices are high, so realized (ex post) volatility looks low exactly when forward risk is elevated.
	•	Biases in analysts' methods — data mining (data snooping) and time-period bias.
	•	Failure to use conditioning information — you should condition estimates on the current phase of the cycle, not quote unconditional long-run averages.
	•	Misinterpretation of correlations — correlation is not causation; watch for a hidden third variable or a lagged relationship.
	•	Psychological biases — anchoring, status quo, confirmation, overconfidence, prudence, and availability.
	•	Model and input uncertainty — is the model itself right, and are the parameters estimated correctly?
Two of these deserve extra attention. Conditioning information means an estimate should reflect the current state of the world — quoting a full-cycle average equity risk premium at the top of a boom ignores that forward returns from expensive prices are lower. Model uncertainty (which model is right?) and input/parameter uncertainty (are the estimates accurate?) compound each other, which is why careful analysts stress-test forecasts across several models rather than trusting a single point estimate.
Watch out: Two traps dominate item sets: appraisal-based (smoothed) real-estate/PE data understating risk and correlation, and ex post vs ex ante risk — a market that just rallied looks LOW-risk on trailing data precisely when it is most expensive.
Drill: An analyst estimates equity risk from the last five years, which happened to be a strong bull market, and concludes equities are low-risk. Which error? (Answer: ex post risk as a biased estimate of ex ante risk — plus time-period bias.)
Exam focus: Vignettes describe an analyst's behavior and ask you to name the bias/error. Learn to distinguish data-mining (searching for a pattern with no prior hypothesis) from time-period bias (a result that holds only in the chosen window).
LOS 3 — Exogenous shocks and economic growth trends
LOS: explain how exogenous shocks may affect economic growth trends
What you must do:  Decompose trend growth and explain how an outside shock shifts it (versus a normal cyclical wobble).
Trend (potential) growth is the long-run sustainable growth rate:
Trend growth ≈ growth in labor input + growth in labor productivity
Labor input = population/labor-force growth + change in participation. Labor productivity = capital deepening + total factor productivity (technology). This trend is your anchor for long-horizon return forecasts.
Capital deepening (more capital per worker) tends to face diminishing returns, so over the long run total factor productivity — broadly, technology and efficiency — is the more durable driver of rising living standards and of sustainable growth differences between economies.
Worked example — building up trend growth
Estimates: labor-force growth 0.5%, participation adds 0.1% (labor input 0.6%); capital deepening 0.8% and total factor productivity 1.1% (productivity 1.9%).
Labor input     = 0.5 + 0.1        = 0.6%
Productivity    = 0.8 + 1.1        = 1.9%
Trend growth    ≈ 0.6 + 1.9        = 2.5%
Exogenous shocks are unanticipated events from outside the system that shift the trend. Sources: policy changes, political events, natural disasters, technological breakthroughs, financial crises, and changes in law/regulation. They can be positive (a productivity boom, pro-growth reform) or negative (an oil-price spike, a banking crisis).
Pro-growth government structural policies
	•	Sound fiscal policy and minimal government intrusion.
	•	Competitive and open markets (free trade, low barriers).
	•	Flexible labor markets.
	•	Investment in education and human capital / infrastructure.
Exam focus: Know that emerging markets typically have higher trend growth (catch-up) and that a shock shifts the trend, while the business cycle is fluctuation AROUND it. Expect a short qualitative prompt or a plug-in trend-growth calculation.
LOS 4 — Applying growth-trend analysis to CME
LOS: discuss the application of economic growth trend analysis to the formulation of capital market expectations
What you must do:  Link trend growth to expected equity and bond returns.
Long-run aggregate earnings growth ≈ nominal GDP growth
Equilibrium real (default-free) yield ≈ trend rate of growth
Equities. Over the long run, aggregate corporate earnings grow roughly with nominal GDP, so a higher sustainable growth rate supports higher long-run equity returns — this is the growth term that feeds models like Grinold–Kroner in Part 2.
Government bonds. Trend growth anchors the average level of the real risk-free rate: the equilibrium real default-free yield tends to sit near the trend growth rate, so faster trend growth implies higher average real yields.
Exam focus: This LOS mostly supports Part 2. Memorize the two directional links: higher trend growth → higher long-run equity returns AND higher average real yields.
LOS 5 — Comparing approaches to economic forecasting
LOS: compare major approaches to economic forecasting
What you must do:  State the pros and cons of each of the three approaches and pick the right one for a described situation.
Table 1 — The three forecasting approaches at a glance
Approach
Key advantages
Key limitations
Econometric models
Detailed, reusable, quantitative, disciplined
Complex and data-heavy; false precision; weak at forecasting recessions
Economic indicators
Simple, intuitive, available off-the-shelf
False signals; historically inconsistent; subject to revision
Checklist approach
Flexible; easy to add/drop variables; less complex
Subjective; time-consuming; not rigorous
Indicators detail: leading indicators include building permits, stock prices, the yield-curve/term spread, money supply, consumer expectations, and average weekly hours. A diffusion index measures the share of indicators pointing up.
Leading, coincident, and lagging indicators
Leading indicators turn before the economy — building permits, the stock market, the term spread, new orders, average weekly hours, and consumer expectations. Coincident indicators move with it — industrial production, payroll employment, personal income, and manufacturing/trade sales. Lagging indicators turn after — the average duration of unemployment, the prime rate, the inventory-to-sales ratio, and labor cost per unit of output. Only the leading set is used to forecast.
Exam focus: A reliable item-set question gives a described method and asks which approach it is, or asks for one advantage/limitation. The favorite fact: econometric models forecast recessions poorly. You may also be asked to classify a given indicator as leading, coincident, or lagging.
LOS 6 — Business cycles and short- vs long-term expectations
LOS: discuss how business cycles affect short- and long-term expectations
What you must do:  Match a described cycle phase to its rate, inflation, curve, and asset-return implications, and separate cyclical from secular views.
Key principle. Short-term (cyclical) expectations should be conditioned heavily on the current phase; long-term (secular) expectations should look through the cycle to the trend.
The engine behind the phases is the interplay of the output gap, inflation, and the central bank's reaction. Early on there is spare capacity, so growth can run hot with little inflation and policy stays easy; as the output gap closes, inflation pressure builds, the central bank tightens, and the conditions that supported risk assets reverse. Knowing where you are lets you set short-term expectations that differ from the long-run trend.
Table 2 — The five business-cycle phases
Phase
Growth & inflation
Rates & yield curve
Favored assets
Initial recovery
Accelerating off the bottom; inflation low/falling
Rates low; curve steep
Cyclicals, equities; riskier credit
Early expansion
Strong; unemployment falling; inflation still low
Rates begin rising; curve flattening
Equities, commodities
Late expansion
Above potential; inflation rising
Central bank tightening; curve flat
Inflation hedges; equities topping/volatile
Slowdown
Decelerating; inflation peaking
Rates near peak; curve flat to inverted
Quality bonds; defensives
Contraction
Falling; unemployment up; inflation falling
Central bank cutting; curve re-steepening
Government / high-quality bonds
For long-horizon (secular) forecasts, average across the whole cycle and lean on the trend-growth analysis from LOS 3–4; for short-horizon (cyclical) forecasts, tilt toward the current phase. Applying a cyclical view to a 20-year forecast — or vice versa — is a common mistake.
Drill: A vignette reports a closed output gap, a tight labor market, rising inflation, and a flat curve. Which phase, and what tilt? (Answer: late expansion — reduce duration and cyclical/credit risk, favor inflation hedges.)
Exam focus: Very high-yield. A common setup describes indicators (labor market, inflation trend, curve slope) and asks you to name the phase and recommend a tilt. Anchor your read on the curve slope + inflation trend.
LOS 7 — Inflation, the cycle, and asset-class returns
LOS: explain the relationship of inflation to the business cycle and the implications of inflation for cash, bonds, equity, and real estate returns
What you must do:  State where inflation sits in the cycle and rank asset classes under different inflation regimes.
Inflation and the cycle. Inflation is pro-cyclical but LAGS — it rises in late expansion, peaks around the top and into early contraction, falls through recession, and bottoms in recovery. Deflation becomes a risk in deep recessions.
The mechanisms behind the grid: nominal bonds suffer because a fixed coupon is worth less when inflation and yields rise (and they are prized when deflation lowers yields). Cash reprices quickly, so it keeps pace with rising rates. Equities depend on whether firms can pass costs through — moderate, expected inflation is manageable, but high or volatile inflation lifts discount rates and compresses multiples, while deflation crushes demand and raises real debt burdens. Real estate rents and replacement costs rise with inflation, making it a natural hedge.
Table 3 — Asset-class returns by inflation regime (relative to expectations)
Asset class
Deflation / below expectations
Inflation as expected
Inflation above expectations / rising
Cash
Low nominal yield; positive real
Normal real return
Attractive — yields reset higher
Bonds (nominal)
Positive — yields fall, prices rise
Coupon return realized
Negative — yields rise, prices fall
Equities
Negative — weak demand, real debt burden
Best — low & stable inflation
Negative — cost & discount-rate pressure
Real estate
Negative
Positive — income + growth
Positive — inflation hedge
Exam focus: Learn this grid cold — it converts directly into item-set answers. The one-liner: rising inflation favors cash and real assets and hurts nominal bonds and (beyond moderate levels) equities; deflation helps high-quality bonds and hurts almost everything else.
LOS 8 — Monetary and fiscal policy over the cycle
LOS: discuss the effects of monetary and fiscal policy on business cycles
What you must do:  Explain how each lever is used counter-cyclically, apply the Taylor rule, and read the policy stance.
Monetary policy (the central bank) sets short-term rates / money supply and is used counter-cyclically — tightening when inflation threatens in late expansion, easing in recession. Gauge the stance with the Taylor rule:
policy rate = neutral real rate + expected inflation
            + 0.5 × (expected inflation − target inflation)
            + 0.5 × (output gap, % of potential GDP)
Worked example — Taylor rule
Inputs: neutral real rate 1.0%, expected inflation 3.0%, target inflation 2.0%, output gap +1.0% (economy above potential). Actual policy rate is 3.5%.
rate = 1.0 + 3.0 + 0.5×(3.0−2.0) + 0.5×(1.0)
     = 1.0 + 3.0 + 0.5 + 0.5           = 5.0%
The rule prescribes 5.0% but the actual rate is 3.5%, so policy is relatively EASY (stimulative) — the central bank is behind the curve and would be expected to tighten.
Fiscal policy (government spending and taxation) stimulates via deficits and restrains via surpluses. Crucially, it is the change in the deficit — not its level — that measures the fiscal impulse. Fiscal policy suffers long recognition, action, and impact lags and is politically constrained.
Limits of monetary policy and the policy interaction
Monetary policy loses traction at the zero (or effective) lower bound: once policy rates approach zero, further cuts are impossible and the economy can slip into a liquidity trap, where extra money is hoarded rather than spent. Central banks then turn to unconventional tools — quantitative easing (large-scale asset purchases that pull down long-term yields) and forward guidance.
The two levers interact. Loose monetary plus loose fiscal is maximally stimulative; tight-plus-tight is maximally restrictive. Heavy government borrowing can push up rates and crowd out private investment unless the central bank accommodates. It is the combination — the policy mix — that drives both growth and, as the next LOS shows, the shape of the yield curve.
Watch out: Taylor-rule reading is directional: if the rule's rate EXCEEDS the actual policy rate, policy is easy/stimulative; if it's BELOW, policy is tight. And on fiscal: a large but STABLE deficit provides no new stimulus — only the change matters.
Exam focus: Two testable pieces: a Taylor-rule calculation with a one-line easy/tight conclusion, and the point that fiscal stimulus is about the change in the deficit.
LOS 9 — The yield curve as an economic predictor
LOS: interpret the shape of the yield curve as an economic predictor and discuss the relationship between the yield curve and fiscal and monetary policy
What you must do:  Read a curve shape as a growth signal and map the policy mix to the resulting slope.
Curve as predictor. An inverted curve (short rates above long) is one of the most reliable recession signals — it says the market expects rate cuts and slower growth. A steep curve signals recovery/expansion ahead, and the term spread (e.g., 10-year minus 3-month) is itself a classic leading indicator.
Table 4 — Policy mix and the resulting yield curve
Monetary
Fiscal
Yield curve
Growth impulse
Easy
Easy
Steep
Strongest
Easy
Tight
Moderately steep
Moderate
Tight
Easy
Flat to moderately steep
Moderate
Tight
Tight
Flat / inverted
Weakest
Drill: Monetary policy is tight and fiscal policy is tight. What curve shape do you expect? (Answer: flat or inverted — the weakest growth impulse.)
Exam focus: Memorize this four-box grid — item sets give you the monetary and fiscal stance and ask for the expected curve shape, or run it the other way.
LOS 10 — Linkages between economies
LOS: identify and interpret macroeconomic, interest rate, and exchange rate linkages between economies
What you must do:  Explain how trade and capital flows link cycles and rates, and apply the policy trilemma.
Macro linkages. Trade and capital flows synchronize business cycles across countries; correlation rises with trade/capital openness, economic size, and structural similarity, and is amplified by multinational firms and shared confidence.
Interest-rate and exchange-rate linkages. Closely linked economies see rates move together. Exchange-rate regime is decisive: a country that pegs its currency effectively imports the anchor country's monetary policy. This is the policy trilemma (impossible trinity):
Pick at most TWO of:
  (1) fixed exchange rate  (2) free capital flows  (3) independent monetary policy
Interest-rate differentials and the parity conditions (covered and uncovered interest-rate parity) tie the rate and currency stories together. Emerging markets are often more sensitive to these external linkages than large developed economies.
How linkages transmit
Cycles spread through two main channels. The trade channel: a slowdown in one country cuts its imports, hurting its trading partners' exports. The financial/capital channel: tighter global conditions or a risk-off mood raise borrowing costs and pull capital out of riskier markets all at once. A large economy also exports its policy — when a major central bank tightens, capital is drawn toward its higher yields, pressuring other currencies and often forcing those countries to tighten too, especially ones with pegs or heavy foreign-currency debt.
Exam focus: The trilemma is the single most testable concept here: a country that wants a currency peg WITH open capital markets must give up independent monetary policy.
Quick reference — key formulas
Every formula in this reading, in one place
Trend growth ≈ labor-input growth + labor-productivity growth
  labor input   = labor-force growth + participation change
  productivity  = capital deepening + total factor productivity
Long-run earnings growth ≈ nominal GDP growth
Equilibrium real default-free yield ≈ trend rate of growth
Taylor rule:
  rate = neutral real rate + expected inflation
       + 0.5×(expected inflation − target) + 0.5×(output gap)
Policy trilemma: choose 2 of {fixed FX, free capital flows,
                 independent monetary policy}
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. Do capital market expectations address alpha or beta?
   → Beta (systematic, macro-driven return); alpha is security selection.
2. Why does trailing data make a market that just rallied look low-risk?
   → Ex post risk is a biased estimate of ex ante risk — prices are now high and forward risk is elevated.
3. Trend growth vs the business cycle — what's the distinction?
   → Trend is the long-run sustainable rate; the cycle is fluctuation around it. A shock shifts the trend.
4. Which forecasting approach is notably poor at predicting recessions?
   → Econometric models.
5. Under rising inflation, rank nominal bonds vs real estate.
   → Real estate benefits (inflation hedge); nominal bonds suffer (yields up, prices down).
6. The Taylor rule prescribes 5% but the policy rate is 3.5%. Easy or tight?
   → Easy/stimulative (rule rate exceeds the actual rate).
7. Tight money + tight fiscal implies what curve shape?
   → Flat or inverted (weakest growth impulse).
8. A country pegs its currency and allows free capital flows. What must it give up?
   → Independent monetary policy (the policy trilemma).

readings.title: Capital Market Expectations, Part 2 — Forecasting Asset Class Returns
readings.orientation: Orientation. This reading turns the macro views from Part 1 into numbers. The three calculations you must be able to run cold are the fixed-income return decomposition, the Grinold–Kroner equity model, and the Singer–Terhaar risk-premium model. Each has a worked example below. The source file split the real-estate LOS across two lines; it's one LOS, treated as such here (8 LOS total).
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Asset Allocation
Reading: Capital Market Expectations, Part 2 — Forecasting Asset Class Returns
Orientation. This reading turns the macro views from Part 1 into numbers. The three calculations you must be able to run cold are the fixed-income return decomposition, the Grinold–Kroner equity model, and the Singer–Terhaar risk-premium model. Each has a worked example below. The source file split the real-estate LOS across two lines; it's one LOS, treated as such here (8 LOS total).

LOS 1 — Setting expectations for fixed-income returns
LOS: discuss approaches to setting expectations for fixed-income returns
What you must do:  Know four ways to build a bond return forecast, and be able to compute the full return decomposition for a bond or bond portfolio.
Approach 1 — Discounted cash flow / YTM
For an option-free bond held to maturity with no default, the yield to maturity is a strong estimate of expected annual return, provided coupons are reinvested near the YTM. It is the most defensible single-number estimate for a buy-and-hold investor, but it ignores reinvestment risk and any active view on rate changes.
If the horizon is shorter than maturity, YTM is a weaker proxy: realized return then depends on the reinvestment rate and the price at which the bond is sold, which is exactly why the full decomposition (Approach 4) is preferred for horizon-based forecasting.
And when there IS credit risk, YTM overstates expected return because it assumes no default. Subtract expected credit losses (probability of default × loss severity) to convert a promised yield into an expected return — the same credit-loss term that appears explicitly in Approach 4.
Approach 2 — Building-block (risk-premium) approach
Stack the compensations an investor requires:
Required return = short-term default-free rate
                + term (maturity) premium
                + credit premium
                + liquidity premium
This is the fixed-income analogue of the equity building-block approach and is useful when no clean market yield exists (e.g., a private or illiquid issue).
The term premium is the extra yield for bearing duration — normally positive and larger when the curve is steep. The credit premium compensates for expected default losses plus a premium for their uncertainty, and the liquidity premium rises for smaller, less-traded issues.
Approach 3 — Forward rates
Forward rates embed the market's expected path of future rates plus a term premium. If you believe the curve will evolve exactly as forwards imply, every bond earns the same short-rate return (the forwards are "realized"). Active value comes from an expected path that differs from the forwards.
Approach 4 — Full return decomposition (the tested one)
For a fixed-income position over a horizon, decompose expected return into its parts:
E(R) ≈ Yield income
     + Rolldown return
     + E(ΔP from investor's yield/spread view)
     − E(credit losses)
     + E(currency gains/losses)
where Yield income = annual coupon ÷ current price; Rolldown return = the price change from the bond "rolling down" an unchanged curve toward maturity; and Rolling yield = yield income + rolldown return. The view-based price change uses duration and convexity:
E(ΔP from view) = (−MD × ΔYield) + (½ × Convexity × ΔYield²)
E(credit losses) = probability of default × loss severity
Worked example — one-year expected return on a 5-year bond
Inputs: 5-yr, 3% annual coupon, current price 98. Over the next year it rolls to the 4-yr point on an unchanged curve, where its price would be 99. The investor expects yields to fall 20 bps; horizon modified duration 3.7, convexity 18. Expected default probability 0.5%, loss severity 40%. No currency exposure.
Yield income      = 3 / 98                     = 3.06%
Rolldown return   = (99 − 98) / 98             = 1.02%   → rolling yield = 4.08%
ΔP from view      = −3.7 × (−0.0020)
                    + 0.5 × 18 × (0.0020)^2    = 0.74%
E(credit losses)  = 0.005 × 0.40              = 0.20%
E(R) ≈ 3.06 + 1.02 + 0.74 − 0.20             = 4.62%
At the portfolio level, aggregate up: the portfolio's expected return is the market-value-weighted average of the individual bond expected returns, and the same weighting applies to each component (yield income, rolldown, and so on).
Watch out: Rolldown assumes the yield curve does NOT change — it's the return you get just from the passage of time on a static curve. The view-based term is added separately for any expected shift. Don't double-count by also moving the curve inside rolldown.
Drill: Recompute the example if the investor instead expects yields to RISE 15 bps. (Answer: view term = −3.7×0.0015 + 0.5×18×0.0015² ≈ −0.55%; E(R) ≈ 3.06 + 1.02 − 0.55 − 0.20 ≈ 3.33%.)
Exam focus: Item sets give you the pieces and ask for total expected return, or for one component (rolldown is the most common single-part ask). Currency G/L only appears when the bond is in a foreign currency — then it's the expected appreciation/depreciation of that currency.
LOS 2 — Emerging-market fixed income and country risk analysis
LOS: discuss risks faced by investors in emerging market fixed-income securities and the country risk analysis techniques used to evaluate emerging market economies
What you must do:  Separate ability-to-pay from willingness-to-pay risks and run the country-risk checklist with rough warning thresholds.
The risks
	•	Economic / ability to pay: volatile growth, large fiscal deficits, high and rising debt, weak external accounts.
	•	Political & legal / willingness to pay: weak rule of law, poor property rights and contract enforcement, political instability, and a history of default or restructuring.
	•	Currency risk: local-currency debt exposes the foreign investor to depreciation; hard-currency debt shifts that risk onto the sovereign's ability to earn foreign exchange.
Country-risk checklist (with rough red-flag levels)
	•	Fiscal deficit / GDP — concern above ~4%.
	•	Debt / GDP — concern when rising and above ~70–80%.
	•	Real GDP growth — want above ~4% for a catch-up economy.
	•	Current-account deficit / GDP — concern above ~4%.
	•	External (foreign-currency) debt / GDP — concern above ~50%.
	•	FX reserves / short-term external debt — reserves should comfortably cover short-term external debt; falling coverage is a classic crisis precursor.
	•	Also weigh the seriousness/credibility of policymakers and the structure of the economy (commodity concentration, diversification).
Worked example — reading a country scorecard
Country X: fiscal deficit 6% of GDP; debt/GDP 85% and rising; real growth 2%; current-account deficit 5%; external debt 60% of GDP; FX reserves cover only 70% of short-term external debt.
Assessment: every metric sits in the red zone — deficit and debt exceed the ~4% and ~70–80% thresholds, growth is below the ~4% catch-up bar, and, most critically, reserves do NOT cover short-term external debt. This is a high default/currency-crisis profile: demand a large risk premium or avoid.
Exam focus: This is qualitative — expect "which of these signals rising default risk" or "evaluate this country." The single most-watched ratio is FX reserves versus short-term external debt; when reserves can't cover maturing external debt, a currency/default crisis is near.
LOS 3 — Setting expectations for equity market returns
LOS: discuss approaches to setting expectations for equity investment market returns
What you must do:  Know the four approaches and be able to run BOTH the Grinold–Kroner model and the Singer–Terhaar risk-premium model.
The four approaches
	•	Historical statistics — sample averages. Weakness: non-stationarity and ex-post bias (a strong past run inflates the estimate exactly when forward returns are lower).
	•	DCF / Grinold–Kroner — a forward-looking decomposition of the equity return; the curriculum's preferred model.
	•	Risk-premium / building block — risk-free rate plus an equity risk premium.
	•	Singer–Terhaar — an international-CAPM way to build that risk premium while accounting for how integrated the market is with global capital markets.
Historical estimates — and why they need adjusting
Sample averages are the starting point but must be adjusted. Survivorship (success) bias inflates the historical equity risk premium because indices that survived and prospered are over-represented. Use geometric (compounded) means for multi-period expectations and arithmetic means only for a single period. The supply-side (Ibbotson–Chen) approach rebuilds the premium from fundamentals — inflation, real earnings growth, income return, and repricing — to strip out the unrepeatable multiple expansion baked into raw history.
Supply-side ERP ≈ income return + expected real earnings growth
                + expected inflation + repricing − Rf
Grinold–Kroner model
E(Re) ≈ (D/P − %ΔS) + (i + g) + %Δ(P/E)
Three intuitive pieces: expected income return = dividend yield minus net share issuance (buybacks make %ΔS negative, which ADDS to return); expected nominal earnings growth = inflation + real earnings growth; and expected repricing = the expected percentage change in the P/E multiple.
Worked example — Grinold–Kroner
Inputs: dividend yield D/P = 2.2%; expected net share change %ΔS = −0.5% (net buybacks); inflation i = 2.0%; real earnings growth g = 1.5%; expected repricing %Δ(P/E) = −1.0% (mild multiple contraction).
Income return   = 2.2 − (−0.5)     = 2.7%
Earnings growth = 2.0 + 1.5        = 3.5%
Repricing       =                   −1.0%
E(Re) ≈ 2.7 + 3.5 − 1.0           = 5.2%
Watch out: Sign of %ΔS trips people up: net buybacks REDUCE shares, so %ΔS is negative and the term −%ΔS becomes a positive contribution. Net issuance (dilution) is the reverse and drags on return.
Worked example — solving Grinold–Kroner for a missing input
A frequent exam twist gives you the expected return and every input except one. Suppose E(Re) = 6.5%, D/P = 2.0%, %ΔS = −0.3% (buybacks), inflation i = 2.2%, repricing %Δ(P/E) = +0.5%. Solve for real earnings growth g.
6.5 = (2.0 − (−0.3)) + (2.2 + g) + 0.5
6.5 = 2.3 + 2.2 + g + 0.5
g   = 6.5 − 5.0                = 1.5%
Gordon growth / DDM — implied equity risk premium
Rearranging the constant-growth dividend discount model gives a forward-looking premium straight from market data:
Implied ERP ≈ (D1 / P) + g − Rf
where D1/P is next year's dividend yield and g is the sustainable dividend/earnings growth rate. It is a quick market-implied read on the premium and a good cross-check on Grinold–Kroner, which is essentially the same DCF logic with an explicit repricing term added.
Singer–Terhaar model
Build the equity risk premium from the global market's Sharpe ratio, then blend a fully-integrated and a fully-segmented estimate by the market's degree of integration:
Global Sharpe ratio        = RP_GM / σ_GM
RP (fully integrated)      = ρ(i,GM) × σ_i × (RP_GM / σ_GM)
RP (fully segmented)       = σ_i × (RP_GM / σ_GM)      [correlation set to 1]
RP (blended)               = φ × RP_integrated + (1−φ) × RP_segmented
E(R_i)                     = Rf + RP_blended (+ illiquidity premium)
Worked example — Singer–Terhaar
Inputs: global market RP = 4.2%, σ_GM = 15% (so global Sharpe = 0.28); asset volatility σ_i = 20%; correlation with the global market ρ = 0.70; degree of integration φ = 0.65; risk-free rate = 2.5%; illiquidity premium = 0.4%.
Integrated RP = 0.70 × 0.20 × 0.28      = 3.92%
Segmented RP  = 1.00 × 0.20 × 0.28      = 5.60%
Blended RP    = 0.65×3.92 + 0.35×5.60   = 4.51%
E(R_i) = 2.5 + 4.51 + 0.4               = 7.41%
Read the result: a more-segmented market carries a HIGHER risk premium because investors can't diversify its risk away, so they price its full standalone volatility. As a market integrates (φ rises toward 1), its required premium falls — and that repricing produces one-time capital gains during the transition.
Drill: Rework Singer–Terhaar with φ = 1.0 (fully integrated). (Answer: RP = 3.92%, E(R) = 2.5 + 3.92 + 0.4 = 6.82% — lower than the partially-segmented case, confirming that segmentation raises the premium.)
Exam focus: Expect one full Singer–Terhaar calculation somewhere in the exam. The two steps graders reward: (1) computing integrated and segmented premiums correctly (remember ρ = 1 for segmented), and (2) blending by φ. Grinold–Kroner shows up as either a full build or a "solve for the missing input" (e.g., given E(R) and everything but g, back out g).
LOS 4 — Risks in emerging-market equities
LOS: discuss risks faced by investors in emerging market equity securities
What you must do:  List the equity-specific EM risks and the two-part way to evaluate an EM equity market.
	•	Higher volatility and drawdown risk, plus greater sensitivity to global risk appetite and capital-flow reversals.
	•	Political and economic instability (the same country-risk factors as in LOS 2) and currency risk.
	•	Weaker corporate governance and minority-shareholder protection — expropriation by controlling owners/managers, related-party dealings, and limited recourse.
	•	Poorer transparency and disclosure, weaker accounting standards, and lower liquidity/investability (free-float limits, foreign-ownership caps).
Two-part evaluation: (1) analyze country-level economic and political risk, then (2) assess corporate governance and whether the market is genuinely investable (free float, transparency, ability to repatriate capital).
Exam focus: Governance is the differentiator versus the fixed-income risk LOS. If a vignette stresses minority-shareholder treatment, disclosure, or free float, it's steering you to the EM-equity answer.
LOS 5 — Economic and competitive factors in real-estate returns
LOS: explain how economic and competitive factors can affect expectations for real estate investment markets and sector returns
What you must do:  Decompose a real-estate return, link the cap rate to fundamentals, and match property sectors to their economic drivers.
Cap rate and return decomposition
Cap rate = NOI / property value
Cap rate ≈ discount rate − NOI growth   →   value = NOI / (r − g)
E(R_RE) ≈ Cap rate + NOI growth − %Δ(cap rate)
The cap rate behaves like a Gordon-growth yield: it rises with the required real rate and risk premium and falls with expected NOI growth. A rising cap rate means falling values (the repricing drag), so it enters the return with a negative sign.
Worked example — expected real-estate return
Inputs: current cap rate 5.5%; expected NOI growth 2.0%; cap rates expected to rise, giving %Δ(cap rate) = +2.0% (values fall).
E(R_RE) ≈ 5.5 + 2.0 − 2.0   = 5.5%
If instead cap rates compress by 1% (%Δ cap rate = −1.0%): E(R) ≈ 5.5 + 2.0 + 1.0 = 8.5%.
Sector sensitivities
	•	Office — employment growth and business expansion.
	•	Retail — consumer spending and disposable income.
	•	Industrial / warehouse — trade volumes, manufacturing, and e-commerce logistics demand.
	•	Multifamily / residential — demographics, household formation, and income growth.
	•	Hotel — the most cyclical (leases reprice nightly), so it tracks the business cycle most tightly.
Watch out: Appraisal-based real-estate indices are smoothed, so they understate true volatility and correlations — the same measurement bias flagged in Part 1. Adjust (de-smooth) before using historical real-estate risk in an allocation.
Exam focus: Two testable pieces: the cap-rate return decomposition (a likely calculation) and matching a sector to its macro driver (qualitative).
LOS 6 — Forecasting exchange rates
LOS: discuss major approaches to forecasting exchange rates
What you must do:  Explain the main forecasting frameworks and their horizons, and reconcile UIP with the observed carry-trade result.
	•	Purchasing power parity (PPP) — relative inflation drives the currency; the higher-inflation currency depreciates. Poor short-run predictor but the main long-run anchor.
	•	Relative economic strength — stronger growth and higher real rates attract capital and support the currency in the short-to-medium run.
	•	Capital flows — inflows into a country's equities and real assets bid up its currency; sudden outflows do the reverse.
	•	Savings–investment imbalances — a country investing more than it saves must run a current-account deficit and import capital, which affects the currency and its sustainability.
	•	Uncovered interest-rate parity (UIP) — theoretically the high-yield currency should depreciate by the interest-rate differential.
The carry-trade wrinkle. Empirically UIP fails at short horizons: high-yield currencies tend to hold up or appreciate, so borrowing low-yield and investing high-yield (the carry trade) has historically paid — until it unwinds violently in risk-off episodes ("picking up pennies in front of a steamroller"). Also weigh current-account sustainability, hot-money flows, and central-bank intervention/reserve adequacy.
Interest-rate parity: covered vs uncovered
Covered interest-rate parity (CIP) is an arbitrage identity enforced by the forward market — the forward premium/discount exactly offsets the interest-rate differential, so a fully-hedged foreign investment earns the domestic rate. Uncovered interest-rate parity (UIP) is the unhedged expectation — the expected spot change equals the differential, so the high-yield currency should depreciate. CIP holds by arbitrage; UIP is only a theory and fails at short horizons, and that failure is exactly what makes the carry trade pay.
CIP (holds):    F/S ≈ (1 + r_domestic) / (1 + r_foreign)
UIP (theory):   E(%Δ foreign currency) ≈ r_domestic − r_foreign
Worked example — carry vs UIP
Funding (domestic) rate 1.0%, investment (foreign) rate 5.0%.
Interest differential        = 5.0 − 1.0     = 4.0%
UIP says foreign currency ↓   ≈ −4.0%  → net ≈ 0
Carry view (currency flat)    = +4.0% earned
UIP predicts the 4% yield pickup is erased by depreciation. The carry trader bets it won't be — pocketing ~4% while the currency holds, but exposed to a sharp loss if it snaps back in a risk-off move.
Exam focus: Usually qualitative: match an approach to a described situation, or explain why a high-rate currency might strengthen despite UIP (forward-rate bias / carry). Know that PPP is long-run, relative economic strength is short-run, and CIP — unlike UIP — always holds.
LOS 7 — Forecasting volatility
LOS: discuss methods of forecasting volatility
What you must do:  Know the methods, and specifically why a large covariance matrix needs factor models or shrinkage.
	•	Sample standard deviation (historical). Simple, but assumes stationarity and is sensitive to the window and to outliers.
	•	The large-matrix problem. With N assets you must estimate N variances and N(N−1)/2 covariances. When the number of observations is small relative to N, the sample covariance matrix is unstable and can be non-invertible (singular) — unusable for optimization.
	•	Factor-based covariance matrices. Impose structure via a few common factors, drastically cutting the number of parameters and guaranteeing a well-behaved, invertible matrix.
	•	Shrinkage estimation. Take a weighted average of the noisy sample matrix and a structured target (e.g., a factor matrix). This lowers estimation error and stabilizes the estimate.
	•	ARCH / GARCH. Volatility clusters (calm follows calm, turbulence follows turbulence); these models make current variance depend on recent shocks and recent variance:
GARCH(1,1):  σ²_t = ω + α·ε²_(t−1) + β·σ²_(t−1)
	•	EWMA (exponentially weighted moving average). A simple time-varying estimator that weights recent observations more heavily through a decay factor λ; a special case of GARCH that also captures clustering with a single parameter.
	•	Implied volatility. Backed out of option prices — forward-looking and reflects the market's current expectation (e.g., the VIX).
Exam focus: Highest-probability point: when assets outnumber observations, use factor models or shrinkage to get a usable covariance matrix. Also be ready to distinguish backward-looking historical/GARCH estimates from forward-looking implied volatility.
LOS 8 — Recommending changes to global portfolio weights
LOS: recommend and justify changes in the component weights of a global investment portfolio based on trends and expected changes in macroeconomic factors
What you must do:  Turn a macro forecast into specific over/underweights and defend each with a transmission mechanism. This is an essay LOS.
The playbook (tie each move to the macro view)
	•	Duration: shorten when you expect yields to rise (tightening / late cycle); extend when you expect easing or recession.
	•	Credit: reduce high-yield/spread exposure late-cycle when spreads are likely to widen; add credit in early recovery when spreads compress.
	•	Equity regions/sectors: overweight markets with improving relative growth and reasonable valuations; favor cyclicals early-cycle and defensives late-cycle.
	•	Real assets: add real estate/commodities when inflation is expected to run above expectations.
	•	Currency: hedge or tilt based on the exchange-rate view (PPP long-run, relative strength/flows short-run).
Two grading anchors: (1) state the causal chain — e.g., "expected central-bank tightening → higher yields → shorten duration" — rather than just naming a trade; and (2) keep the recommendation internally consistent across asset classes (your rate, credit, and equity calls should reflect the same macro scenario).
Worked example — a model constructed-response answer
Prompt: The economy is late-cycle, the central bank has signaled further tightening, and inflation is running above target. Recommend and justify two portfolio tilts.
Tilt 1 — Shorten duration. Expected tightening pushes yields higher; higher yields reduce bond prices, and a shorter duration limits that capital loss.
Tilt 2 — Reduce high-yield credit and add real assets. Late-cycle, credit spreads tend to widen, which hurts high-yield; meanwhile above-target inflation favors real estate and commodities, which tend to rise with inflation.
Why this scores: each tilt names a specific move, gives the causal chain, and both reflect the SAME late-cycle/rising-inflation scenario — so the answer is internally consistent.
Exam focus: This synthesizes the whole reading and appears in constructed-response form: you'll be given a macro scenario and asked to recommend and justify two or three specific tilts. Always pair each tilt with its mechanism and cite the direction of the effect.
Quick reference — key formulas
Every formula in this reading, in one place
Fixed-income expected return
E(R) ≈ Yield income + Rolldown + (−MD·Δy + ½·Cvx·Δy²) − (PD×LGD) + FX
Building blocks: Rf(short) + term + credit + liquidity premiums
Equity expected return
Grinold–Kroner: E(Re) ≈ (D/P − %ΔS) + (i + g) + %Δ(P/E)
Gordon implied ERP: (D1/P) + g − Rf
Singer–Terhaar risk premium
RP_integrated = ρ(i,GM) · σ_i · (RP_GM/σ_GM)
RP_segmented  = σ_i · (RP_GM/σ_GM)      [ρ = 1]
RP_blended    = φ·RP_int + (1−φ)·RP_seg
E(R_i)        = Rf + RP_blended + illiquidity premium
Real estate & volatility
E(R_RE) ≈ Cap rate + NOI growth − %Δ(cap rate);   Cap rate ≈ r − g
GARCH(1,1): σ²_t = ω + α·ε²_(t−1) + β·σ²_(t−1)
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. In the fixed-income decomposition, which component assumes an unchanged yield curve?
   → Rolldown return.
2. Do net buybacks raise or lower the Grinold–Kroner return, and through which term?
   → Raise it; %ΔS is negative, so −%ΔS is a positive income-return contribution.
3. What correlation does Singer–Terhaar assume for the fully-segmented premium?
   → 1.0.
4. As a market integrates, what happens to its required premium and its price during the transition?
   → Premium falls; price rises (a one-time capital gain).
5. Which single external ratio best flags an approaching EM sovereign crisis?
   → FX reserves relative to short-term external debt.
6. When assets outnumber return observations, why is the sample covariance matrix unusable, and what fixes it?
   → It is unstable / non-invertible (singular); use factor models or shrinkage.
7. Over what horizon is PPP a useful exchange-rate predictor?
   → The long run (it is a poor short-run predictor).

readings.title: Overview of Asset Allocation
readings.orientation: Orientation. This reading sets up the whole asset-allocation topic. The spine is the three-way split — asset-only, liability-relative, and goals-based — so the comparison grid below is the anchor you keep coming back to. The other must-knows are the economic balance sheet (human capital drives the tilt), the criteria for defining an asset class, the factor-vs-asset-class debate, and the rebalancing corridor logic. Note: the source file duplicated the governance LOS and split the implementation LOS across two lines; this is 10 unique LOS.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Asset Allocation
Reading: Overview of Asset Allocation
Orientation. This reading sets up the whole asset-allocation topic. The spine is the three-way split — asset-only, liability-relative, and goals-based — so the comparison grid below is the anchor you keep coming back to. The other must-knows are the economic balance sheet (human capital drives the tilt), the criteria for defining an asset class, the factor-vs-asset-class debate, and the rebalancing corridor logic. Note: the source file duplicated the governance LOS and split the implementation LOS across two lines; this is 10 unique LOS.

LOS 1 — Elements of effective investment governance
LOS: describe elements of effective investment governance and investment governance considerations in asset allocation
What you must do:  List the elements of a sound governance model and spot a governance weakness in a described setup.
Core idea. Investment governance is the framework for making and overseeing decisions in the interest of beneficiaries. It runs down a hierarchy — governing body (board) → investment committee → staff → external managers — and its guiding rule is that each decision should sit with whoever has the time, knowledge, and resources to make it well.
The six elements of an effective governance structure
	•	Articulate the long- and short-term investment objectives.
	•	Allocate decision rights and responsibilities across the hierarchy, matched to each party's knowledge, capacity, and time.
	•	Specify the process for developing and approving the investment policy statement (IPS).
	•	Specify the process for developing and approving the strategic asset allocation.
	•	Establish a reporting framework to monitor progress toward objectives and compliance with the IPS.
	•	Periodically undertake a governance audit.
Two considerations specific to asset allocation. First, the governance structure must have the capacity to develop, approve, and periodically review the strategic asset allocation — the single highest-impact decision — rather than letting it be set by default. Second, governance is not free: a governance budget recognizes that more complex strategies (active management, alternatives, frequent tactical shifts) demand more expertise and oversight, and a structure should not adopt strategies it cannot properly govern. The periodic governance audit checks that the framework is actually functioning as designed.
Exam focus: Either "list the elements" or, more often, "identify the governance weakness." The classic flaw: a board making detailed manager-selection or tactical decisions it lacks the time and expertise for — those should be delegated to staff or committees with the requisite skill.
LOS 2 — The economic balance sheet
LOS: formulate an economic balance sheet for a client and interpret its implications for asset allocation
What you must do:  Build an economic balance sheet including nonfinancial items and infer the resulting portfolio tilt.
The economic balance sheet extends the conventional one to include extended (nonfinancial) assets and liabilities:
Economic assets      = financial assets + human capital
                       + PV of expected pension/benefits
Economic liabilities = financial debts + PV of future consumption
Economic net worth   = economic assets − economic liabilities
Human capital is the present value of expected future earnings, and it drives the tilt. Bond-like human capital (stable, secure salary) supports MORE equity in the financial portfolio; equity-like human capital (variable, cyclical income) argues for MORE bonds. Human capital also shrinks with age, so portfolios typically de-risk over the life cycle as financial capital replaces it.
Worked example — two clients, same portfolio, different tilt
Client A — tenured professor, highly stable salary (bond-like human capital). Because her "extended" balance sheet is already heavy in a bond-like asset, she can hold a MORE equity-tilted financial portfolio without over-concentrating in risk.
Client B — commission-based entrepreneur, volatile and cyclical income (equity-like human capital). His financial portfolio should hold MORE bonds to offset the equity-like risk already on his economic balance sheet.
Worked example — a numeric economic balance sheet
A 45-year-old has: investment portfolio 600; home equity 250; human capital (PV of future salary) 900; PV of expected pension 200. Financial debts: mortgage 200. PV of future consumption needs: 1,100. (All figures in 000s.)
Economic assets      = 600 + 250 + 900 + 200 = 1,950
Economic liabilities = 200 + 1,100          = 1,300
Economic net worth   = 1,950 − 1,300        =   650
Human capital (900) is the largest single asset and, being salary-based, is bond-like — so the 600 financial portfolio can carry a healthy equity weight without over-loading total economic risk.
Exam focus: Expect a described client and a "recommend a tilt" question. The reasoning graders want: identify whether human capital is bond-like or equity-like, then tilt the FINANCIAL portfolio the opposite way to balance total economic risk.
LOS 3 & 4 — Asset-only, liability-relative, and goals-based approaches
LOS: compare the investment objectives of asset-only, liability-relative, and goals-based asset allocation approaches
LOS: contrast concepts of risk relevant to asset-only, liability-relative, and goals-based asset allocation approaches
What you must do:  Match each approach to its objective, its risk measure, and the investor it suits — the single most tested distinction in the reading.
Table 1 — The three approaches compared
Approach
Objective
Risk measure
Typical investor
Asset-only
Maximize risk-adjusted asset return (e.g., MVO / max Sharpe); no liabilities modeled
Volatility of returns; downside risk (VaR, drawdown)
Some endowments/foundations; individuals without defined liabilities
Liability-relative
Fund and hedge liabilities; manage the surplus (assets − liabilities)
Shortfall vs liabilities; funded-ratio volatility; underfunding probability
DB pensions, insurers, banks
Goals-based
Achieve multiple goals, each with its own horizon and required success probability
Probability of failing to meet a goal within its horizon
Individuals / private wealth
Goals-based mechanics: build sub-portfolios, each tied to a specific goal with its own time horizon and minimum required probability of success. Higher-priority, must-meet goals get lower-risk sub-portfolios; aspirational goals can carry more risk.
Liability-relative mechanics: assets are typically split into a hedging portfolio (matched to the liabilities to control funded-ratio risk) and a return-seeking portfolio (to grow the surplus). The analytical tool is surplus optimization — mean-variance optimization run on the surplus (assets minus liabilities) rather than on assets alone. Because liabilities behave like a short long-duration bond position, the hedging assets are usually long-duration fixed income.
Goals-based mechanics in more detail: each goal is assigned a required probability of success and a time horizon, and capital is bucketed accordingly — near-term, must-meet goals in low-risk assets, distant or aspirational goals in higher-risk assets. The overall portfolio is simply the sum of these sub-portfolios. It can look sub-optimal from a pure mean-variance view but is more behaviorally robust, because clients relate to goals rather than to an aggregate Sharpe ratio.
Drill: A DB pension wants to control the volatility of its funded ratio. Which approach, and which portfolio split? (Answer: liability-relative — a hedging portfolio matched to the liabilities plus a return-seeking portfolio for the surplus.)
Exam focus: The comparison table IS the exam answer. Watch the phrasing: "probability of not meeting a goal" → goals-based; "funded ratio / surplus" → liability-relative; "maximize Sharpe with no liabilities" → asset-only.
LOS 5 — Asset classes as systematic-risk exposures
LOS: explain how asset classes are used to represent exposures to systematic risk and discuss criteria for asset class specification
What you must do:  State the criteria for a well-specified asset class and use them to judge a proposed classification.
Asset classes are groupings that represent exposures to distinct sources of systematic risk. A useful specification satisfies these criteria:
	•	Homogeneous — assets within a class are similar to one another.
	•	Mutually exclusive — classes do not overlap (overlap double-counts risk and blurs the allocation).
	•	Diversifying — classes are not highly correlated (a pairwise correlation above roughly 0.95 means the class adds no diversification).
	•	As a group, cover a preponderance of world investable wealth (a complete opportunity set).
	•	Have capacity — each class can absorb a meaningful allocation without distorting prices or liquidity.
How many classes? More asset classes give a finer opportunity set but multiply the return, volatility, and correlation inputs the optimizer needs — and estimation error compounds quickly. Too few classes, and you miss diversifying exposures. The practical answer is a manageable set of well-specified, distinct classes rather than the largest possible list.
Watch out: Splitting into "domestic equities" AND "domestic large-cap equities" violates mutual exclusivity (the second is inside the first). Also weigh liquidity and transaction costs when specifying classes.
Exam focus: You will be given a proposed classification and asked which criterion it breaks. Overlap → mutual exclusivity; two near-identical classes → diversifying; a niche sliver that can't hold real money → capacity.
LOS 6 — Risk factors and their relation to asset classes
LOS: explain the use of risk factors in asset allocation and their relation to traditional asset class–based approaches
What you must do:  Explain what a factor approach is, how it relates to asset classes, and its trade-offs.
Risk factors are the underlying, non-diversifiable drivers of return — inflation, real interest rates, credit spread, the equity market, and style factors like size, value, momentum, liquidity, and volatility. A factor approach allocates to these drivers directly rather than to asset-class labels.
How they relate: asset classes BUNDLE several factor exposures (a corporate bond is real rate + inflation + credit), and different asset classes can share the same factors — creating hidden correlation and concentration. Factors are more granular and typically less correlated with each other, so a factor lens can reveal risk that an asset-class lens hides.
Construction and trade-offs: factors are often isolated with long-short portfolios (value = long cheap, short expensive), which can require leverage and shorting. Pros: exposes the true risk drivers and reduces hidden overlaps. Cons: less intuitive, and factors are not directly investable — implementation adds complexity, cost, leverage, and shorting risk. Neither approach is inherently superior; they are two lenses on the same risks and can complement each other.
Illustration — hidden factor overlap
A portfolio holds high-yield bonds, public equities, and private equity, and looks well-diversified across three "asset classes." But all three load heavily on the same equity/credit-risk factor, so in a downturn they fall together — the diversification is largely illusory. A factor lens exposes this shared exposure that the asset-class labels conceal.
Exam focus: Qualitative. Be ready to explain why two asset classes can look diversified yet share a common factor (hidden concentration), and to name a drawback of factor investing (implementation via long-short/derivatives).
LOS 7 — Recommending and justifying an asset allocation
LOS: recommend and justify an asset allocation based on an investor’s objectives and constraints
What you must do:  Turn stated objectives and constraints into a recommended allocation and defend each element. This is an essay LOS.
Framework: objectives = return requirement and risk tolerance (both ability AND willingness); constraints = time horizon, liquidity, taxes, legal/regulatory, and unique circumstances. First choose the approach that fits the investor (asset-only, liability-relative, or goals-based), then set the allocation consistent with it.
Worked example — a model constructed-response answer
Prompt: A mid-career couple has stable salaries, a 25-year horizon, ample liquidity, and moderate risk tolerance. Recommend an approach and a broad allocation, with justification.
Approach — goals-based. As individuals with distinct objectives (retirement, a home, education), sub-portfolios by goal fit better than a single asset-only mandate.
Allocation — equity-tilted overall. The long horizon and bond-like (stable) human capital support meaningful equity; ample liquidity means little cash drag; moderate risk tolerance argues for a diversified multi-asset mix rather than a concentrated one.
Why it scores: each choice is tied explicitly to a stated objective or constraint, and the approach is justified before the numbers.
Exam focus: Constructed-response: you will critique or recommend an allocation against the stated objectives and constraints. Always link each element of the allocation back to a specific objective or constraint — an unjustified number earns nothing.
LOS 8 — The global market portfolio as a baseline
LOS: describe the use of the global market portfolio as a baseline portfolio in asset allocation
What you must do:  Explain why the global market portfolio is a sound neutral starting point and why investors depart from it.
The global market portfolio (GMP) is the market-value-weighted portfolio of all investable assets worldwide. As a baseline it is:
	•	Theoretically efficient — the tangency (maximum-Sharpe) portfolio under CAPM assumptions.
	•	Neutral and non-judgmental — it reflects the aggregate positioning of all investors (the "wisdom of the crowd") and takes no active view.
	•	Low governance burden — holding it requires no forecasting skill, so it minimizes the demands on the governance structure.
Investors then deviate from the GMP for their own circumstances — liabilities, horizon, taxes, liquidity needs, and any genuine views. A well-founded departure should be deliberate and justified, not accidental (e.g., unintentional home bias).
Exam focus: Describe the GMP's three virtues (efficient, neutral, minimal governance) and give a legitimate reason to depart from it (an investor-specific liability, tax, or horizon consideration).
LOS 9 — Strategic implementation: passive/active and vehicles
LOS: discuss strategic implementation choices in asset allocation, including passive/active choices and vehicles for implementing passive and active mandates
What you must do:  Place a mandate on the passive–active spectrum and match an implementation vehicle to an investor's needs.
Passive–active spectrum. Mandates range from fully passive (index) through factor/smart-beta to fully active. The choice turns on belief in manager skill NET of fees, cost sensitivity, and governance capacity to select and monitor active managers. A formal active-risk (tracking-error) budget caps how much active risk the program takes.
Implementation vehicles
	•	Pooled / mutual funds — simple, diversified access; less customization; shared tax consequences.
	•	Separately managed accounts (SMAs) — full customization and tax management (loss harvesting); higher minimums and cost.
	•	ETFs — low-cost, liquid, tax-efficient broad exposure; intraday trading.
	•	Derivatives (futures/swaps) — efficient, capital-light exposure and overlays for quick or temporary positioning.
Exam focus: Match vehicle to need: tax-sensitive investor wanting customization → SMA; fast, cheap, broad exposure → ETF or futures; temporary tactical exposure or an overlay → derivatives.
LOS 10 — Strategic considerations in rebalancing
LOS: discuss strategic considerations in rebalancing asset allocations
What you must do:  Compare the two rebalancing disciplines and explain what makes an optimal corridor wider or narrower.
Rebalancing returns the portfolio toward its strategic weights, preserving the intended risk profile. It is inherently CONTRARIAN — it sells what has risen and buys what has fallen.
	•	Calendar rebalancing — rebalance on a fixed schedule (e.g., quarterly). Simple, but ignores market moves between dates.
	•	Percentage-range (corridor) rebalancing — rebalance when a class breaches a band around its target. Ties action to actual drift but requires ongoing monitoring.
Table 2 — What sets the optimal corridor width
Factor
Effect on width
Rationale
Higher transaction costs
Wider
Rebalance less often to avoid paying costs
Higher risk tolerance
Wider
More drift from target is acceptable
Higher correlation with rest of portfolio
Wider
The class's drift matters less — it moves with everything else
Higher asset-class volatility
Ambiguous
Raises the cost of drifting (narrower) but also causes more frequent, costly trades (wider)
Rebalancing and returns: in mean-reverting markets, disciplined rebalancing earns a "rebalancing return" (buy low, sell high); in strongly trending markets it detracts. Taxable investors limit or defer rebalancing to manage gains, and can rebalance cheaply using new cash flows or derivatives.
Why it matters: left alone, a portfolio drifts toward whatever asset has risen most — usually the riskiest — so its risk profile creeps upward over time. Disciplined rebalancing is what keeps realized risk aligned with the strategic target; that risk control is its primary purpose, and any rebalancing return is a secondary benefit.
Exam focus: Two reliable questions: "how does factor X affect corridor width" (memorize the table — the first three are clean, volatility is ambiguous) and "why is rebalancing contrarian / when does it hurt" (it sells winners and buys losers, so it detracts in trending markets).
Quick reference — key relationships
The formulas and rules worth memorizing
Economic net worth = (financial assets + human capital + PV pensions)
                   − (financial debts + PV future consumption)
Surplus       = assets − PV(liabilities)
Funded ratio  = assets / PV(liabilities)
Asset-class 'diversifying' test: pairwise correlation < ~0.95
Corridor width: costs^ -> wider; risk tolerance^ -> wider;
                correlation^ -> wider; volatility -> ambiguous
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. Which approach models no explicit liabilities and maximizes risk-adjusted asset return?
   -> Asset-only.
2. A client has a stable, secure salary (bond-like human capital). More equity or more bonds in the financial portfolio?
   -> More equity — the financial portfolio offsets the bond-like human capital.
3. How is risk defined in goals-based investing?
   -> The probability of failing to meet a goal within its time horizon.
4. Proposed classes: "domestic equities" and "domestic large-cap equities." Which criterion is violated?
   -> Mutual exclusivity (the second sits inside the first).
5. Give two reasons the global market portfolio is a good neutral baseline.
   -> It is theoretically efficient and requires minimal governance/forecasting (reflects aggregate investor views).
6. A taxable investor wants a customized, tax-managed portfolio. Which vehicle?
   -> A separately managed account (SMA).
7. Higher transaction costs imply a wider or narrower rebalancing corridor?
   -> Wider.
8. Why is rebalancing contrarian, and when does it hurt returns?
   -> It sells assets that rose and buys those that fell; it detracts in strongly trending markets.

readings.title: Principles of Asset Allocation
readings.orientation: Orientation. This is the mechanical heart of asset allocation — how the allocation actually gets built. The core is mean–variance optimization (MVO): know how it works, why its outputs misbehave, and the four fixes (reverse optimization, Black–Litterman, resampling, and adding constraints). Then come the tools around it — Monte Carlo for multi-period robustness, risk budgeting (with a real MCTR/ACTR calculation), the three liability-relative approaches, goals-based construction, and the heuristic shortcuts. Two calculations to have cold: the utility score and the risk-contribution decomposition.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Asset Allocation
Reading: Principles of Asset Allocation
Orientation. This is the mechanical heart of asset allocation — how the allocation actually gets built. The core is mean–variance optimization (MVO): know how it works, why its outputs misbehave, and the four fixes (reverse optimization, Black–Litterman, resampling, and adding constraints). Then come the tools around it — Monte Carlo for multi-period robustness, risk budgeting (with a real MCTR/ACTR calculation), the three liability-relative approaches, goals-based construction, and the heuristic shortcuts. Two calculations to have cold: the utility score and the risk-contribution decomposition.

LOS 1 & 2 — Mean–variance optimization
LOS: describe and evaluate the use of mean–variance optimization in asset allocation
LOS: recommend and justify an asset allocation using mean–variance optimization
What you must do:  Explain how MVO produces the efficient frontier, pick the utility-maximizing portfolio, and critique MVO's weaknesses with their remedies.
How it works. MVO takes expected returns, variances, and covariances and traces the efficient frontier — the set of portfolios with the highest expected return for each level of risk. The investor's optimal portfolio maximizes a mean–variance utility score:
U = E(R) − 0.005 × λ × σ²      (returns and σ in %; λ = risk aversion)
A higher risk-aversion coefficient λ penalizes variance more heavily, pushing the optimum toward lower-risk portfolios. With a risk-free asset, the optimum sits where the capital allocation line is tangent to the frontier (the maximum-Sharpe portfolio). Under long-only and other constraints, the frontier is piecewise-linear between corner portfolios.
Worked example — choosing between two portfolios by utility
Portfolio X: E(R) = 8%, σ = 12%. Portfolio Y: E(R) = 10%, σ = 18%. Investor risk aversion λ = 4.
U_X = 8  − 0.005 × 4 × 12^2 = 8  − 2.88 = 5.12
U_Y = 10 − 0.005 × 4 × 18^2 = 10 − 6.48 = 3.52
X wins: its lower risk more than offsets Y's 2% higher return for this risk-averse investor. A less risk-averse investor (small λ) could prefer Y.
Corner portfolios and interpolation
When the frontier is constrained (e.g., long-only), it is a series of straight segments joining corner portfolios — points where an asset's weight just hits zero or a constraint binds. Any efficient portfolio between two adjacent corner portfolios is a linear blend of them, so you can hit a target expected return by interpolating.
Worked example — hitting a target return between corner portfolios
Corner portfolio 1: E(R) = 9.0%. Adjacent (lower-risk) corner portfolio 2: E(R) = 7.0%. The investor requires 8.0%. Find the blend.
w1 × 9.0 + (1 − w1) × 7.0 = 8.0
2.0 × w1 = 1.0   ->   w1 = 0.50
Hold 50% corner portfolio 1 and 50% corner portfolio 2; each asset-class weight is the same 50/50 blend of the two corner portfolios' weights. (If a risk-free asset is available, instead combine the max-Sharpe tangency portfolio with cash.)
Evaluating MVO — the criticisms and their fixes
Table 1 — MVO's weaknesses and the standard remedies
Weakness
Why it hurts
Remedy
Input sensitivity ('error maximization')
Tiny changes in expected returns swing the weights wildly
Reverse optimization; Black–Litterman; resampling
Concentrated allocations
Weights pile into a few asset classes
Constraints; Black–Litterman; resampling
Single-period, ignores taxes/trading
Real problems are multi-period and path-dependent
Monte Carlo simulation
Assumes normal returns / variance-only risk
Ignores skewness and fat tails
Mean–CVaR or higher-moment optimization
Asset-only (ignores liabilities)
Wrong objective for a pension or insurer
Surplus / liability-relative optimization
Reverse optimization and Black–Litterman. Reverse optimization starts from market-cap weights and backs out the expected returns the market implies — a neutral, well-diversified starting point. Black–Litterman then blends those implied returns with the investor's own views, tilting the portfolio only where a view exists. Both dramatically stabilize the output versus raw MVO.
Resampling (Michaud). Simulate many sets of inputs, run MVO on each, and average the resulting frontiers. The resampled frontier gives more diversified, more stable weights that are less sensitive to any single input estimate.
Exam focus: Guaranteed content. Be ready to (1) compute and compare utility scores, and (2) name a fix for a described MVO problem — "the weights are extreme and unstable" → reverse optimization / Black–Litterman / resampling; "the client withdraws income over 30 years" → Monte Carlo.
LOS 3 — Allocation and the economic balance sheet
LOS: interpret and evaluate an asset allocation in relation to an investor’s economic balance sheet
What you must do:  Judge whether a financial-portfolio allocation makes sense once human capital and other extended assets are included.
Recall the economic balance sheet adds human capital and the present value of pensions to the asset side, and future consumption to the liability side. An allocation that looks fine in isolation can be wrong on the full balance sheet: an investor with bond-like human capital who also holds a bond-heavy financial portfolio is over-exposed to bond-like risk in total, and vice versa. Evaluate the FINANCIAL portfolio as the balancing item that shapes total economic risk.
Exam focus: You'll be given an allocation and a description of the client's human capital and asked to critique the fit. The reasoning: bond-like human capital → financial portfolio can hold more equity; equity-like human capital → tilt financial toward bonds.
LOS 4 — Allocation based on the global market portfolio
LOS: recommend and justify an asset allocation based on the global market portfolio
What you must do:  Use the global market portfolio as the neutral starting weights and justify deliberate tilts away from it.
The global market portfolio (market-cap weights of all investable assets) is the reverse-optimization baseline: holding it requires no forecasting skill and is theoretically efficient. From there, recommend tilts justified by the investor's liabilities, horizon, taxes, liquidity needs, or genuine active views — and make sure any deviation is intentional, not an accidental home bias or legacy position.
Exam focus: Often paired with reverse optimization: the market weights imply the equilibrium returns, and your recommended tilts should be defended by investor-specific circumstances or explicit views.
LOS 5 — Monte Carlo simulation and scenario analysis
LOS: discuss the use of Monte Carlo simulation and scenario analysis to evaluate the robustness of an asset allocation
What you must do:  Explain what each technique adds beyond single-period MVO and when to reach for it.
Monte Carlo simulation generates thousands of possible return paths to reveal the DISTRIBUTION of outcomes — not just a single expected value. It is essential where the problem is multi-period and path-dependent: retirement withdrawals and sequence-of-returns risk, the interaction of taxes and rebalancing, and non-normal return distributions. It answers questions MVO cannot, such as the probability of not running out of money over 30 years.
Scenario analysis evaluates the portfolio under a small number of specific, discrete scenarios — a recession, an inflation shock, a rates spike — often built from historical stress episodes or factor shocks. It stress-tests resilience to particular events rather than mapping the full distribution.
Exam focus: Distinguish the two: Monte Carlo = full outcome distribution over many periods (robustness of a plan); scenario analysis = impact of specific stress events. A retiree drawing income is the classic Monte Carlo prompt.
LOS 6 — Liquidity considerations
LOS: discuss asset class liquidity considerations in asset allocation
What you must do:  Match an investor's capacity to bear illiquidity to the role of illiquid asset classes, and handle their measurement problems.
Illiquid asset classes — real estate, private equity, infrastructure — can offer an illiquidity premium, but only investors with a long horizon and modest near-term liquidity needs (large endowments, sovereign funds) can safely harvest it. Investors with near-term liabilities cannot. Three complications: illiquid assets are hard to value (appraisal/smoothed data understates risk and correlation), hard to rebalance, and have no directly investable index.
Practical handling: assess the investor's true liquidity needs first; then either exclude illiquid classes from the MVO and size them separately, or include them using de-smoothed (unlagged) risk estimates so they aren't made to look artificially safe.
Watch out: Never feed raw appraisal-based volatilities and correlations into an optimizer — the smoothing makes private assets look low-risk and low-correlation, so the optimizer over-allocates to them. De-smooth first.
Exam focus: Qualitative: given an investor's horizon and liquidity needs, judge how much illiquid exposure is appropriate, and cite the valuation/rebalancing/indexing problems of illiquid classes.
LOS 7 — Absolute and relative risk budgets
LOS: explain absolute and relative risk budgets and their use in determining and implementing an asset allocation
What you must do:  Define the risk-budgeting terms, compute marginal and absolute risk contributions, and state the optimality condition.
Definitions. A risk budget allocates total risk across positions. An ABSOLUTE risk budget allocates total portfolio volatility across asset classes; a RELATIVE risk budget allocates active risk (tracking error) across active positions relative to a benchmark.
MCTR_i  = β(i,P) × σ_P            (marginal contribution to total risk)
ACTR_i  = w_i × MCTR_i             (absolute contribution to total risk)
Σ ACTR_i = σ_P                     (contributions sum to portfolio risk)
% contribution_i = ACTR_i / σ_P
Worked example — risk-contribution decomposition
Two-asset portfolio: A at 60%, B at 40%; portfolio volatility σ_P = 12%. Betas to the portfolio: β_A = 0.8, β_B = 1.3. (Check: 0.6×0.8 + 0.4×1.3 = 1.0.)
MCTR_A = 0.8 × 12% = 9.6% ;  MCTR_B = 1.3 × 12% = 15.6%
ACTR_A = 0.6 × 9.6%  = 5.76% ; ACTR_B = 0.4 × 15.6% = 6.24%
Sum = 5.76 + 6.24 = 12.0% = σ_P  ✓
%CTR: A = 5.76/12 = 48% ;  B = 6.24/12 = 52%
So B, though only 40% of capital, contributes 52% of the risk — the point of the exercise is that capital weight ≠ risk weight.
Optimality condition. A risk budget is optimal when the ratio of expected excess return to MCTR is equal across all positions — that common ratio is the portfolio's Sharpe ratio. If one position's return-per-unit-of-risk-contribution is higher than another's, shifting risk toward it improves the portfolio.
The relative (active) case. The same logic applies to active return and active risk: a program is efficient when the ratio of expected active return to marginal contribution to TRACKING ERROR is equal across all active positions, and that common ratio is the information ratio. This is how a plan sponsor allocates a tracking-error budget across active managers.
Exam focus: Expect a full MCTR/ACTR calculation. Remember MCTR uses the beta to the PORTFOLIO (not to the market), ACTR sums to σ_P, and the optimum equalizes excess-return-to-MCTR across positions.
LOS 8 — Incorporating client risk preferences
LOS: describe how client needs and preferences regarding investment risks can be incorporated into asset allocation
What you must do:  Translate a client's stated needs and preferences — beyond ability and willingness — into allocation choices.
Beyond the standard ability/willingness assessment, clients bring specific risk preferences: a demand for downside protection, a maximum acceptable loss, liquidity requirements, ESG or values-based constraints, and — in goals-based settings — different risk tolerances for different goals (mental accounting). These are incorporated as constraints on the optimization, through goal-specific sub-portfolios, or by choosing a risk measure the client actually cares about (e.g., shortfall or drawdown rather than variance).
Exam focus: Qualitative. Recognize that a stated preference (downside protection, an ESG screen, a spending floor) becomes a constraint or a sub-portfolio, and that risk tolerance can legitimately differ across a single client's goals.
LOS 9 — Investment factors in constructing an allocation
