LOS: describe the use of investment factors in constructing and analyzing an asset allocation
What you must do:  Explain how a factor-based allocation is built and analyzed, and how it complements an asset-class view.
A factor-based allocation targets exposures to the underlying return drivers — equity, term, credit, inflation, size, value, momentum, liquidity — rather than asset-class labels. Construction can start from a factor risk budget (how much of total risk each factor should contribute) and then map factors to investable instruments, often via long-short portfolios. For analysis, decomposing an existing asset-class portfolio into its factor exposures reveals hidden concentrations — for example, that several 'diversified' classes all load on the same equity/credit factor.
Exam focus: Tie to the prior reading: factors are more granular and less correlated than asset classes, they reveal hidden overlaps, but they are implemented via long-short/derivatives (added complexity, leverage, shorting). Neither lens is strictly superior.
LOS 10 — Characteristics of liabilities relevant to allocation
LOS: describe and evaluate characteristics of liabilities that are relevant to asset allocation
What you must do:  List the liability characteristics that shape the asset allocation of a liability-driven investor.
Liability characteristics that matter
	•	Fixed vs contingent cash flows — are the payments certain, or do they depend on events (e.g., insurance claims)?
	•	Legal vs quasi-liabilities — contractually binding, or expected-but-not-legally-required?
	•	Duration and convexity of the liability cash flows — sets the interest-rate exposure to hedge.
	•	Size of the liabilities relative to the sponsor — a large plan relative to the firm constrains risk-taking.
	•	Timing / longevity uncertainty — how predictable is the payment schedule?
	•	Regulatory and accounting requirements, and inflation sensitivity of the liabilities.
Exam focus: Given a described liability stream, identify the features that drive the hedge — most often duration (interest-rate exposure) and whether the cash flows are fixed or inflation-linked.
LOS 11 & 12 — Liability-relative approaches
LOS: discuss approaches to liability-relative asset allocation
LOS: recommend and justify a liability-relative asset allocation
What you must do:  Compare the three approaches and pick the right one for a described institution.
Table 2 — The three liability-relative approaches
Approach
What it does
Best suited to
Surplus optimization
MVO run on the surplus (assets − PV liabilities); efficient frontier in surplus-return / surplus-risk space
Cases where liabilities can be approximated and their correlations with assets estimated
Hedging + return-seeking
Split assets into a liability-hedging portfolio and a return-seeking portfolio; fund the hedge, then seek return on the surplus
When a clean liability-matching portfolio exists and funded status is the priority
Integrated asset–liability
Jointly and dynamically model assets and liabilities, usually multi-period via Monte Carlo
Complex, interacting balance sheets — banks and insurers
Choosing: the hedging/return-seeking split is the intuitive default when funded status matters and a matching portfolio is available; surplus optimization is the analytical middle ground; the integrated approach is the most comprehensive but also the most data- and model-intensive, reserved for balance sheets where assets and liabilities interact strongly.
Worked example — funded ratio and the hedge
A pension has assets of 900 and PV of liabilities of 1,000.
Funded ratio = 900 / 1,000 = 0.90   (90% funded; deficit of 100)
Under a hedging + return-seeking split, the plan first allocates enough long-duration fixed income to hedge the liabilities' interest-rate exposure, then invests the remainder in return-seeking assets to close the deficit and grow the surplus. A better-funded plan can hedge more (locking in the surplus); an underfunded plan must accept some surplus risk to catch up.
Exam focus: Match approach to institution: a well-funded pension prioritizing funded status → hedging/return-seeking; a bank or insurer with interacting assets and liabilities → integrated asset–liability. Justify with the liability characteristics from LOS 10.
LOS 13 — Goals-based asset allocation
LOS: recommend and justify an asset allocation using a goals-based approach
What you must do:  Build sub-portfolios per goal and aggregate them into an overall allocation.
Goals-based construction assigns each goal a required return, a time horizon, and a minimum acceptable probability of success, then funds it from a sub-portfolio (module) whose risk level meets that requirement. Must-meet, near-term goals draw on low-risk modules; distant or aspirational goals can use higher-risk modules. The overall portfolio is simply the sum of the goal sub-portfolios. It can look inefficient from a single-Sharpe standpoint but is more behaviorally durable because clients see each goal funded appropriately.
Selecting the module: for each goal you know the required return (to fund it) and the maximum acceptable probability of failure. You choose the lowest-risk module whose return distribution meets that required return at the required confidence level. Putting a must-meet goal in a high-risk module would raise the shortfall probability beyond what the client will accept.
Drill: A client's essential retirement income is a must-meet goal 5 years out; a legacy gift is aspirational and 30 years out. How do the modules differ? (Answer: the near-term must-meet goal uses a low-risk module with a high required success probability; the distant aspirational goal can use a higher-risk, equity-tilted module.)
Exam focus: Constructed-response: assign goals to modules by required return, horizon, and success probability, then justify. Higher priority and shorter horizon → lower risk; lower priority and longer horizon → more risk.
LOS 14 — Heuristic and other approaches
LOS: describe and evaluate heuristic and other approaches to asset allocation
What you must do:  Recognize the common rule-of-thumb approaches and weigh their pros and cons versus formal optimization.
Table 3 — Heuristic and alternative approaches
Approach
Rule
Note
Age-based (120 − age)
Equity % ≈ 120 (or 100) − age
De-risks with age; ignores individual circumstances
1/N (equal weight)
Equal weight across N assets
Simple and robust to estimation error; ignores return/risk differences
Endowment (Yale) model
Heavy allocation to alternatives/illiquids
Needs scale, expertise, and a long horizon
Risk parity
Equalize each asset's risk contribution
Ignores expected returns; often uses leverage
60/40
60% equities / 40% bonds
Traditional balanced benchmark
Risk parity in focus. Risk parity sets weights so that each asset (or factor) contributes the same amount to total portfolio risk — using the ACTR machinery from LOS 7. Because low-risk assets get large weights, it typically applies leverage to reach a target return, and it deliberately ignores expected-return forecasts, relying only on risk.
Exam focus: Know each rule and its main limitation. The two most tested: risk parity (equal risk contribution, ignores returns, uses leverage) and 1/N (robust to estimation error precisely because it uses no estimates).
LOS 15 — Factors affecting rebalancing policy
LOS: discuss factors affecting rebalancing policy
What you must do:  State what widens or narrows rebalancing bands and how taxes and beliefs feed in.
Rebalancing can be calendar-based, percentage-range (corridor) based, or a combination. The factors that set an optimal corridor: higher transaction costs → WIDER bands (trade less); higher risk tolerance → WIDER; higher correlation of the class with the rest of the portfolio → WIDER (its drift matters less); higher asset-class volatility → ambiguous (raises the cost of drift but also the cost of frequent trades).
Two more policy inputs: taxable investors rebalance less often and use cash flows or derivatives to limit realized gains; and beliefs about market dynamics matter — disciplined rebalancing (contrarian) adds a rebalancing return in mean-reverting markets but detracts when markets trend, so a manager who expects momentum may rebalance less aggressively.
Exam focus: The corridor-width factors are the reliable ask (first three are clean; volatility is ambiguous). Add the tax point (taxable → rebalance less) and the mean-reversion-vs-trend logic.
Quick reference — key formulas
The formulas worth memorizing
Utility:  U = E(R) − 0.005 × λ × σ²      (returns, σ in %)
Risk contributions:
  MCTR_i = β(i,P) × σ_P
  ACTR_i = w_i × MCTR_i      ;   Σ ACTR_i = σ_P
  % contribution_i = ACTR_i / σ_P
Optimal risk budget: excess return_i / MCTR_i equal across i
                     (= portfolio Sharpe ratio)
Surplus = assets − PV(liabilities)
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. In U = E(R) − 0.005λσ², what does a higher λ imply?
   -> Greater risk aversion — variance is penalized more, favoring lower-risk portfolios.
2. What is MVO's most-cited flaw, and name two remedies.
   -> Extreme input sensitivity ('error maximization'); reverse optimization / Black–Litterman and resampling.
3. Why use Monte Carlo rather than MVO for a retiree drawing income?
   -> It handles multi-period paths, sequence-of-returns risk, taxes, and non-normal outcomes; MVO is single-period.
4. Write the MCTR and ACTR formulas.
   -> MCTR_i = β(i,P) × σ_P; ACTR_i = w_i × MCTR_i; the ACTRs sum to σ_P.
5. At the optimal risk budget, what is equalized across positions?
   -> The ratio of excess return to MCTR (equal to the portfolio's Sharpe ratio).
6. Which liability-relative approach splits into a hedging and a return-seeking portfolio?
   -> The hedging + return-seeking (two-portfolio) approach.
7. Risk parity allocates on what basis, and what does it ignore?
   -> Equal risk contribution from each asset; it ignores expected returns (and often uses leverage).
8. Why must appraisal-based risk estimates be de-smoothed before optimization?
   -> Smoothing understates volatility and correlation, so the optimizer over-allocates to illiquid assets.

readings.title: Asset Allocation with Real-World Constraints
readings.orientation: Orientation. Only five LOS, but the tax LOS is the one that carries real math — after-tax return, after-tax risk, and the widened rebalancing band. The rest is applied judgment: how asset size, liquidity, horizon, and regulation constrain the allocation; when to revise the strategic allocation versus make a temporary tactical shift; and the behavioral biases that derail allocation, with their remedies. Learn the tax formulas cold and know the bias-remedy pairs.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Asset Allocation
Reading: Asset Allocation with Real-World Constraints
Orientation. Only five LOS, but the tax LOS is the one that carries real math — after-tax return, after-tax risk, and the widened rebalancing band. The rest is applied judgment: how asset size, liquidity, horizon, and regulation constrain the allocation; when to revise the strategic allocation versus make a temporary tactical shift; and the behavioral biases that derail allocation, with their remedies. Learn the tax formulas cold and know the bias-remedy pairs.

LOS 1 — Asset size, liquidity, horizon, and regulatory constraints
LOS: discuss asset size, liquidity needs, time horizon, and regulatory or other considerations as constraints on asset allocation
What you must do:  Explain how each constraint limits the feasible allocation, especially the two-sided effect of asset size.
Asset size
Size cuts both ways. A very large fund faces capacity limits and market impact — it can't move the needle with small or niche asset classes and is pushed toward large-cap, liquid holdings — though it CAN access scale-only strategies (large private equity, direct real estate). A very small fund is blocked from asset classes with high minimums (private equity, hedge funds, direct real estate), can't diversify across enough managers, and may lack the resources for alternatives due diligence.
Table 1 — Asset size as a constraint
Size
Core challenge
Consequence for allocation
Very large
Capacity limits and market impact
Pushed toward large-cap/liquid; niche classes can't move the needle; but can access scale-only alternatives
Very small
High minimums, thin resources
Blocked from PE / hedge funds / direct real estate; can't diversify across managers; limited due-diligence capacity
Liquidity, time horizon, and regulation
	•	Liquidity — the investor's cash needs (spending, redemptions, capital calls) cap the allocation to illiquid assets; weigh both the investor's needs AND the asset classes' liquidity, including under stress. Low-liquidity-need investors (large endowments, sovereign funds) can hold more illiquids.
	•	Time horizon — a longer horizon supports more equity, illiquidity, and risk; horizon shortens over the life cycle as human capital declines, and many investors have multi-stage horizons (e.g., pre- and post-retirement).
	•	Regulatory and other — pensions face funding rules; insurers face risk-based capital and asset restrictions and must match liabilities; banks face regulatory capital and liquidity coverage; foundations face spending rules (e.g., the US 5% payout); sovereign funds face political and legal constraints.
Liquidity budgeting: beyond a single liquidity figure, assess time-to-liquidate for each holding and model liquidity under stress (correlations and redemption demands spike together). Committed but undrawn capital calls to private funds are a future liquidity claim that must be reserved for.
Table 1b — Dominant constraints by institution type
Institution
Dominant constraints
DB pension
Funding rules; liability matching; the sponsor's covenant/ability to contribute
Insurer
Risk-based capital; asset-eligibility rules; duration matching of liabilities
Bank
Regulatory capital; liquidity coverage; asset–liability management
Endowment / foundation
Spending rule (often ~5%); long horizon; UPMIFA prudence standard
Sovereign wealth fund
Political and legal constraints; transparency; sometimes a very long horizon
Exam focus: The favorite point is that asset size constrains BOTH ways — small funds are locked out of alternatives, large funds are constrained by capacity and market impact. For institutions, tie the constraint to the investor type (insurer → capital rules + liability matching; foundation → spending rule).
LOS 2 — Tax considerations in allocation and rebalancing
LOS: discuss tax considerations in asset allocation and rebalancing
What you must do:  Compute after-tax return and risk, widen the rebalancing band for taxes, and apply asset location.
Taxes reduce both return and risk, because the government effectively shares in gains and losses. The core relationships:
After-tax return:   r_at = r_pt × (1 − t)
Decomposed:         r_at = p_i·r·(1 − t_i) + p_cg·r·(1 − t_cg)
After-tax risk:     σ_at = σ_pt × (1 − t)
After-tax range:    R_at = R_pt / (1 − t)     (WIDER when taxed)
Worked example — after-tax return (income vs gains)
A portfolio's 7% pre-tax return is 30% income (taxed at 35%) and 70% price appreciation (taxed at 20%).
r_at = 0.30 × 7 × (1 − 0.35) + 0.70 × 7 × (1 − 0.20)
     = 0.30 × 7 × 0.65   +   0.70 × 7 × 0.80
     = 1.365            +   3.92
     = 5.285%   ≈  5.29%
Worked example — after-tax risk and the rebalancing band
Pre-tax volatility σ_pt = 15%; blended tax rate t = 25%; pre-tax rebalancing corridor ±10%.
σ_at = 15% × (1 − 0.25)   = 11.25%   (lower after-tax risk)
R_at = 10% / (1 − 0.25)   = 13.33%   (wider band)
Because after-tax volatility is lower, the same economic risk tolerance corresponds to a WIDER rebalancing corridor for the taxable investor.
Asset location and tax management
Asset location (distinct from asset allocation) places tax-inefficient assets — taxable bonds, high-turnover strategies, REITs — in tax-deferred or tax-exempt accounts, and tax-efficient assets — low-turnover equities held for long-term gains — in taxable accounts. Add tax-loss harvesting, holding for long-term rates, low turnover, and municipal bonds for high-bracket investors. Taxable investors also rebalance LESS often and use cash flows to limit realized gains.
Tax-efficient techniques worth naming: harvest losses to offset realized gains; hold winners for long-term rates and let unrealized gains compound (the deferral itself is valuable, since untaxed money keeps earning); donate appreciated securities or pass them on at death, where a basis step-up can erase the embedded gain; and rebalance using new contributions or by directing income distributions rather than by selling appreciated positions.
Exam focus: High-probability calculations: after-tax return (both the blended and the income/gains-decomposed forms), after-tax volatility, and the widened corridor R/(1−t). Qualitatively, know asset location and that taxable investors rebalance less frequently.
LOS 3 — Revising the strategic asset allocation
LOS: recommend and justify revisions to an asset allocation given change(s) in investment objectives and/or constraints
What you must do:  Identify what triggers a strategic revision and recommend the change, distinguishing it from a tactical shift.
Revisit the strategic asset allocation (SAA) when the inputs that produced it change materially:
	•	Objectives — a changed return requirement or new goals (e.g., a new spending need).
	•	Constraints — a shortening horizon, rising liquidity needs, a change in tax status, new regulation, or a large change in wealth.
	•	Beliefs — a significant, durable change in capital market expectations.
A strategic revision permanently changes the SAA and its target weights; it is different from a tactical shift, which is a temporary deviation to exploit a short-term opportunity (LOS 4). Life events — retirement, an inheritance, a liquidity event, a new regulation — are the classic revision triggers.
Drill: A client retires and shifts from saving to drawing income. What SAA revision follows? (Answer: shorten effective horizon and de-risk — more fixed income and liquidity to fund withdrawals and manage sequence-of-returns risk, since human capital is now largely spent.)
Exam focus: Constructed-response: given a described life or regulatory change, recommend a specific SAA revision and justify via the changed objective or constraint. Be explicit that this is a STRATEGIC change, not a tactical bet.
LOS 4 — Short-term (tactical) shifts in asset allocation
LOS: discuss the use of short-term shifts in asset allocation
What you must do:  Explain what tactical asset allocation is, its two forms, the signals it uses, and its costs.
Tactical asset allocation (TAA) is a deliberate short-term deviation from the SAA to exploit perceived near-term opportunities (mispricing, cyclical signals). It is always measured AGAINST the SAA: success means adding return or reducing risk relative to the strategic mix.
Table 2 — Two forms of TAA
Form
Basis
Typical signals
Discretionary TAA
Manager judgment and forecasts
Business cycle, monetary policy, relative valuation
Systematic TAA
Rules-based, repeatable signals
Value, momentum, carry, trend-following
Signals in practice: economic (cycle, policy), valuation (earnings yield vs bond yield, credit spreads), sentiment (VIX, put/call ratios, margin debt), and momentum/trend. 
Costs and evaluation: TAA incurs transaction costs and taxes and adds active risk, so it must overcome those hurdles to add value. Evaluate it by the improvement in Sharpe ratio versus the SAA, the information ratio, and the hit rate (percentage of correct calls).
A note on valuation signals and realism: a common relative-value signal is the gap between the equity earnings yield and the government bond yield (the "Fed model" idea) — a wide gap argues for equities. But most evidence shows TAA is hard to profit from consistently once transaction costs, taxes, and wrong calls are netted out, so it should be sized modestly against the SAA.
Exam focus: Distinguish TAA (temporary, exploits short-term opportunity) from a strategic revision (permanent, driven by changed objectives/constraints). Know the discretionary vs systematic split and that TAA is judged relative to the SAA net of costs.
LOS 5 — Behavioral biases in asset allocation
LOS: identify behavioral biases that arise in asset allocation and recommend methods to overcome them
What you must do:  Identify a bias from a described behavior and prescribe the remedy.
Table 3 — Biases in allocation and how to counter them
Bias
Effect on allocation
Remedy
Loss aversion
Holds losers, avoids risk it should take
Goals-based framing; a disciplined written policy
Illusion of control
Over-trading, concentrated bets
Anchor to the global market portfolio; diversify; evidence-based process
Mental accounting
Sub-optimal aggregate portfolio
Use goals-based framing constructively; also review total risk
Representativeness / recency
Performance chasing
Disciplined rebalancing; long-term capital market expectations
Availability / framing
Overweights vivid recent events
Base rates; present choices in multiple frames; formal review
The general antidotes run through every bias: a formal investment policy statement, a disciplined rebalancing policy, using the global market portfolio as the neutral anchor, and goals-based framing that aligns the portfolio with how clients actually think about money.
Three more that surface in allocation: overconfidence (over-trading, under-diversification, and excessive active risk — countered by an honest track-record review and a systematic process); home bias (overweighting domestic assets relative to the global market portfolio — countered by anchoring to the GMP and justifying any deviation); and regret aversion (herding or inaction to avoid the pain of a future wrong decision — countered by a pre-committed policy and rebalancing rules).
Exam focus: Vignettes describe a behavior and ask for the bias and its cure. High-frequency pairs: performance chasing → recency/representativeness (fix: rebalancing + long-term CME); over-trading and concentration → illusion of control / overconfidence (fix: GMP anchor, systematic process); refusing to sell a loser → loss aversion (fix: disciplined policy).
Quick reference — key formulas
The tax formulas worth memorizing
After-tax return:  r_at = r_pt × (1 − t)
  decomposed:      r_at = p_i·r·(1 − t_i) + p_cg·r·(1 − t_cg)
After-tax risk:    σ_at = σ_pt × (1 − t)
After-tax range:   R_at = R_pt / (1 − t)     (wider)
Asset location: tax-inefficient assets -> tax-deferred/exempt;
                tax-efficient assets    -> taxable
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. Name a constraint that binds a VERY LARGE fund and one that binds a VERY SMALL fund.
   -> Large: capacity limits / market impact. Small: high minimums block PE/HF/direct real estate.
2. Write the after-tax volatility formula.
   -> σ_at = σ_pt × (1 − t).
3. Should a taxable portfolio use wider or narrower rebalancing bands, and by what factor?
   -> Wider — the range scales up by 1/(1 − t).
4. Under asset location, where do tax-inefficient bonds and REITs go?
   -> In tax-deferred/exempt accounts; keep tax-efficient equities in taxable accounts.
5. Strategic revision vs tactical shift — what's the distinction?
   -> Strategic = permanent change to the SAA from changed objectives/constraints/beliefs; tactical = temporary bet on a short-term opportunity.
6. Name the two forms of TAA.
   -> Discretionary (judgment) and systematic (rules-based signals).
7. A client keeps chasing last year's best performers. Bias and remedy?
   -> Representativeness/recency; disciplined rebalancing and long-term CME.
8. What single anchor best counters the illusion of control?
   -> The global market portfolio as the neutral baseline.

readings.title: Overview of Equity Portfolio Management
readings.orientation: Orientation. A framing reading — no heavy math, but a lot of lists the exam likes to test as "describe" or "identify" prompts. Lock down four things: the roles equities play, the ways the universe is segmented, the income sources and costs of running an equity book (and the effect on net return), and the properties of a good benchmark plus the four index-weighting schemes and their tilts.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Portfolio Construction
Reading: Overview of Equity Portfolio Management
Orientation. A framing reading — no heavy math, but a lot of lists the exam likes to test as "describe" or "identify" prompts. Lock down four things: the roles equities play, the ways the universe is segmented, the income sources and costs of running an equity book (and the effect on net return), and the properties of a good benchmark plus the four index-weighting schemes and their tilts.

LOS 1 — The roles of equities in a portfolio
LOS: describe the roles of equities in the overall portfolio
What you must do:  List the functions equities serve and connect them to client objectives.
	•	Capital appreciation — the primary growth engine; equities have delivered the highest long-run returns among the major asset classes.
	•	Dividend income — a stream of cash returns, valuable for income-oriented clients and total return alike.
	•	Diversification — imperfect correlation with bonds and other assets improves the overall risk/return trade-off.
	•	Inflation hedge (long run) — companies can pass rising costs through to prices, so equities offer some real protection over long horizons (not reliably in the short run).
The appropriate role depends on the client: long-horizon, growth-oriented, risk-tolerant investors lean on equities heavily, while the equity weight also depends on the correlation of equities with the client's other holdings and liabilities.
Against these benefits, equities carry higher volatility and larger drawdowns than bonds, and they are more sensitive to the business cycle — so the equity weight is bounded by the client's risk tolerance, time horizon, and (for institutions) the need to fund liabilities.
Exam focus: A "describe the roles" list. Keep the four clean: growth, income, diversification, long-run inflation protection — and note that the short-run inflation hedge is weak.
LOS 2 — Segmenting the equity universe
LOS: describe how an equity manager’s investment universe can be segmented
What you must do:  Name the segmentation dimensions and the practical issues with classification.
Table 1 — Ways to segment the equity universe
Dimension
Segments
Size (market cap)
Large, mid, small, micro cap
Style
Value, growth, blend
Geography
Domestic, developed international, emerging, frontier
Economic sector / industry
GICS or ICB classification (e.g., 11 GICS sectors)
Size and style are often combined into a style box (small/mid/large × value/blend/growth). Segmentation defines the opportunity set, guides benchmark and manager selection, and supports diversification.
Watch out: There is no single universal classification: schemes differ (GICS vs ICB), some companies are hard to place (conglomerates, changing business mix), and indexes reconstitute periodically — so a stock's segment can change over time.
Exam focus: Know the four dimensions and the caveat that classification is not standardized. The size × style box is the most commonly referenced framework.
LOS 3 — Income and costs of owning an equity portfolio
LOS: describe the types of income and costs associated with owning and managing an equity portfolio and their potential effects on portfolio performance
What you must do:  Separate income sources from cost types and explain their drag on net return.
Table 2 — Income sources and cost types
Income sources
Cost types
Dividends
Management (and performance) fees
Securities-lending income (lending shares to short sellers for a fee)
Administration and custody costs
Dividend reinvestment / compounding
Explicit trading costs: commissions, taxes/stamp duty
—
Implicit trading costs: bid-ask spread, market impact
Effect on performance. Costs reduce net return directly, and high-turnover strategies incur far more trading cost, so turnover must be justified by added return. Securities-lending income can partly offset costs. Scale helps: larger portfolios spread fixed costs, though very large trades raise market impact.
Exam focus: Distinguish explicit costs (commissions, taxes — observable) from implicit costs (spread, market impact — estimated). Note securities lending as an income source that offsets costs, and that turnover is the main driver of trading cost.
LOS 4 — Shareholder engagement
LOS: describe the potential benefits of shareholder engagement and the role an equity manager might play in shareholder engagement
What you must do:  State the benefits and costs of engagement and the manager's channels for it.
What it is. Shareholder engagement is ongoing dialogue between investors and a company's management and board on strategy, capital allocation, governance, risk, remuneration, and ESG issues.
Benefits
	•	Can improve company performance and governance, and reduce risk.
	•	Better aligns management with shareholders' long-term interests.
	•	Gives the manager deeper insight into the company (an information benefit).
The manager's channels
	•	Voting proxies; direct dialogue and management meetings; filing or supporting shareholder proposals; and, for activists, public campaigns.
	•	Even passive/index managers engage — because they cannot sell an index constituent, stewardship is their primary lever to protect value.
Watch out: Engagement has costs: it consumes time and resources, can create conflicts of interest, may push short-term outcomes over long-term value, and suffers a free-rider problem (the benefits accrue to all shareholders, not just the engaging one).
Exam focus: A likely "describe the benefits/role" prompt. The high-value insight: passive managers must engage precisely because selling isn't an option.
LOS 5 — Rationales across the active-management spectrum
LOS: describe rationales for equity investment across the active management spectrum
What you must do:  Explain where passive, factor-based, and active sit on the spectrum and the rationale for each.
Table 3 — The active-management spectrum
Approach
Rationale
Passive (index)
Low cost and fees, tax efficiency, diversification, and a belief that markets are largely efficient — earns benchmark-like returns
Factor / smart beta / enhanced index
Capture rewarded factor premia (value, size, quality, momentum) at modest cost and tracking error
Active — fundamental
Discretionary judgment (top-down and/or bottom-up) to exploit mispricing and express views
Active — quantitative
Systematic, rules- and model-based signals to harvest inefficiencies at scale
Choosing a point on the spectrum depends on belief in market efficiency, the availability of manager skill NET of fees, cost sensitivity, tolerance for tracking error, and governance capacity to select and monitor active managers.
Exam focus: Know the passive-vs-active rationales (cost/efficiency vs alpha belief) and the fundamental-vs-quantitative split within active (discretionary judgment vs systematic models).
LOS 6 — Choosing an equity benchmark
LOS: discuss considerations in choosing a benchmark for an equity portfolio
What you must do:  State the properties of a valid benchmark and compare the four index-weighting schemes and their tilts.
Properties of a good benchmark
	•	Unambiguous — constituents and weights are clearly identifiable.
	•	Investable — the benchmark can be held or replicated.
	•	Measurable — returns can be calculated on a reasonably frequent basis.
	•	Appropriate — consistent with the manager's style and universe.
	•	Reflective of current investment opinions — the manager has views on the constituents.
	•	Specified in advance — set before the evaluation period.
	•	Accountable (owned) — the manager accepts it as the performance yardstick.
Table 4 — Index-weighting schemes and their tilts
Scheme
How weighted
Tilt / trait
Market-cap weighted
By float-adjusted market value
The investable default; concentrates in large names; low turnover; a momentum tilt
Price weighted
By share price (e.g., DJIA)
Arbitrary; a high-priced stock dominates
Equal weighted
Every constituent the same weight
Small-cap and value tilt; needs frequent rebalancing
Fundamentally weighted
By fundamentals (earnings, dividends, book)
Value/contrarian tilt; rebalances against price moves
Match the benchmark to the manager's universe and style; a float-adjusted, market-cap-weighted index is the default for broad mandates. Also weigh reconstitution frequency, float adjustment, and any sector/concentration caps.
Custom and strategy benchmarks. When no standard index fits — a factor tilt, a concentrated or thematic mandate, or a portfolio with unusual constraints — a custom or strategy-specific benchmark (e.g., a factor index or a blended benchmark) is more appropriate than forcing a broad market index that the manager does not actually track.
Exam focus: Two reliable asks: the benchmark-property checklist (know the seven), and the tilts of each weighting scheme — especially that equal weighting adds a small-cap/value tilt and needs more rebalancing, while cap weighting concentrates in large caps with a momentum tilt.
Quick reference — key lists
The lists worth memorizing
Roles of equities: growth, income, diversification, long-run inflation hedge.
Segmentation: size, style, geography, sector (size × style box).
Costs: fees, admin/custody, explicit trading (commissions, taxes), implicit trading (spread, impact).
Benchmark properties: unambiguous, investable, measurable, appropriate, reflective of opinions, specified in advance, accountable.
Weighting tilts: cap = large-cap/momentum; equal = small-cap/value + rebalancing; fundamental = value/contrarian; price = arbitrary.
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. Name three roles equities play in a portfolio.
   -> Capital growth, dividend income, diversification (plus a long-run inflation hedge).
2. Two common ways to segment the equity universe.
   -> Size and style (also geography and sector).
3. An income source for an equity portfolio beyond dividends.
   -> Securities-lending income.
4. Two implicit trading costs.
   -> Bid-ask spread and market impact (explicit = commissions, taxes).
5. Why do even passive index managers engage with companies?
   -> They can't sell an index constituent, so stewardship is their main way to protect value.
6. Fundamental vs quantitative active — the distinction?
   -> Fundamental = discretionary judgment; quantitative = systematic, rules/model-based.
7. Name three properties of a valid benchmark.
   -> Unambiguous, investable, measurable, appropriate, reflective of opinions, specified in advance, accountable.
8. What tilt does equal weighting add versus cap weighting?
   -> A small-cap (and value) tilt, requiring more frequent rebalancing.

readings.title: Overview of Fixed-Income Portfolio Management
readings.orientation: Orientation. The foundation for the fixed-income specialization readings. Three pieces carry the math: the fixed-income return model, the effect of leverage, and the duration measures. The rest is applied structure — the roles and mandate types, bond-market liquidity, taxable vs tax-exempt management, and the three liability-management techniques (immunization, cash-flow matching, and laddering). Two calculations to have cold: the return decomposition and the levered-return formula.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Portfolio Construction
Reading: Overview of Fixed-Income Portfolio Management
Orientation. The foundation for the fixed-income specialization readings. Three pieces carry the math: the fixed-income return model, the effect of leverage, and the duration measures. The rest is applied structure — the roles and mandate types, bond-market liquidity, taxable vs tax-exempt management, and the three liability-management techniques (immunization, cash-flow matching, and laddering). Two calculations to have cold: the return decomposition and the levered-return formula.

LOS 1 — Roles of fixed income and mandate classification
LOS: discuss roles of fixed-income securities in portfolios and how fixed-income mandates may be classified
What you must do:  List the roles bonds play and the two broad ways mandates are classified.
Roles of fixed income
	•	Diversification — low or negative correlation with equities, especially high-quality government bonds.
	•	Regular income — predictable coupon cash flows.
	•	Capital preservation — high-quality bonds return principal at maturity.
	•	Liability matching — duration and cash flows can be structured to fund liabilities (LDI).
	•	Inflation protection — via inflation-linked bonds (e.g., TIPS).
Mandate classification
Two broad categories: (1) liability-based (structured) mandates dedicated to funding a set of liabilities, and (2) total-return mandates measured against a bond index — which range from pure indexing to enhanced indexing to active management. Mandates are also described by sector, credit quality, duration, and region.
Exam focus: The clean split is liability-based vs total-return (index-benchmarked). Within total-return, know the pure-indexing → enhanced-indexing → active progression.
LOS 2 — Fixed-income risk, return, and correlation measures
LOS: describe fixed-income portfolio measures of risk and return as well as correlation characteristics
What you must do:  Know the duration family and other risk measures, and the correlation profile of bonds.
Table 1 — Fixed-income risk measures
Measure
What it captures
Macaulay duration
Weighted-average time to receive the bond's cash flows
Modified duration
Approx. % price change for a 1% parallel yield change
Effective duration
Price sensitivity for bonds with embedded options (uses curve shifts)
Key rate durations
Sensitivity to specific maturities — captures non-parallel shifts
Spread duration
Price sensitivity to a change in the credit spread
Money duration / PVBP
Dollar price change (per 1bp for PVBP)
Convexity
Second-order curvature of the price–yield relationship
Portfolio duration is the market-value-weighted average of the constituents' durations. Price change is approximated as 
ΔP/P ≈ (−MD × Δy) + (½ × convexity × Δy²)
Correlation. High-quality government bonds have low or negative correlation with equities (the diversification benefit), while credit-sensitive bonds — investment-grade and especially high-yield — are more correlated with equities, since they share exposure to the economic/credit cycle.
Exam focus: Match the duration to the job: effective duration for optionality, key rate durations for curve reshaping, spread duration for credit moves, PVBP for the dollar hedge. Remember portfolio duration is a market-value-weighted average.
LOS 3 — Bond-market liquidity
LOS: describe bond market liquidity, including the differences among market sub-sectors, and discuss the effect of liquidity on fixed-income portfolio management
What you must do:  Rank sub-sector liquidity and explain how illiquidity shapes management.
Bonds are far less liquid than equities: they trade over-the-counter through dealers, are highly heterogeneous (one issuer has many bonds), trade infrequently, and carry wider bid-ask spreads.
Table 2 — Liquidity by sub-sector (most to least liquid)
Sub-sector
Liquidity
Note
Sovereign / government
Highest
On-the-run issues most liquid; off-the-run less so
Investment-grade corporate
Moderate
Larger, recent issues trade more readily
High-yield corporate
Lower
Wider spreads, thinner market
Securitized / structured / EM
Lowest
Complex, heterogeneous, infrequently traded
Effect on management: illiquidity means higher transaction costs and difficulty trading in size, so managers often use liquid proxies (government futures, bond ETFs, on-the-run bonds, CDS), tilt toward buy-and-hold, and demand a liquidity premium for less-liquid holdings.
Exam focus: Know the liquidity ranking (sovereign > IG > high-yield > securitized/EM) and the on-the-run vs off-the-run distinction, plus the use of futures/ETFs/CDS as liquid proxies.
LOS 4 — A model for fixed-income returns
LOS: describe and interpret a model for fixed-income returns
What you must do:  Decompose expected fixed-income return into its components and compute it.
E(R) ≈ Yield income + Rolldown return
     + E(ΔP from investor's yield/spread view)
     − E(credit losses) + E(currency gains/losses)
Yield income = coupon (plus reinvestment) ÷ current price ≈ current yield; rolldown return is the price gain as the bond rolls down an unchanged curve; rolling yield = yield income + rolldown. The view term uses −MD × Δy + ½ × convexity × Δy²; expected credit losses = probability of default × loss severity.
Worked example — expected one-year return on a 10-year bond
10-yr, 4% coupon, price 99. Over a year it rolls to the 9-yr point on an unchanged curve, where its price would be 99.6. The investor expects spreads to tighten, so yield falls 10 bps; MD 7.5, convexity 65. Default probability 1%, loss severity 45%.
Yield income   = 4 / 99                    = 4.04%
Rolldown       = (99.6 − 99) / 99          = 0.61%   (rolling yield 4.65%)
ΔP from view   = −7.5×(−0.0010)
                 + 0.5×65×(0.0010)^2       = 0.75%
Credit losses  = 0.01 × 0.45              = 0.45%
E(R) ≈ 4.65 + 0.75 − 0.45                 = 4.95%
Exam focus: Item sets give you the pieces and ask for total return or one component. Rolldown assumes an UNCHANGED curve; the expected shift is captured separately in the view term. Currency only appears for foreign-currency bonds.
LOS 5 — Leverage in fixed-income portfolios
LOS: discuss the use of leverage, alternative methods for leveraging, and risks that leverage creates in fixed-income portfolios
What you must do:  Name the leverage methods, compute the levered return, and state the risks.
Methods of leverage
	•	Repurchase agreements (repos) — borrow cash against bonds at the repo rate, subject to a haircut; the most common tool.
	•	Futures — embedded leverage: large notional exposure for a small margin.
	•	Swaps — interest-rate and total-return swaps create exposure without full funding.
	•	Securities lending, structured instruments (inverse floaters), and credit lines.
The equity (portfolio) return under leverage is the asset return plus the leverage ratio times the spread of the asset return over the borrowing cost:
r_p = r_I + (V_B / V_E) × (r_I − r_B)
Worked example — leverage amplifies both ways
Equity 100; borrow 150 at r_B = 3%; total 250 invested in bonds. Leverage ratio V_B/V_E = 1.5.
If assets return 5%:  r_p = 5 + 1.5×(5 − 3) = 5 + 3 = 8%
If assets return 1%:  r_p = 1 + 1.5×(1 − 3) = 1 − 3 = −2%
The 1.5x leverage turns a 5% asset return into 8% — but a weak 1% return into a −2% loss. Leverage magnifies the spread over borrowing cost in both directions.
Watch out: Leverage adds more than return volatility: repos must be rolled (rollover/funding risk), margin calls can force selling into a falling market (forced deleveraging), and leverage effectively lengthens duration/exposure. These are the risks graders want, not just 'bigger losses.'
Exam focus: Expect the levered-return calculation and a listing of methods (repo, futures, swaps) and risks (rollover/funding, margin calls/forced deleveraging, magnified losses).
LOS 6 — Taxable vs tax-exempt investors
LOS: discuss differences in managing fixed-income portfolios for taxable and tax-exempt investors
What you must do:  Contrast the two and compute a taxable-equivalent yield.
Taxable investors care about after-tax return: coupon income is usually taxed as ordinary income, capital gains are taxed (long vs short), and (in the US) municipal bonds pay tax-exempt interest that is attractive to high-bracket investors. They manage with tax-aware trading, loss harvesting, longer holding periods, and lower turnover. Compare a muni to a taxable bond with the taxable-equivalent yield:
Taxable-equivalent yield = muni yield / (1 − tax rate)
Worked example — taxable-equivalent yield
A 3% municipal bond for an investor in a 35% bracket:
TEY = 3% / (1 − 0.35) = 3% / 0.65 = 4.62%
So the muni is preferable to any comparable taxable bond yielding less than 4.62%.
Tax-exempt investors (pensions, endowments) focus on pre-tax/total return, gain nothing from municipals, and can trade freely without tax drag.
Exam focus: Know the taxable-equivalent-yield calculation and the behavioral difference: taxable investors trade less and prefer munis / tax efficiency; tax-exempt investors simply maximize pre-tax return.
LOS 7 — Liability-driven investing (LDI)
LOS: describe liability-driven investing
What you must do:  Classify liabilities by certainty of amount/timing and state the immunization conditions.
Table 3 — Liability types by certainty
Type
Amount
Timing
Example
I
Known
Known
Fixed-rate bond; fixed-term GIC
II
Known
Uncertain
Callable / putable bond
III
Uncertain
Known
Floating-rate note
IV
Uncertain
Uncertain
DB pension (longevity + inflation)
Immunization structures assets to hedge the interest-rate risk of the liabilities. For a SINGLE liability: match present values, set the portfolio's Macaulay duration equal to the liability's horizon, and minimize convexity/dispersion to reduce exposure to non-parallel shifts (structural risk). For MULTIPLE liabilities: match PV, match money duration, and keep asset convexity/dispersion slightly above the liabilities' but minimized, with the asset cash-flow range bracketing the liabilities.
Watch out: For a single liability, minimizing dispersion/convexity is what controls structural risk. A barbell (high dispersion) is exposed to curve twists even if its duration matches; a bullet (low dispersion) immunizes better.
Exam focus: Know the single-liability immunization conditions cold: match PV, Macaulay duration = horizon, minimize dispersion. This underpins the Liability-Driven and Index-Based Strategies reading later in the curriculum.
LOS 8 — Cash-flow matching
LOS: describe the strategy of cash flow matching
What you must do:  Explain cash-flow matching and contrast it with immunization.
Cash-flow matching builds a dedicated portfolio whose inflows — coupons plus maturing principal — exactly meet each liability outflow in timing and amount. Because each liability is funded by a maturing bond, there is essentially no interest-rate or reinvestment risk once the portfolio is set.
Versus immunization: cash-flow matching is more conservative — no rebalancing and no reinvestment risk — but it is costlier and more rigid, because it requires specific bonds maturing on specific dates. Immunization is more flexible and cheaper but must be rebalanced as yields and durations drift, and it bears some structural risk.
Exam focus: The contrast is the exam point: cash-flow matching = no reinvestment risk but costly/rigid; immunization = flexible but needs rebalancing and carries structural risk.
LOS 9 — Laddered bond portfolios
LOS: describe construction, benefits, limitations, and risk–return characteristics of a laddered bond portfolio
What you must do:  Describe a ladder and its benefits versus bullet and barbell structures.
Construction. A ladder holds roughly equal amounts of bonds maturing at evenly spaced intervals (e.g., equal weights maturing each year from 1 to 10).
Benefits
	•	Diversifies across the yield curve, balancing price risk and reinvestment risk rather than betting on one maturity.
	•	Provides regular liquidity — a bond matures each period, giving predictable cash flow and reinvestment flexibility.
	•	Higher convexity than a duration-matched bullet, because the cash flows are spread out — a benefit when yields move.
	•	Simple to run, with low turnover.
Limitations and positioning. A ladder is not optimized and will not match a specific liability as precisely as cash-flow matching. Against the alternatives: a bullet concentrates at one maturity (higher curve-point risk, less liquidity); a barbell splits short and long (more convexity but more exposure to curve twists); the ladder sits between, offering liquidity and convexity with minimal active management.
Exam focus: The reliable points: a ladder balances price vs reinvestment risk, throws off regular liquidity, and has MORE convexity than a bullet of the same duration. Contrast bullet (concentrated) vs barbell (dispersed) vs ladder (even).
Quick reference — key formulas
The formulas worth memorizing
Portfolio duration = Σ w_i × D_i
ΔP/P ≈ (−MD × Δy) + (½ × convexity × Δy²)
E(R) ≈ yield income + rolldown + ΔP(view) − credit losses + FX
Levered return:  r_p = r_I + (V_B / V_E) × (r_I − r_B)
Taxable-equivalent yield = muni yield / (1 − tax rate)
Single-liability immunization: match PV; Macaulay duration =
                horizon; minimize dispersion/convexity
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. Two broad ways fixed-income mandates are classified.
   -> Liability-based (LDI) vs total-return (index-benchmarked).
2. Which duration is used for bonds with embedded options?
   -> Effective duration.
3. Rank liquidity: sovereign, high-yield, IG corporate.
   -> Sovereign > IG corporate > high-yield.
4. In the return model, which term equals probability of default × loss severity?
   -> Expected credit losses.
5. Equity 100; borrow 100 at 2%; assets return 6%. Levered return?
   -> 6 + 1.0 × (6 − 2) = 10%.
6. Taxable-equivalent yield of a 3% muni for a 35% investor?
   -> 3% / (1 − 0.35) = 4.62%.
7. Single-liability immunization: set Macaulay duration to what, and minimize what?
   -> Equal to the liability horizon; minimize dispersion/convexity (structural risk).
8. Cash-flow matching vs immunization — the key trade-off?
   -> Cash-flow matching eliminates reinvestment risk but is costly/rigid; immunization is flexible but needs rebalancing and bears structural risk.
9. Two benefits of a bond ladder?
   -> Regular liquidity/reinvestment flexibility and higher convexity than a duration-matched bullet.

readings.title: Asset Allocation to Alternative Investments
readings.orientation: Orientation. This reading moves from "should we hold alternatives" to "how do we size, frame, fund, and monitor them." There are eight unique learning outcomes (the source text split the first and repeated it). None is a pure formula LOS, but two areas — the diversification math behind the roles, and the cash-flow mechanics behind liquidity planning — reward quantitative work, so this note carries worked examples for both. The through-lines the exam returns to again and again: (1) not every alternative diversifies equity risk, and the reliable mitigators trade away liquidity; (2) the asset-class label understates how correlated "diversifiers" really are, which is why a risk-factor lens matters; (3) appraisal-based (smoothed) data breaks naive optimizers; and (4) illiquidity forces you to plan cash flows years ahead. Learn the categories, the risk-based framing, the fee and denominator-effect arithmetic, and the commitment-pacing logic.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Portfolio Construction
Reading: Asset Allocation to Alternative Investments
Orientation. This reading moves from "should we hold alternatives" to "how do we size, frame, fund, and monitor them." There are eight unique learning outcomes (the source text split the first and repeated it). None is a pure formula LOS, but two areas — the diversification math behind the roles, and the cash-flow mechanics behind liquidity planning — reward quantitative work, so this note carries worked examples for both. The through-lines the exam returns to again and again: (1) not every alternative diversifies equity risk, and the reliable mitigators trade away liquidity; (2) the asset-class label understates how correlated "diversifiers" really are, which is why a risk-factor lens matters; (3) appraisal-based (smoothed) data breaks naive optimizers; and (4) illiquidity forces you to plan cash flows years ahead. Learn the categories, the risk-based framing, the fee and denominator-effect arithmetic, and the commitment-pacing logic.

LOS 1 — The roles of alternatives in a multi-asset portfolio
LOS: explain the roles that alternative investments play in multi-asset portfolios
What you must do:  List the functions alternatives serve, map each to the category that delivers it, and be able to show the diversification benefit quantitatively.
Alternatives are added for four broad reasons, and a well-constructed program is explicit about which role each allocation fills — a fund held "for diversification" should actually diversify, not simply relabel equity risk.
	•	Capital growth. Private equity (buyout and venture), and long-biased and event-driven hedge funds, target equity-like or higher long-run returns, in part by harvesting an illiquidity premium and manager skill that public markets do not offer.
	•	Income. Core real estate, infrastructure, and private credit throw off contractual cash yield, valuable for spending-driven investors and for total return.
	•	Diversification. Imperfect correlation with public stocks and bonds improves the portfolio's risk/return trade-off; diversifying hedge-fund styles (global macro, equity market-neutral, managed futures) are held primarily for this.
	•	Inflation protection and risk mitigation. Real assets (real estate, commodities, infrastructure, timberland, farmland) hedge inflation because their cash flows and values rise with the price level; certain hedge-fund strategies mitigate equity drawdowns.
Table 1 — Alternative categories, their primary role, and behavior
Category
Primary role
Behavior / caution
Private equity
Growth
Equity-like risk plus illiquidity premium; still carries equity beta
Real estate / infrastructure
Income + inflation hedge
Yield and real-asset protection; appraisal-based (smoothed) values
Private credit
Income
Yield with credit and liquidity risk; correlates with credit cycle
Commodities / real assets
Inflation protection
Real protection; can be volatile and non-income-producing
Hedge funds — long-biased / event
Growth
Retain meaningful equity exposure
Hedge funds — macro / market-neutral / trend
Diversification / mitigation
Low equity correlation; the genuine diversifiers
Why role clarity matters. Correlations are not uniform across the category: a long-biased equity hedge fund still carries most of the market's beta, so calling it a "diversifier" overstates its benefit. The diversification role is best filled by strategies whose returns are structurally independent of equity direction — market-neutral, macro, and managed futures.
Quantifying the diversification role
The reason diversification adds value is that portfolio risk grows less than proportionally with the correlation between assets. For a two-asset mix:
σ_p = sqrt( w_A²σ_A² + w_B²σ_B² + 2 w_A w_B ρ σ_A σ_B )
Worked example — the diversification benefit
A 60/40 blend of equities (σ = 15%) and a diversifying alternative (σ = 10%) with correlation ρ = 0.3.
σ_p = sqrt( 0.6²×15² + 0.4²×10² + 2×0.6×0.4×0.3×15×10 )
    = sqrt( 81 + 16 + 21.6 )
    = sqrt(118.6) = 10.89%
The weighted-average volatility would be 0.6×15 + 0.4×10 = 13.0%, so the low correlation removes about 2.1 percentage points of risk. If ρ were higher (say 0.8), the benefit would shrink — which is exactly why a role clarity check on correlation matters.
Exam focus: A "describe the roles" list, but be ready to defend it: tie each role to the category that delivers it, and stress that long-biased strategies are NOT true diversifiers. If given volatilities and a correlation, be able to compute the blended-portfolio risk and explain that a lower correlation produces a larger diversification benefit.
LOS 2 — Alternatives vs bonds as equity-risk mitigators
LOS: compare alternative investments and bonds as risk mitigators in relation to a long equity position
What you must do:  Explain when alternatives beat high-quality bonds at cushioning equity risk, and articulate the trade-offs of each.
The bond benchmark. High-quality government bonds are the classic equity-risk mitigator for three reasons: they tend to rally when equities sell off (a flight-to-quality that produces negative correlation in a crisis), they are highly liquid precisely when liquidity is scarce, and their duration delivers price gains as central banks cut rates in a downturn. This makes their hedge dependable — but it comes at the cost of a low expected return, and in a low-yield or rising-rate environment both the expected return AND the size of the negative-correlation cushion shrink (there is less room for yields to fall).
Where alternatives can do better. Some alternatives mitigate equity risk while offering a higher expected return than bonds. Managed futures / trend-following in particular has historically produced "crisis alpha" — it tends to profit in prolonged equity drawdowns as it rides the downward trend — and global macro and equity market-neutral can also hold up when equities fall. These strategies can therefore serve as return-generating diversifiers rather than low-yielding insurance.
Table 2 — Comparing equity-risk mitigators
Attribute
High-quality bonds
Diversifying alternatives
Expected return
Low
Higher
Liquidity
High (best in a crisis)
Often low; lock-ups
Crisis correlation to equities
Reliably negative/low
Varies — trend low, others can spike
Reliability of the hedge
High and well-understood
Less certain; strategy-dependent
Watch out: The trap is assuming every "diversifier" holds up in a crash. Long-biased and credit-oriented strategies actually correlate WITH equities when markets fall, and illiquid alternatives cannot be sold to raise cash mid-crisis. A true mitigator must (a) be reliably uncorrelated or negatively correlated when equities drop, and (b) be liquid enough to matter. Trend/managed futures is the go-to example that meets both; a long-biased equity hedge fund meets neither.
Drill: Rank three candidates as equity-risk mitigators: (i) 10-year government bonds, (ii) a managed-futures fund, (iii) a long-biased equity hedge fund. (Answer: bonds and managed futures are genuine mitigators — bonds most reliable and liquid, managed futures higher-returning but less certain; the long-biased hedge fund is NOT a mitigator, since it carries equity beta and falls with the market.)
Exam focus: The nuance the graders want: bonds are the reliable, liquid, low-return mitigator whose cushion weakens at low yields; select alternatives (trend, macro, market-neutral) mitigate with higher expected return but sacrifice liquidity and may fail when correlations spike. Name trend/managed futures as the crisis-alpha example, and be explicit that a mitigator must work WHEN equities fall.
LOS 3 — Traditional vs risk-based opportunity sets
LOS: compare traditional and risk-based approaches to defining the investment opportunity set, including alternative investments
What you must do:  Contrast grouping by asset class with grouping by underlying risk factor, and explain why alternatives expose the weakness of the asset-class view.
Table 3 — Two ways to frame the opportunity set
Approach
How it groups
Strengths
Weaknesses
Traditional (asset-class)
By named class: equities, bonds, real estate, PE, hedge funds
Intuitive, simple, maps to how managers and mandates are organized
Classes overlap in risk; hides shared factor exposures; understates true correlation
Risk-based (factor)
By underlying factor: equity, rates, credit, inflation, liquidity, currency, volatility
Reveals the real risk drivers and hidden overlaps; better diversification
Less intuitive; harder to implement and communicate; factor definitions are judgment calls
Why alternatives break the asset-class view. A single alternative bundles several risk factors at once. Decompose a typical long/short equity hedge fund and you might find it is mostly equity beta, plus some credit and liquidity exposure, plus a residual of manager-specific (idiosyncratic) return. Under the asset-class lens it sits in a tidy "hedge fund" bucket that looks like a diversifier; under the factor lens it is revealed as largely more equity risk. The asset-class label can therefore badly understate how correlated the "alternatives" bucket is with the equity the portfolio already holds.
Illustration — factor decomposition of an alternative
A long/short equity fund's return might decompose approximately as:
Return ≈ 0.5 × (equity factor)
       + 0.2 × (credit factor)
       + 0.1 × (liquidity factor)
       + idiosyncratic (manager) return
The asset-class view files this under "hedge funds" (apparent diversifier). The risk-based view shows it is half equity beta — so adding it does far less to diversify an equity-heavy portfolio than the label implies.
Exam focus: Know the trade-off cleanly: asset-class grouping is intuitive but hides shared factors; risk-based grouping exposes the real drivers but is harder to implement. Alternatives are precisely where the asset-class lens most often misleads, because one holding carries several factors and may be dominated by the very equity risk it is supposed to diversify.
LOS 4 — Investment considerations by type of alternative
LOS: discuss investment considerations that are important in allocating to different types of alternative investments
What you must do:  Identify the practical considerations that differ across alternative types and quantify the fee drag.
Because alternatives differ so much from public securities and from each other, allocation requires attention to a set of considerations that barely arise for a public-index investor:
	•	Return/risk and correlation profile. What role does the investment fill, and — critically — how does it behave in stress rather than on average?
	•	Liquidity and lock-ups. Private funds have multi-year lock-ups, draw capital via calls, and return it via distributions on their own schedule (the J-curve). Committed capital is not the same as invested capital.
	•	Fees. Fees are high and often performance-based (the classic "2 and 20"), so the net-of-fee return can be far below the gross return, and the fee structure itself affects manager incentives.
	•	Manager selection and dispersion. The gap between top-quartile and bottom-quartile managers is enormous in alternatives — far wider than in public markets — so manager selection is a primary driver of outcomes, not a secondary refinement.
	•	Operational complexity, transparency, and valuation. Reporting is less frequent and less standardized, valuations are appraisal-based and lagged (and therefore smoothed), and operational and governance risks (controls, custody, key-person) demand dedicated due diligence.
Worked example — the drag from a "2 and 20" fee
A hedge fund earns a 10% gross return. It charges a 2% management fee and a 20% incentive fee on the return above the management fee.
Management fee   = 2.0%
Incentive fee    = 20% × (10% − 2%) = 20% × 8% = 1.6%
Net return       = 10% − 2.0% − 1.6% = 6.4%
Total fee drag   = 3.6%  (about 36% of the gross return)
More than a third of the gross return is consumed by fees. This is why fee terms, hurdle rates, and high-water marks are central to alternatives due diligence — a modest edge can be entirely eaten by fees.
Watch out: Do not evaluate an alternative on gross returns or on smoothed (appraisal-based) volatility. Gross returns ignore the large fee drag, and smoothed valuations understate true risk and correlation — both make the investment look more attractive than it is.
Exam focus: The recurring theme: alternatives demand far more due diligence than public assets because of illiquidity, high (performance-based) fees, huge manager dispersion, and weak transparency/valuation. Be able to compute a net return after a 2-and-20 fee, and cite operational due diligence as distinct from investment due diligence.
LOS 5 — Suitability considerations
LOS: discuss suitability considerations in allocating to alternative investments
What you must do:  Judge whether a given investor is suited to hold alternatives, and how large an allocation is prudent.
Suitability turns on whether the investor can bear the defining features of alternatives — illiquidity, complexity, and the need for skilled oversight. The key tests:
	•	Time horizon — is it long enough to bear multi-year lock-ups and let the illiquidity premium and value creation play out?
	•	Liquidity elsewhere — are there enough liquid assets to meet spending AND capital calls, even in a down market when calls may accelerate and distributions slow?
	•	Governance and expertise — does the investor have the capacity to source, conduct due diligence on, select, and monitor managers (given the huge dispersion)?
	•	Scale — is the pool large enough to diversify across managers, strategies, and vintage years and to meet minimum investment sizes?
	•	Tolerance for complexity and transparency limits — can the investor accept lagged valuations, limited reporting, and the career/headline risk of illiquid positions?
Applying it across investor types. Large endowments and sovereign wealth funds — long horizons, low near-term liquidity needs, strong governance, and scale — are well suited and often hold large alternatives allocations. A private individual with near-term spending needs or a small, thinly-governed pool is poorly suited, and the appropriate response is a smaller allocation (or liquid-alternative substitutes) rather than the full illiquid program.
Drill: An investor has a 30-year horizon, modest near-term spending, a professional investment staff, and $5bn in assets. A second has a 4-year horizon, large near-term withdrawals, and $10m with no dedicated staff. Who is suited to a large illiquid alternatives allocation? (Answer: the first — long horizon, liquidity elsewhere, governance, scale. The second fails horizon, liquidity, governance, and scale, and should hold little or none.)
Exam focus: Match the investor to alternatives: long horizon + low near-term liquidity needs + strong governance + scale = suitable. A vignette that stresses near-term liquidity needs, a short horizon, or thin governance is steering you toward "not suitable" or "reduce/limit the allocation."
LOS 6 — Approaches to allocating to alternatives
LOS: discuss approaches to asset allocation to alternative investments
What you must do:  Compare the ways alternatives are incorporated into the allocation, and explain why naive mean-variance optimization fails.
Standard single-period mean-variance optimization (MVO) fails for alternatives for two reasons: their returns are non-normal (skewed, fat-tailed, path-dependent through capital calls and lock-ups), and their reported risk/correlation inputs are understated by appraisal smoothing. Three better approaches:
	•	Monte Carlo simulation. Model multi-period, non-normal outcomes and path dependency — capital calls, distributions, illiquidity, and the sequence of returns — rather than relying on a single-period mean and variance.
	•	Optimization with adjusted inputs. Include alternatives in an MVO framework, but first DE-SMOOTH the appraisal-based volatility and correlation estimates (and/or impose constraints), so the optimizer does not over-allocate to assets that only appear low-risk and low-correlation.
	•	Risk-factor-based allocation. Allocate by factor exposure rather than by asset-class bucket, which handles the overlapping risks of alternatives (the LOS 3 problem) far better than asset-class weights.
Why smoothed data misleads the optimizer
Appraisal-based valuations are updated infrequently and tend to lag the market, so successive reported returns are positively autocorrelated. That autocorrelation dampens the measured volatility and lowers the measured correlation with public assets — making the asset look artificially attractive. De-smoothing reverses this, restoring a more realistic (higher) risk and correlation.
Illustration — smoothing understates risk
A private real-asset fund reports a volatility of about 8% from its quarterly appraisal-based returns.
Because those returns are smoothed (positively autocorrelated), the de-smoothed volatility might be on the order of 12–13%, with a higher correlation to equities than the raw data shows.
Feeding the raw 8% into an optimizer would cause it to load up on the fund; feeding the de-smoothed ~13% produces a far more modest — and more honest — allocation.
Watch out: Never feed raw (smoothed) private-asset volatilities and correlations into an optimizer. The smoothing makes them look low-risk and low-correlation, and an unconstrained optimizer will respond by massively over-weighting them. De-smooth the inputs or impose allocation constraints first.
Exam focus: The tested point mirrors the Principles-of-Asset-Allocation reading: alternatives break naive MVO because of stale/smoothed data and non-normal, path-dependent returns. The fixes are Monte Carlo simulation, de-smoothed (or constrained) MVO inputs, and a risk-factor approach. Be ready to explain WHY smoothing understates risk — positive autocorrelation from lagged appraisals.
LOS 7 — Liquidity planning
LOS: discuss the importance of liquidity planning in allocating to alternative investments
What you must do:  Explain the cash-flow mechanics of private funds, plan for them with commitment pacing, and quantify the denominator effect.
The mechanics. A private fund does not take all your money at once. It draws committed capital over time through capital calls (often roughly a quarter of the commitment per year over the first few years) and returns capital later through distributions as investments are realized. The result is the J-curve: early years show negative net cash flow and negative returns (fees and calls precede value realization), and the payoff comes later. Two consequences follow: committed capital is not the same as invested capital, and the investor must hold enough liquid assets to meet calls even when markets — and the investor's other assets — are down.
Commitment pacing
Because a fund takes years to draw and then return capital, reaching and holding a target private-asset allocation requires a steady commitment program across vintage years, so that as older funds begin distributing, those distributions help fund the calls of newer commitments. This keeps net exposure roughly stable over time. Over-committing — pledging a large amount quickly to hit the target sooner — is dangerous: in a downturn, distributions slow (realizations are postponed) while calls may accelerate (managers deploy into cheap assets), and the investor can face a liquidity crunch.
Illustration — commitment pacing
An investor wants a steady private-equity exposure. Funds call ~25% of commitments per year over four years and have a ~10-year life.
Rather than committing the whole target in one vintage, the investor commits a similar amount EACH year across vintages. As the earliest funds mature and distribute, those proceeds fund the calls of the newest commitments, holding invested exposure near target.
Committing everything in a single year instead would create a wave of calls up front and leave the investor exposed to a call spike with no offsetting distributions if markets turn.
The denominator effect
When public markets fall, the private allocation — whose stale, appraisal-based value has not yet marked down — rises as a SHARE of the now-smaller total portfolio, pushing the investor over the private-asset target or limit. This happens precisely when private stakes are hardest to sell, so it can force awkward decisions (halting new commitments, or selling on the secondary market at a discount).
Worked example — the denominator effect
A $1,000M portfolio holds $800M public and $200M private (a 20% private allocation, with a 25% policy limit).
Equities fall 30%: public value 800 → 560
Private value (stale, slow to mark) stays ≈ 200
New total = 560 + 200 = 760
Private share = 200 / 760 = 26.3%
With no new commitment and no change in the private assets themselves, the private allocation jumped from 20% to 26.3% — through the 25% limit — purely because the denominator shrank. Liquidity planning must anticipate this so the investor is not forced to sell illiquid stakes at a discount to get back under the limit.
Exam focus: High-value and heavily tested. Know the J-curve, the difference between committed and invested capital, commitment pacing (steady vintage-year commitments so distributions fund new calls, and why over-committing is dangerous), and the denominator effect (be able to recompute the private share after a public-market drop). Liquidity planning is the theme graders reward here.
LOS 8 — Monitoring alternative investment programs
LOS: discuss considerations in monitoring alternative investment programs
What you must do:  State what ongoing monitoring of an alternatives program must cover, and why it differs from monitoring public managers.
Because you cannot simply sell out of a disappointing private fund, monitoring is both harder and more important than for public managers. It spans four areas:
	•	Performance vs the right benchmark. Compare against public-market equivalents (PME) or peer/vintage benchmarks, recognizing that private returns are appraisal-lagged and that internal rates of return (IRRs) are money-weighted — so they are not directly comparable to the time-weighted returns of public managers.
	•	Manager and organizational changes. Watch for key-person departures, strategy or style drift, rapid asset growth (capacity strain), and changes in ownership or incentives.
	•	Operational and risk monitoring. Track valuation practices, leverage, counterparty and liquidity risk, and adherence to the fund's terms and guidelines.
	•	Commitment and liquidity tracking. Monitor actual versus planned capital calls and distributions, and current versus target exposure — feeding directly back into the commitment-pacing and liquidity plan of LOS 7.
Watch out: Do not treat a private fund's reported IRR as comparable to a public manager's time-weighted return. IRR is money-weighted and sensitive to the timing of calls/distributions, and appraisal lags flatter interim figures. Use PME or vintage-peer comparisons for a fair read.
Exam focus: Monitoring must go beyond returns to manager/organizational stability, operational and valuation risk, and commitment/liquidity tracking — because exiting a poor private fund is slow and costly. Know that IRRs are money-weighted and that PME is the tool for benchmarking private performance against public markets.
Quick reference — key concepts and formulas
The essentials worth memorizing
Two-asset risk: σ_p = sqrt(w_A²σ_A² + w_B²σ_B² + 2 w_A w_B ρ σ_A σ_B)
Net return after 2-and-20 (on excess over mgmt fee):
   net = gross − mgmt − 0.20 × (gross − mgmt)
Denominator effect: private share = private / (fallen public + private)
Roles: growth (PE) / income (RE, private credit) / diversification
   (macro, market-neutral, trend) / inflation hedge (real assets)
Mitigators: bonds = reliable, liquid, low-return; trend = crisis alpha
Frame the opportunity set by RISK FACTOR, not asset-class label
De-smooth appraisal data before optimizing (smoothing understates risk)
Fund cash flows: J-curve; committed != invested; use commitment pacing
Benchmark private performance with PME (IRR is money-weighted)
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. Name the four roles alternatives play and one category that fills each.
   -> Growth (private equity), income (real estate / private credit), diversification (macro / market-neutral / trend), inflation protection (real assets).
2. Equities σ 15%, alternative σ 10%, ρ 0.3, 60/40. Portfolio volatility?
   -> sqrt(81 + 16 + 21.6) = sqrt(118.6) = 10.89% (vs 13.0% weighted average).
3. Why can trend/managed futures beat bonds as an equity-risk mitigator, and what's the cost?
   -> It can mitigate with a higher expected return (crisis alpha), but sacrifices liquidity and is less reliable than bonds.
4. Why is a long-biased equity hedge fund NOT a true diversifier?
   -> It retains most of the equity beta, so it falls with the market.
5. Traditional vs risk-based opportunity set — key difference, and why alternatives expose it?
   -> Traditional groups by asset class (hides shared factors); risk-based groups by factor. One alternative bundles several factors, so the asset-class label understates its true (often equity) risk.
6. Gross 10%, 2% management fee, 20% incentive on the excess. Net return?
   -> 10 − 2 − 0.20×(10−2) = 10 − 2 − 1.6 = 6.4% (3.6% fee drag).
7. Why does naive MVO over-allocate to private assets, and the fix?
   -> Appraisal smoothing (positive autocorrelation) understates their volatility and correlation; de-smooth or constrain the inputs (or use Monte Carlo / a factor approach).
8. What is the J-curve, and how does committed capital differ from invested capital?
   -> Early negative net cash flow/returns from a private fund; committed capital is pledged but drawn over time via calls, so it exceeds invested capital initially.
9. $800M public / $200M private (20%). Public falls 30%; private unmarked. New private share?
   -> 200 / (560 + 200) = 26.3% (the denominator effect pushes it over a 25% limit).
10. Why can't a private fund's IRR be compared directly to a public manager's return?
   -> IRR is money-weighted (timing-sensitive) while public returns are time-weighted; appraisal lags also flatter interim figures. Use PME.

readings.title: An Overview of Private Wealth Management
readings.orientation: Orientation. The foundation for every individual-investor question on the exam, and a rich source of constructed-response (essay) material — above all the private-client Investment Policy Statement, whose required-return calculation is a perennial. Five learning outcomes, all built on one organizing idea from the asset-allocation readings: an individual's TOTAL wealth is human capital plus financial capital, and both the portfolio and the risk-management plan (insurance, annuities) must reflect how that balance shifts across the life cycle. This note works the quantitative pieces the exam actually tests — the present value of human capital, the life-insurance need, taxable-equivalent yield, after-tax and real returns — and closes with a full worked IPS, the format you will most likely see in the morning session. Learn the human-capital/financial-capital framework, the tax and inflation arithmetic, and the IPS return-requirement build cold.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Portfolio Construction
Reading: An Overview of Private Wealth Management
Orientation. The foundation for every individual-investor question on the exam, and a rich source of constructed-response (essay) material — above all the private-client Investment Policy Statement, whose required-return calculation is a perennial. Five learning outcomes, all built on one organizing idea from the asset-allocation readings: an individual's TOTAL wealth is human capital plus financial capital, and both the portfolio and the risk-management plan (insurance, annuities) must reflect how that balance shifts across the life cycle. This note works the quantitative pieces the exam actually tests — the present value of human capital, the life-insurance need, taxable-equivalent yield, after-tax and real returns — and closes with a full worked IPS, the format you will most likely see in the morning session. Learn the human-capital/financial-capital framework, the tax and inflation arithmetic, and the IPS return-requirement build cold.

LOS 1 — Types of individual wealth and its global distribution
LOS: discuss the different types of individual wealth and how wealth is created and distributed globally
