What you must do:  Describe how wealth is created, the client segments and what each needs, and the shape of the global distribution.
How wealth is created
Wealth arises from three broad sources, and the source shapes the client's balance sheet and planning needs far more than the dollar amount alone:
	•	Entrepreneurship — ownership or sale of a business. This wealth is typically concentrated, illiquid, and tied to a single company, so diversification, liquidity events, and succession/estate planning dominate.
	•	Employment and executive compensation — salary, bonus, and equity awards. Senior executives often hold concentrated employer stock and options, raising concentration-risk and tax-timing issues.
	•	Inheritance and family transfer — wealth received rather than created, which brings multi-generational, governance, and estate-tax considerations and often a stewardship mindset.
Client segments and the global distribution
Private clients are grouped by wealth level, with progressively more customized advice up the ladder:
Table 1 — Private-client segments
Segment
Typical needs
Mass market
Standardized products, basic planning, low cost
Mass affluent
Some customization, retirement and tax planning
High-net-worth (HNW)
Bespoke portfolios, tax/estate planning, alternatives access
Ultra-high-net-worth (UHNW)
Family-office services, philanthropy, complex estate/tax, direct deals
Global distribution. Wealth is highly concentrated — a small fraction of individuals holds a large share of total global wealth (a steep pyramid: a broad base of mass-market households and a narrow apex of HNW/UHNW individuals holding a disproportionate share). The distribution also varies markedly across regions and is shifting as emerging markets create new wealth.
The private wealth management process
Advising a private client follows a repeatable cycle, and the exam expects you to know its stages:
	•	Discovery — understand the client's goals, circumstances, risk tolerance, and constraints (a structured interview plus data gathering).
	•	IPS — document the return and risk objectives and the constraints (the deliverable in LOS 5).
	•	Implementation — set the strategic asset allocation, construct the portfolio tax-efficiently, and select vehicles.
	•	Monitoring and review — track progress toward goals, rebalance, and update the plan as the client's life and markets change.
Much of an adviser's value is not security selection but planning, behavioral coaching (keeping clients disciplined through market stress), and coordinating tax and estate matters — services that matter more for individuals than for institutions.
Exam focus: Know the segment ladder (mass market → mass affluent → HNW → UHNW) and — the more testable point — that the SOURCE of wealth (entrepreneurial vs employment vs inherited) drives concentration, liquidity, and the planning agenda. Entrepreneurial wealth = concentrated/illiquid; executive wealth = concentrated employer stock; inherited wealth = estate/governance focus. Also know the four-stage process: discovery → IPS → implementation → monitoring.
LOS 2 — Human capital, financial capital, and life stages
LOS: evaluate how changes in human capital, financial capital, and economic net worth across the financial stages of an individual’s life influence their financial decision making
What you must do:  Define the two forms of capital, value human capital, and explain how the balance shifts across life and what it implies for risk-taking.
The core identity. Human capital is the present value of an individual's expected future labor earnings; financial capital is their accumulated investable assets. Together, net of liabilities, they define economic net worth:
Economic net worth = human capital + financial capital − liabilities
Valuing human capital. Human capital is the present value of future after-tax earnings, discounted at a rate reflecting the riskiness of those earnings. For earnings growing at a constant rate, use the growing-annuity present value:
HC = [ earnings₁ / (r − g) ] × [ 1 − ((1+g)/(1+r))ⁿ ]
Worked example — present value of human capital
A 45-year-old earns 100,000 after tax, expects 3% annual growth (g), has 20 years to retirement (n), and discounts earnings at 6% (r).
HC = [100,000 / (0.06 − 0.03)] × [1 − (1.03/1.06)^20]
   = 3,333,333 × [1 − 0.9717^20]
   = 3,333,333 × [1 − 0.5630]
   = 3,333,333 × 0.4370 ≈ 1,456,000
So this person's human capital is roughly 1.46M — likely their largest asset early in life, and the thing life insurance exists to protect.
Worked example — the economic (total) balance sheet
A 45-year-old has: financial assets 400,000; human capital 1,456,000 (from above); a home worth 500,000; a mortgage of 300,000; and other debt of 50,000.
Total assets = 400,000 + 1,456,000 + 500,000 = 2,356,000
Total liabilities = 300,000 + 50,000 = 350,000
Economic net worth = 2,356,000 − 350,000 = 2,006,000
Unlike an accounting balance sheet, the ECONOMIC balance sheet includes human capital (and, in fuller versions, the present value of future goals), giving a truer picture of the client's capacity to bear risk — here, human capital is nearly three-quarters of gross assets.
The life-cycle shift
Human capital is largest early in life and declines as working years are consumed; financial capital accumulates to replace it. Because early-career individuals hold most of their wealth as human capital, they can bear more financial-portfolio risk; as retirement approaches and human capital runs out, the portfolio de-risks and shifts toward income.
Table 2 — Capital and risk across life stages
Stage
Human capital
Financial capital
Risk posture
Education / early career
Very high
Low
High risk capacity; build savings
Career development
High
Growing
Still high; peak earnings begin
Peak accumulation
Declining
High
Moderating; max financial assets
Pre-retirement
Low
High
De-risking; protect the nest egg
Retirement / spending
Minimal
Drawn down
Lower risk; income and longevity focus
Exam focus: The tested idea: as human capital declines with age, shift the financial portfolio toward lower risk and income. Be able to place a described client in a life stage and infer the appropriate risk posture, and to compute human capital with the growing-annuity formula.
LOS 3 — Objectives and constraints tied to human and financial capital
LOS: justify how returns, risks, objectives, and constraints for individuals relate to their human and financial capital
What you must do:  Connect the character of human capital to the portfolio tilt, quantify the insurance need it creates, and frame the return/risk objectives.
Bond-like vs equity-like human capital
The RISK character of human capital drives the financial-portfolio tilt, because the portfolio should balance total-wealth risk. Bond-like human capital — a stable, secure salary (e.g., a tenured professor) — behaves like a large bond holding, so the financial portfolio can hold MORE equity. Equity-like human capital — variable, cyclical, commission-based, or entrepreneurial income — already carries equity risk, so the financial portfolio should hold MORE bonds to compensate.
Drill: Two 40-year-olds each have 500,000 in financial assets and 2,000,000 of human capital. One is a tenured civil servant (stable salary); the other is a commission-based salesperson in a cyclical industry. Who should hold more equities in the financial portfolio, and why? (Answer: the civil servant — bond-like human capital lets the financial portfolio take more equity risk; the salesperson's equity-like human capital argues for more bonds to balance total-wealth risk.)
Total-wealth allocation and the glide path
The financial portfolio's asset mix follows from holding the TARGET allocation at the TOTAL-wealth level. If total wealth should be a given percentage in equity, and human capital is (mostly) bond-like, the financial portfolio must supply the equity that human capital does not:
w(equity in FC) = [ target equity% × total wealth − equity from HC ] / FC
Worked example — the glide path from total-wealth allocation
Target total-wealth equity = 30%. Human capital is bond-like (contributes zero equity).
Early career: HC = 700,000, FC = 300,000, total wealth = 1,000,000.
Target equity value = 0.30 × 1,000,000 = 300,000
Equity from HC = 0 → FC must supply 300,000
w(equity in FC) = 300,000 / 300,000 = 100%  → financial portfolio ~100% equity
Near retirement: HC has fallen to 300,000, FC grown to 700,000, total still 1,000,000.
Target equity value = 300,000; FC supplies it
w(equity in FC) = 300,000 / 700,000 = 43%  → portfolio glides toward bonds
Holding total-wealth risk constant therefore implies a financial portfolio that DE-RISKS automatically as human capital is spent — the theoretical basis for target-date/glide-path investing.
Exam focus: This is the quantitative core of the human-capital story: with bond-like human capital, the young investor's financial portfolio is heavily (even 100%) equity, gliding toward bonds as human capital shrinks. Be ready to compute the financial-portfolio equity weight from a total-wealth target.
Human-capital risks and the hedges
Human capital faces risks the investment portfolio cannot address, each with its own hedge:
	•	Premature death — destroys the family's future human capital. Hedged with life insurance, sized to the human capital at risk net of existing assets.
	•	Longevity (outliving assets) — hedged with annuities, which convert assets into guaranteed lifetime income.
	•	Earnings and health risk — hedged with disability and health insurance.
Worked example — life-insurance need (human-capital approach)
From the LOS 2 example, human capital is ≈ 1,456,000. The family already has 300,000 in liquid assets earmarked to support survivors and an existing 200,000 group life policy.
Human capital at risk        ≈ 1,456,000
Less existing assets/coverage  = 300,000 + 200,000 = 500,000
Additional life-insurance need ≈ 1,456,000 − 500,000 = 956,000
The family needs roughly 950,000 of additional coverage to replace the earner's human capital. As human capital declines with age, so does the insurance need.
Framing the objectives. The RETURN objective flows from the client's goals and spending needs; the RISK objective combines ABILITY to take risk (long horizon, ample wealth relative to needs, and stable/bond-like human capital raise ability) with WILLINGNESS (the client's stated attitude and behavior). Where ability and willingness conflict, the adviser documents both and generally follows the LOWER of the two while educating the client.
Exam focus: Two high-value pairings: bond-like vs equity-like human capital → opposite financial-portfolio tilt; and premature death → life insurance (sized to human capital net of assets), longevity → annuities. Be able to compute an insurance need. On risk tolerance, separate ABILITY (horizon, wealth, human-capital stability) from WILLINGNESS, and resolve conflicts toward the lower.
LOS 4 — Taxes and inflation
LOS: evaluate how various types of taxes imposed on individual investors and the impact of inflation influence investment decisions
What you must do:  Identify the taxes individuals face, compute after-tax and taxable-equivalent returns, and show how inflation (taxed on nominal gains) erodes real wealth.
Table 3 — Taxes individuals face
Tax type
Base
Planning implication
Income tax
Salary, interest, dividends
Often progressive; favors tax-deferred vehicles
Capital gains tax
Realized gains
Lower long-term rate rewards holding; harvest losses
Wealth tax
Net wealth held annually
Reduces compounding; location/structure matters
Property tax
Real estate value
Ongoing carrying cost of property
Estate / inheritance / gift
Transfers at death or by gift
Drives gifting, trusts, and bequest planning
Consumption / VAT
Spending
Affects after-tax spending power
After-tax and taxable-equivalent returns
Taxes reduce returns and reward tax-aware strategies — asset location (tax-inefficient assets in tax-deferred/exempt accounts, tax-efficient assets in taxable accounts), holding for long-term rates, loss harvesting, and using tax-advantaged accounts. To compare a tax-exempt bond (e.g., a municipal) with a taxable bond, use the taxable-equivalent yield:
Taxable-equivalent yield = tax-exempt yield / (1 − tax rate)
Worked example — taxable-equivalent yield and after-tax return
An investor in a 35% bracket compares a 3.0% municipal bond with a 4.2% taxable corporate bond.
Muni taxable-equivalent yield = 3.0% / (1 − 0.35) = 4.62%
Corporate after-tax yield     = 4.2% × (1 − 0.35) = 2.73%
The muni's 4.62% taxable-equivalent yield beats the corporate's 4.2% pre-tax (2.73% after tax), so the high-bracket investor prefers the municipal bond.
Tax-advantaged accounts and tax regimes
Tax regimes differ across countries — some tax all income at progressive rates, some favor long-term capital gains and dividends, some tax interest heavily, and some are comparatively light — and the regime shapes both asset location and the choice of vehicle. Tax-advantaged accounts (tax-deferred or tax-exempt) let returns compound without the annual tax drag, which is powerful over long horizons:
Worked example — the value of tax sheltering
10,000 invested for 20 years at a 7% return, with a 30% tax rate.
Taxable (return taxed each year): after-tax return = 7% × (1 − 0.30) = 4.9%
   FV = 10,000 × (1.049)^20 ≈ 26,030
Tax-exempt account (no tax on growth):
   FV = 10,000 × (1.07)^20 ≈ 38,697
Advantage of sheltering ≈ 38,697 − 26,030 = 12,667
Sheltering adds roughly 12,700 — about half again as much — over 20 years. This is why tax-inefficient, high-turnover assets belong in tax-sheltered accounts (asset location) and why deferral itself has value.
Inflation's double bite
Inflation erodes real purchasing power, so an individual's return objective should be stated in REAL terms. Worse, because capital gains and income are usually taxed on their NOMINAL amount, inflation raises the effective tax burden — the investor pays tax on inflationary "gains" that represent no real increase in wealth.
Worked example — inflation and the real after-tax return
A bond yields 6% nominal; the investor is taxed at 30% and inflation is 2.5%.
After-tax nominal return = 6% × (1 − 0.30) = 4.20%
Real after-tax return    = (1.042 / 1.025) − 1 = 1.66%
Of the 6% headline yield, only about 1.66% is real, after-tax wealth growth — tax is levied on the full nominal 6%, including the portion that merely compensates for inflation.
Exam focus: Know the tax menu and BOTH calculations: taxable-equivalent yield = exempt/(1−t) and after-tax = pre-tax×(1−t). State return objectives in real, after-tax terms, and be ready to explain the double bite — nominal gains are taxed, so inflation raises the effective tax rate and shrinks the real after-tax return (compute it as (1+after-tax nominal)/(1+inflation) − 1).
LOS 5 — Private vs institutional clients and the private-client IPS
LOS: discuss the differences between private and institutional clients and formulate an appropriate Investment Policy Statement for private clients
What you must do:  Contrast the two client types and build a private-client IPS — including the required-return calculation that anchors the essay.
Table 4 — Private vs institutional clients
Dimension
Private client
Institutional client
Time horizon
Finite, multi-stage (life cycle)
Often long or perpetual
Objectives
Personal, goals-based
Defined mandate / liabilities
Taxes
Usually taxable
Often tax-exempt
Governance
Individual / family, emotional
Committees, professional staff
Regulation
Lighter, varied
Heavy, specialized
Risk tolerance
Behavioral, personal
Policy-driven, less emotional
Size / uniqueness
Smaller, highly individual
Larger, more standardized
Components of a private-client IPS
	•	Return objective — tied to goals and spending; state in real, after-tax terms.
	•	Risk tolerance — ability (horizon, wealth vs needs, human-capital stability) AND willingness.
	•	Time horizon — often multi-stage (e.g., pre- and post-retirement).
	•	Liquidity — spending needs, emergency reserve, and near-term goals.
	•	Taxes — the client's bracket, jurisdiction, and account types.
	•	Legal / regulatory — trusts, estate plans, and jurisdiction.
	•	Unique circumstances — concentrated holdings, values/ESG constraints, family considerations.
Worked IPS — the required-return calculation
This is the calculation the morning session loves. Work the year-1 cash need, convert to a real return on the portfolio, then gross up for inflation (and tax if the need is stated after-tax).
Worked example — private-client required return
A couple, both 60, are retiring now. Investable portfolio: 2,000,000. After-tax spending need: 90,000 in year 1, growing with inflation of 2.5%. They receive a 20,000/year (after-tax) pension. Compute the required return.
Year-1 net withdrawal from portfolio = 90,000 − 20,000 = 70,000
Real after-tax return required = 70,000 / 2,000,000 = 3.50%
Nominal after-tax required return = (1.035)(1.025) − 1 = 6.09%
So the portfolio must earn about 6.1% nominal after tax. If pre-tax figures were required and the blended tax rate were, say, 20%, gross up: 6.09% / (1 − 0.20) ≈ 7.6% pre-tax (approximate).
Then assess risk and constraints (essay body). Ability to take risk: the 3.5% real withdrawal on a 2,000,000 portfolio is moderate and the horizon is long (two-stage, 25–30 years), supporting above-average ability — but the couple is retired with essentially no human capital and no ability to replace losses from labor income, which tempers it. Willingness comes from the vignette's stated attitude. Constraints: Time horizon (long, two-stage); Liquidity (the 70,000 draw plus an emergency reserve, e.g., a further year of spending); Taxes (their bracket and account mix); Legal (wills, trusts, estate plan); Unique (any bequest goal, concentrated positions, or ESG wishes).
Watch out: Sequencing and units trip candidates up. Compute the NET portfolio withdrawal first (spending minus pension/other income), divide by the CURRENT portfolio to get the REAL return, then multiply by (1 + inflation) — do not simply add inflation. Gross up for taxes only if the spending need is stated after-tax and a pre-tax return is required. Keep after-tax vs pre-tax straight throughout.
Exam focus: Constructed-response staple: given a client vignette, (1) calculate the required return — net withdrawal ÷ portfolio for the real rate, then compound with inflation, then gross up for tax if needed; (2) assess ability vs willingness; (3) list the constraints (Time horizon, Liquidity, Taxes, Legal, Unique). Emphasize the private-client differences: taxable, behavioral risk tolerance, and a finite, multi-stage horizon.
Estate and wealth-transfer basics
Transferring wealth efficiently across generations is a core private-wealth objective and a frequent source of the IPS's legal and unique constraints. The main tools and concepts:
	•	Lifetime gifting — using annual and lifetime exclusions to move assets — and their future growth — out of the taxable estate while the client is alive.
	•	Trusts — revocable trusts (flexible but still in the estate) versus irrevocable trusts (remove assets from the estate, add creditor protection); used to control timing, protect assets, and reduce estate tax.
	•	Bequests and the basis step-up — assets passed at death may receive a step-up in cost basis that eliminates the embedded gain, which interacts directly with the hold-versus-sell decision on low-basis holdings.
	•	Charitable vehicles — donor-advised funds, private foundations, and charitable remainder trusts for tax-efficient philanthropy.
The core trade-off is control versus tax efficiency: giving assets away (or locking them in an irrevocable trust) reduces estate tax but relinquishes control, so the plan must balance the family's tax savings against their desire to retain flexibility.
Exam focus: Know the transfer toolkit — lifetime gifting, revocable vs irrevocable trusts, the death-time basis step-up, and charitable vehicles — and the control-vs-tax-efficiency trade-off. The basis step-up is a favorite: it can make HOLDING a low-basis asset until death more tax-efficient than selling and diversifying during life, which must be weighed against the concentration risk.
Behavioral considerations for private clients
Individuals bring behavioral biases that institutions largely avoid, and the IPS should account for them. The common ones: loss aversion (holding losers, avoiding needed risk), overconfidence (over-trading and under-diversification), familiarity/home bias (concentration in employer or domestic stock), recency (chasing recent performance), and mental accounting. A useful distinction is between cognitive errors — correctable with information and education — and emotional biases, which are usually better accommodated than fought. Where a client's WILLINGNESS to take risk is driven by bias, the adviser documents it and either moderates the plan or coaches the client, using goals-based framing and a disciplined rebalancing policy to keep behavior in check.
Managing concentrated positions
Entrepreneurs and executives often hold a large, low-cost-basis position in a single stock — high idiosyncratic risk combined with a big embedded capital gain that makes an outright sale expensive. The main strategies to reduce the risk:
	•	Outright or staged sale — diversify directly; triggers capital gains tax, so stage the sale over several years to spread the tax hit.
	•	Hedging with derivatives — buy protective puts (a downside floor for a premium) or a collar / zero-cost collar (a floor funded by selling upside via a call), reducing downside risk without an immediate taxable sale.
	•	Monetization — borrow against the position or use a prepaid variable forward to raise cash while deferring the sale and its tax.
	•	Exchange (swap) funds — contribute the stock to a pooled fund of many concentrated holders to obtain a diversified position without a taxable sale.
	•	Charitable strategies — donate appreciated shares (a deduction while avoiding the gain) or fund a charitable remainder trust (CRT) to diversify tax-efficiently while retaining an income stream.
Watch out: Watch the tax-versus-risk trade-off: the embedded gain makes clients reluctant to sell, but deferring tax should not override prudent risk reduction. When a client resists selling, hedging or monetization can cut the risk while deferring the tax — a practical middle ground the exam likes.
Exam focus: Know the concentrated-position toolkit and its tax angle: staged sale (spreads tax), collar/protective put (hedges without selling), prepaid variable forward/borrowing (monetizes and defers), exchange fund (diversifies without a sale), and charitable gift/CRT (diversifies while avoiding the gain). This is a recurring private-wealth essay topic.
Goals-based investing
Private clients think in terms of specific goals — retirement income, a child's education, a bequest, a second home — not a single portfolio risk number. Goals-based investing assigns a separate sub-portfolio to each goal, with risk and time horizon matched to that goal, using mental accounting CONSTRUCTIVELY rather than fighting it. High-priority, near-term goals (essential spending) are funded with low-risk assets; lower-priority, long-horizon or aspirational goals can bear more risk. The adviser still reviews aggregate risk to ensure the combined portfolio is coherent.
Capital sufficiency analysis
Before finalizing the plan, test whether it is actually likely to fund the goals. A deterministic projection grows assets at a single assumed return, but a Monte Carlo simulation is better: it models the VARIABILITY of returns and the sequence in which they occur, producing a probability of meeting the goals. Retired clients are especially exposed to sequence-of-returns risk — poor returns in the early withdrawal years can permanently impair the portfolio even if the average return is fine — which a Monte Carlo analysis surfaces and a single-return projection hides.
Exam focus: Goals-based investing uses mental accounting constructively — a sub-portfolio per goal, risk matched to each goal's priority and horizon. Capital sufficiency is best assessed with Monte Carlo (not a single-return projection) because it captures return variability and sequence-of-returns risk, which is acute for retirees drawing down assets.
Retirement decumulation
Turning a portfolio into lifetime income raises issues distinct from accumulation, and each has a standard mitigant:
	•	Safe withdrawal rate — a sustainable initial withdrawal (a rough historical anchor is around 4% of the starting portfolio, inflation-adjusted thereafter), which must be adjusted for horizon, market valuations, and the client's spending flexibility — it is a guideline, not a guarantee.
	•	Sequence-of-returns risk — poor returns early in retirement are far more damaging than the same returns later, because withdrawals lock in losses; mitigate by holding a cash/bond buffer and by trimming withdrawals after down years.
	•	Bucketing (time segmentation) — hold near-term spending in cash and short bonds and later spending in growth assets, so the retiree is not forced to sell equities in a downturn.
	•	Floor-and-upside — cover essential spending with a secure floor (annuities, a bond ladder, or inflation-linked bonds) and pursue growth with the remainder for discretionary goals.
Exam focus: Decumulation is tested through sequence-of-returns risk: the fix is a cash/bond buffer or bucketing so the retiree doesn't sell growth assets in a downturn, plus a floor-and-upside split (secure the essentials with annuities/bond ladders, invest the rest for growth). A safe withdrawal rate (~4% anchor) is a guideline, not a promise.
Quick reference — key formulas
The formulas worth memorizing
Economic net worth = human capital + financial capital − liabilities
Human capital (growing annuity):
   HC = [earnings₁/(r−g)] × [1 − ((1+g)/(1+r))ⁿ]
Life-insurance need ≈ human capital − existing assets/coverage
Taxable-equivalent yield = exempt yield / (1 − t)
After-tax return = pre-tax × (1 − t)
Real after-tax return = (1 + after-tax nominal)/(1 + inflation) − 1
Required return: net withdrawal/portfolio = real; ×(1+infl) = nominal;
   ÷(1−t) if grossing up to pre-tax
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. Write economic net worth in terms of capital.
   -> Human capital + financial capital − liabilities.
2. Earnings₁ 100,000, g 3%, r 6%, n 20. Human capital?
   -> 3,333,333 × [1 − (1.03/1.06)^20] ≈ 3,333,333 × 0.437 ≈ 1.46M.
3. As human capital declines with age, how should the financial portfolio change?
   -> De-risk — shift toward lower risk and income.
4. Bond-like vs equity-like human capital — which argues for more equities in the portfolio?
   -> Bond-like (stable salary) — it lets the financial portfolio hold more equity.
5. Which hedges premature death, and which hedges longevity?
   -> Life insurance hedges premature death; annuities hedge longevity.
6. HC 1,456,000; existing assets/coverage 500,000. Additional life-insurance need?
   -> ≈ 956,000.
7. 35% bracket: taxable-equivalent yield of a 3% muni?
   -> 3% / (1 − 0.35) = 4.62%.
8. Why does inflation raise the effective tax burden?
   -> Gains are taxed on their nominal amount, so you pay tax on inflationary 'gains' that aren't real.
9. Spending 90,000, pension 20,000, portfolio 2,000,000, inflation 2.5%. Nominal after-tax required return?
   -> Net 70,000/2,000,000 = 3.5% real; (1.035)(1.025) − 1 = 6.09%.
10. List the private-client IPS constraints.
   -> Time horizon, Liquidity, Taxes, Legal/regulatory, Unique circumstances.

readings.title: Portfolio Management for Institutional Investors
readings.orientation: Orientation. A broad, content-heavy reading — eight unique learning outcomes (the source duplicated the first) spanning every major institution. It rewards structured memorization and applied judgment more than a single formula, but there is genuine math here: funded status and funded ratio for pensions, spending rules for endowments and foundations, and the duration-gap / surplus arithmetic for banks and insurers — all worked below. The two highest-yield areas for the exam are the institution comparison grid (liabilities, horizon, liquidity, risk, and constraints by type) and the five risk considerations of a private defined-benefit plan, which appear repeatedly as constructed-response prompts. Master the grid, the DB risk factors (with a worked risk-capacity assessment), the spending-rule calculations, and asset-liability management for banks and insurers.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Portfolio Construction
Reading: Portfolio Management for Institutional Investors
Orientation. A broad, content-heavy reading — eight unique learning outcomes (the source duplicated the first) spanning every major institution. It rewards structured memorization and applied judgment more than a single formula, but there is genuine math here: funded status and funded ratio for pensions, spending rules for endowments and foundations, and the duration-gap / surplus arithmetic for banks and insurers — all worked below. The two highest-yield areas for the exam are the institution comparison grid (liabilities, horizon, liquidity, risk, and constraints by type) and the five risk considerations of a private defined-benefit plan, which appear repeatedly as constructed-response prompts. Master the grid, the DB risk factors (with a worked risk-capacity assessment), the spending-rule calculations, and asset-liability management for banks and insurers.

LOS 1 — Common characteristics of institutional investors
LOS: discuss common characteristics of institutional investors as a group
What you must do:  List what institutional investors tend to share, and why the asset-liability framework unifies them.
Despite their variety, institutional investors share a recognizable set of features:
	•	Scale — large asset pools that grant access to alternatives, negotiating power on fees, and the ability to build internal expertise.
	•	Long time horizons (for many) — pensions, endowments, foundations, and some sovereign funds invest over decades or in perpetuity, giving high risk capacity and the ability to harvest illiquidity premia.
	•	Governance structures — boards, investment committees, and professional staff operating under a formal, written IPS.
	•	Defined objectives and often explicit liabilities — the portfolio serves a mandate or funds a liability, not personal preference.
	•	Regulatory oversight and (often) tax-advantaged status — most are exempt or lightly taxed, though insurers are notably taxed on investment income.
	•	An agency relationship — decisions are made by agents (staff, trustees) on behalf of beneficiaries, raising governance and accountability considerations.
The unifying theme is the asset-liability framework: almost every institutional decision — how much risk to take, how much liquidity to hold, how to set duration — flows from the nature of the institution's liabilities or spending needs. Reading the liabilities is the key to reading the portfolio.
Exam focus: A quick list, but frame it around liabilities: the differences that matter across institution types (the next LOS) all trace back to the character of each one's liabilities/spending. Note the tax exception — insurers are taxed while most others are exempt.
LOS 1b — Governance of institutional portfolios
Institutional investing runs on a governance structure, and its quality materially affects results. The typical roles: the board or trustees set the mission and approve the IPS and strategic asset allocation, then delegate; an investment committee oversees strategy, risk, and manager selection; internal staff or a CIO implement day-to-day decisions; and external consultants and managers advise or execute. A strong governance model features clear accountability, appropriate investment expertise on the committee, a genuinely long-term orientation insulated from short-term political or career pressure, and disciplined periodic review.
Weak governance — committees lacking expertise, short-termism, or unclear accountability — leads to procyclical mistakes (selling low in a panic, chasing last year's winners) that erode returns. For this reason, good governance is itself a source of long-run value, not merely an administrative wrapper. The investment committee should focus on the decisions that matter most (the strategic allocation and risk policy) rather than micromanaging individual holdings.
Exam focus: Know the governance roles (board sets policy and delegates → investment committee oversees → staff implements → consultants/managers advise/execute) and that strong governance — expertise, long-term orientation, clear accountability, regular review — is itself a driver of returns because it prevents procyclical errors.
LOS 2 & 3 — Investment policy and the institution grid
LOS: discuss investment policy of institutional investors
LOS: discuss the stakeholders, liabilities, investment time horizons, and liquidity needs of different types of institutional investors
What you must do:  Compare the major institution types across liabilities, horizon, and liquidity — the core grid — and know the SWF sub-types and endowment/foundation spending mechanics.
Table 1 — Institutional investor profiles
Institution
Liabilities / purpose
Horizon
Liquidity need
Risk / notes
DB pension
Pension benefits owed to members
Depends on plan maturity
Moderate (benefit payments)
LDI; funded status drives risk
DC pension
Individual participant accounts
Participant's own
Participant-driven
Member directs; sponsor sets menu
Sovereign wealth fund
Depends on type (see below)
Often very long
Varies by type
Stabilization needs liquidity; savings does not
Endowment
Support the institution's budget (perpetual)
Very long / perpetual
Moderate (annual spending)
~5% spending; heavy alternatives
Foundation
Grants / mission (often perpetual)
Long / perpetual
~5% payout (US private)
Similar to endowment
Bank
Depositor and funding liabilities
Short
High
ALM; invests reserves; regulatory capital
Insurer (life)
Long-dated policy liabilities
Long
Moderate
Duration matching; tax matters
Insurer (P&C)
Short, lumpy claim liabilities
Shorter
High
Liquidity for claims; more equity capacity
Sovereign wealth fund types
The label "sovereign wealth fund" spans five distinct mandates, each with its own horizon and liquidity profile:
	•	Stabilization funds — buffer government budgets against commodity-price or fiscal volatility; SHORT horizon, HIGH liquidity, conservative.
	•	Savings funds — transfer wealth across generations; VERY LONG horizon, LOW liquidity, high risk capacity.
	•	Reserve investment funds — earn a higher return on excess FX reserves.
	•	Development funds — finance domestic economic and infrastructure projects.
	•	Pension reserve funds — pre-fund future public pension obligations; long horizon.
Endowment and foundation spending rules
Endowments and foundations must spend enough to fund the mission (US private foundations face a ~5% minimum payout) while preserving real value in perpetuity. To keep spending stable and avoid procyclical cuts, they smooth it rather than applying a fixed rate to a volatile market value. Two common rules:
Rolling-average rule:   Spending = rate × (N-year average market value)
Geometric (smoothing) rule:
  Spending_t = w × [Spending_(t−1) × (1+infl)] + (1−w) × [rate × MV]
Worked example — two spending rules
Rolling 3-year average (rate 5%): market values of 900M, 1,000M, 1,100M.
Average MV = (900 + 1,000 + 1,100)/3 = 1,000M
Spending = 0.05 × 1,000 = 50.0M
Geometric smoothing (w = 0.70, inflation 2%, rate 5%): prior spending 48M, current MV 1,050M.
Smoothing term = 0.70 × (48 × 1.02)      = 0.70 × 48.96 = 34.27M
Market term    = 0.30 × (0.05 × 1,050)    = 0.30 × 52.5  = 15.75M
Spending_t     = 34.27 + 15.75           = 50.0M
Both smooth spending against market swings: the geometric rule blends last year's inflation-adjusted spending with a market-based target, dampening the volatility that applying 5% directly to the current value would create.
Defined-benefit vs defined-contribution pensions
Defined benefit (DB). The sponsor promises a specified benefit and therefore bears the investment and longevity risk; the plan exists to fund that liability. DB plans are managed with liability-driven investing (LDI) and typically follow a de-risking glide path — shifting from return-seeking assets toward liability-hedging bonds as funded status improves. Open (actively accruing) plans have longer-duration liabilities than closed or frozen plans, which are winding down.
Defined contribution (DC). The participant bears the investment risk and directs contributions among a menu the sponsor selects. The key issues are the design of the default option, target-date funds (which glide automatically from equities toward bonds as the participant ages — the human-capital logic in product form), fees, and participant education. The sponsor's fiduciary duty runs to the quality of the menu and process, not to investment outcomes.
Endowments and foundations
Both are typically perpetual and tilt heavily toward equities and alternatives (the "endowment model") to fund ongoing spending while preserving real value. The central tension is intergenerational equity: spending enough to serve today's mission versus preserving purchasing power for the future. Foundations come in types — private/independent, community, and operating — and some are deliberately spend-down (finite life). Because the portfolio must fund spending AND keep pace with inflation AND cover costs, the required return is:
Required return ≈ spending rate + expected inflation + costs   (additive)
             = (1+spend)(1+infl)(1+cost) − 1               (compounded)
Worked example — endowment required return
Spending rate 5.0%, expected inflation 2.5%, investment/operating costs 0.4%.
Additive     ≈ 5.0 + 2.5 + 0.4 = 7.9%
Compounded   = (1.05)(1.025)(1.004) − 1 = 8.06%
The endowment must earn roughly 8% just to sustain spending and preserve real value net of costs — a demanding target that explains the heavy equity/alternatives allocation and the long horizon needed to bear that risk.
Exam focus: The institution grid is the single highest-yield content in the reading — memorize each type's horizon and liquidity profile. Know the five SWF sub-types (stabilization = short/liquid; savings = long/illiquid), that P&C insurers have shorter, lumpier liabilities than life insurers (more liquidity, more equity capacity), and be able to COMPUTE spending from a rolling-average or geometric smoothing rule AND the endowment required return (spending + inflation + costs).
LOS 4 — Legal, regulatory, and tax constraints
LOS: describe the focus of legal, regulatory, and tax constraints affecting different types of institutional investors
What you must do:  Match the dominant legal/regulatory/tax constraint to each institution.
Table 2 — Dominant constraints by type
Institution
Legal / regulatory focus
Tax
DB pension
Funding rules, fiduciary/prudent-investor duty (ERISA-type)
Usually tax-exempt
Endowment / foundation
Prudence (UPMIFA); minimum spending (~5% for US private foundations)
Largely tax-exempt
Bank
Risk-based capital, liquidity coverage, asset eligibility
Taxable
Insurer
Risk-based capital, reserve rules, asset restrictions
Taxed on investment income
Sovereign wealth fund
Political and legal constraints; transparency (Santiago Principles)
Generally exempt
Exam focus: Tie the constraint to the type: pension → funding/fiduciary; foundation → spending rule + prudence; bank/insurer → capital and liquidity regulation (and insurers are taxed). Most institutions are tax-exempt; insurers are the key taxable exception.
LOS 4b — Setting institutional return and risk objectives
Return objectives flow from the liability or spending need. A DB pension's return objective is tied to the actuarial discount rate — the return needed to fund promised benefits at an acceptable contribution level. An endowment's or foundation's is the spending + inflation + costs figure computed earlier. A bank's or insurer's is set relative to the cost of its liabilities plus a required spread. In every case, the liability sets the target, not an abstract preference.
Risk objectives flow from the ability to bear loss: for a pension, the funded surplus and sponsor strength; for an endowment, spending flexibility and the long horizon; for a bank or insurer, the size of surplus (capital). Ability to take risk rises with the cushion between assets and liabilities.
Worked example — DB de-risking glide path
A DB plan sets its return-seeking allocation by funded status:
Funded 80%  → 70% return-seeking / 30% liability-hedging
Funded 90%  → 50% return-seeking / 50% liability-hedging
Funded 100% → 30% return-seeking / 70% liability-hedging
Funded 110% → 10% return-seeking / 90% liability-hedging
As a market rally lifts the plan from 80% to 100% funded, it de-risks from 70% to 30% return-seeking — locking in the improvement and cutting the chance of falling back into deficit. This is the LDI glide path in action.
Worked example — DC target-date glide path
A target-date fund glides its equity weight down with the participant's age:
Age 25 → ~90% equities   (human capital high; long horizon)
Age 45 → ~70% equities
Age 60 → ~50% equities
Age 70 → ~30% equities   (human capital largely spent)
The glide reflects declining human capital — exactly the private-wealth logic, delivered as an off-the-shelf DC default option.
Exam focus: Institutional return objectives derive from the liability/spending need (pension → discount rate; endowment → spending + inflation + costs); risk objectives derive from the cushion (surplus, horizon, capital). Know the DB de-risking glide path (de-risk as funded status improves) and that DC target-date funds glide down in equity with age on the human-capital logic.
Measuring risk relative to liabilities
For liability-driven institutions, asset volatility alone understates the real risk — what matters is the volatility of the SURPLUS (assets minus liabilities) or of the funded ratio. Two assets with identical standalone volatility can differ sharply in their contribution to risk depending on how they correlate with the liabilities: long-duration bonds may look "risky" in isolation yet REDUCE surplus risk, because their value moves with the rate-sensitive liabilities they are meant to hedge. Institutions therefore measure liability-relative risk — surplus-at-risk, or shortfall risk (the probability the funded ratio falls below a threshold over a horizon) — rather than asset value-at-risk alone. This is precisely why an LDI portfolio holds long bonds that would look imprudent for a total-return investor: relative to the liabilities, they are the low-risk asset.
Exam focus: The key idea: for a liability-driven investor, manage the volatility of the SURPLUS/funded ratio, not asset volatility. An asset's risk depends on its correlation with the liabilities — long bonds reduce a pension's surplus risk even though they look volatile on their own. Know surplus-at-risk / shortfall risk as the liability-relative risk measures.
LOS 5 — Risk considerations of private DB pension plans
LOS: evaluate risk considerations of private defined benefit (DB) pension plans in relation to plan funded status, sponsor financial strength, sponsor–fund interactions, plan design, and workforce characteristics
What you must do:  Assess a DB plan's risk CAPACITY using the five factors — the most frequent essay prompt in the reading — and compute funded status.
Funded status
Surplus = plan assets − PV(liabilities)
Funded ratio = plan assets / PV(liabilities)
Worked example — funded status and risk capacity
Plan A: assets 1,150M, PV of liabilities 1,000M. Plan B: assets 800M, PV of liabilities 1,000M.
Plan A: funded ratio = 1,150/1,000 = 115% ; surplus = +150M  (overfunded)
Plan B: funded ratio = 800/1,000 = 80%  ; deficit  = −200M  (underfunded)
Plan A's surplus is a cushion that supports MORE risk-taking; Plan B's deficit reduces risk capacity and raises the priority of hedging the liabilities (LDI).
The five risk considerations
	•	Funded status — a surplus allows more risk; a deficit reduces risk capacity and raises the priority of liability hedging.
	•	Sponsor financial strength — a strong, profitable sponsor that can make contributions supports more plan risk; a weak sponsor cannot backstop losses, so the plan should take less.
	•	Sponsor–fund interactions (correlation) — avoid plan assets correlated with the sponsor's business, because both would fall together, hitting funded status and the sponsor's ability to contribute at once.
	•	Plan design — open vs frozen, active vs retired members, and features like lump-sum options change the liability's duration and liquidity needs.
	•	Workforce characteristics — a younger, active workforce means longer-duration liabilities and more risk capacity; a mature plan (mostly retirees) needs shorter duration and more liquidity.
Worked example — assessing risk capacity from plan facts
Plan A: funded ratio 115%; sponsor is a highly profitable firm in a stable industry; workforce young and mostly active; plan open.
→ HIGH risk capacity: surplus cushion, strong sponsor able to contribute, long-duration liabilities, ongoing plan. Can carry more equity/return-seeking risk.
Plan B: funded ratio 80%; sponsor a struggling cyclical manufacturer; workforce mostly retirees; plan frozen.
→ LOW risk capacity: deficit, weak sponsor that cannot backstop losses, short-duration liabilities with high near-term liquidity needs, frozen plan. Should de-risk, hedge liabilities (LDI), and hold more liquidity — and specifically AVOID cyclical/manufacturing equities correlated with the sponsor.
LDI and the de-risking glide path
A DB plan reduces the risk to its funded status by hedging the interest-rate sensitivity of its liabilities with long-duration bonds and derivatives (interest-rate swaps, bond futures) — liability-driven investing. The hedge ratio expresses how much of the liability's rate risk is hedged; a fully hedged plan neutralizes rate moves' effect on the surplus. As funded status improves, plans typically follow a de-risking glide path: they progressively shift out of return-seeking assets and into liability-hedging assets, locking in gains and reducing the chance of sliding back into deficit. A poorly funded plan with a strong sponsor may instead keep more return-seeking risk to try to close the gap — the choice depends on the five factors above.
Watch out: The correlation point is the classic trap: a plan should NOT hold assets tied to the sponsor's own industry, because a downturn would hit plan assets and the sponsor's ability to contribute simultaneously — a double blow to funded status. A tech sponsor overweighting tech, or a bank sponsor holding financials, is the error to flag.
Exam focus: Very high-yield essay content. For a described plan, judge risk capacity using ALL five factors and compute the funded ratio/surplus. Strong sponsor + surplus + young/active workforce + uncorrelated assets = more risk; the reverse = less risk, more LDI hedging, and more liquidity. Always check for sponsor-correlated holdings.
LOS 6 & 7 — Evaluating institutional IPSs and portfolios
LOS: evaluate the investment policy statement of an institutional investor
LOS: evaluate the investment portfolio of a private DB plan, sovereign wealth fund, university endowment, and private foundation
What you must do:  Critique an institution's IPS and portfolio against its liabilities, horizon, and constraints — and flag mismatches.
Evaluating the IPS. Check internal consistency: do the return objective, risk tolerance, and constraints (horizon, liquidity, legal/tax, unique) fit the institution's liabilities and purpose? A perpetual endowment with a long horizon but an ultra-conservative, all-bond policy is inconsistent; so is an underfunded plan with a weak sponsor pursuing an aggressive equity policy.
Evaluating the portfolio. Does the asset allocation match the stated risk capacity, hedge the liabilities appropriately (duration for a pension), hold enough liquidity for spending/claims, and avoid inappropriate concentrations?
	•	DB plan — is duration matched to the liabilities; is risk consistent with funded status and sponsor strength; are sponsor-correlated assets avoided?
	•	Endowment / foundation — does the long horizon justify the equity/alternatives tilt while retaining enough liquidity for the ~5% spending and any capital calls?
	•	Sovereign wealth fund — does the allocation match the fund TYPE's horizon and liquidity (a stabilization fund should be liquid and conservative; a savings fund can be illiquid and aggressive)?
Drill: An underfunded corporate DB plan (funded ratio 80%) with a financially weak, cyclical sponsor holds 80% equities, heavily weighted to its own industry, and little liquidity. Identify the mismatches. (Answer: risk far exceeds capacity given the deficit and weak sponsor; the liabilities are unhedged — no LDI/duration match; the portfolio is dangerously correlated with the sponsor; and liquidity is inadequate for benefit payments. De-risk, hedge liabilities, remove sponsor-correlated holdings, and raise liquidity.)
Worked example — evaluating an endowment's policy and portfolio
A university endowment (perpetual, 5% spending rule, 2,000M) holds 45% public equity, 40% alternatives (much of it illiquid private equity and real assets), 10% bonds, 5% cash. The university relies on the endowment for 30% of its operating budget, and recent private-market commitments were increased sharply.
Return/horizon — consistent: the perpetual horizon and the ~8% required return (5% spending + inflation + costs) justify the heavy equity/alternatives tilt.
Liquidity — the problem: only ~15% is liquid (bonds + cash) against a 5% annual spending draw PLUS ongoing capital calls into the 40% alternatives. A market drawdown would trigger the denominator effect (the illiquid share rising as the total shrinks) and could force spending cuts that hit 30% of the university's budget.
Recommendations: raise the liquidity buffer, slow and pace new private commitments, arrange a committed credit line, and stress-test spending and calls under a severe market decline.
Exam focus: Constructed-response: given an IPS/portfolio, flag inconsistencies — an underfunded plan with a weak sponsor taking equity risk, an endowment with too little liquidity for its spending rule and capital calls, a stabilization SWF holding illiquid assets, or a plan concentrated in sponsor-correlated securities. Structure the answer as: is the return/risk consistent with the horizon and liabilities, is liquidity adequate, and are there inappropriate concentrations — then recommend specific fixes.
LOS 8 — Balance-sheet management of banks and insurers
LOS: describe considerations affecting the balance sheet management of banks and insurers
What you must do:  Explain asset-liability management (ALM) for banks and insurers, and compute the effect of a rate change on surplus via the duration gap.
Banks manage a portfolio of reserves and securities against depositor and funding liabilities. Priorities: control the DURATION GAP between assets and liabilities to manage interest-rate risk, maintain liquidity (liquidity-coverage rules), meet regulatory capital, and manage credit risk — all over a relatively short horizon.
Insurers match assets to policy liabilities. Life insurers have long-dated, fairly predictable liabilities and emphasize duration matching and tax efficiency; property & casualty insurers have shorter, lumpier, less predictable claim liabilities, so they hold more liquidity and can carry more equity/risk capital. Both face risk-based capital and reserve regulation.
The duration gap and surplus
The sensitivity of an institution's equity (surplus) to rates depends on the mismatch between the duration of its assets and of its liabilities:
Surplus = assets − liabilities
Leverage-adjusted duration gap = D_A − (L/A) × D_L
Δ Surplus ≈ − [ D_A × A − D_L × L ] × Δy
Worked example — duration gap and a rate rise
A bank has assets of 1,000M with duration D_A = 5, and liabilities of 900M with duration D_L = 3 (equity = 100M).
Leverage-adjusted duration gap = 5 − (900/1,000)×3 = 5 − 2.7 = 2.3
Rates rise 100 bps (Δy = +0.01):
Δ Surplus ≈ −[5×1,000 − 3×900] × 0.01 = −[5,000 − 2,700] × 0.01 = −23M
Equity falls from 100M to 77M — a 23% hit from a 1% rate move, because assets are longer-duration than liabilities. To IMMUNIZE surplus, set D_A × A = D_L × L, i.e., shorten asset duration to D_A = (900/1,000)×3 = 2.7.
Watch out: A positive duration gap (assets longer than liabilities, leverage-adjusted) means rising rates REDUCE surplus; a negative gap means falling rates hurt. Banks generally aim to keep the gap near zero, or position it deliberately for a rate view — but a large gap plus high leverage can wipe out a big share of equity on a modest rate move, as the example shows.
Banks as investors — in depth
A bank's securities portfolio provides liquidity and income around its core lending book. The bank earns a net interest margin by funding longer-duration assets with shorter-duration deposits — profitable, but the source of its duration-gap risk and of disintermediation risk (deposit flight when rates rise). Its ALM priorities are the liquidity-coverage ratio (holding enough high-quality liquid assets to survive a stress period), regulatory capital adequacy, the credit quality of the securities book, and steering the duration gap toward the desired position. How securities are classified (for example, held-to-maturity versus available-for-sale) affects whether unrealized gains and losses flow through to capital.
Insurers as investors — in depth
Life insurers hold long-dated, fairly predictable liabilities (whole-life policies, annuities) and match them with long-duration, high-quality fixed income via duration and cash-flow matching. Their key risks are disintermediation (policyholders surrendering and withdrawing when rates rise, forcing asset sales at a loss), credit risk, and reinvestment risk; investment income is taxed. Because the liabilities are long and predictable, liquidity needs are moderate and the emphasis is on close asset-liability matching.
Property & casualty (P&C) insurers face short, lumpy, uncertain claim liabilities (catastrophes, litigation), so they hold more liquidity and shorter-duration assets. Because their liabilities are shorter and their surplus (capital) is larger relative to reserves, they can carry MORE equity and risk capital than life insurers — and their results swing with the underwriting cycle.
Illustration — surplus and equity capacity
A P&C insurer: assets 1,200M, reserves 800M → surplus 400M (33% of assets).
A life insurer: assets 1,050M, reserves 1,000M → surplus 50M (5% of assets).
The P&C insurer's far larger surplus cushion absorbs more loss and supports a higher equity allocation; the thinly capitalized life insurer must stay conservative and closely duration-match. For both, surplus (assets − liabilities) is what regulators watch, and managing the VOLATILITY of surplus — through the duration gap and the riskiness of assets — is the core ALM task.
Exam focus: Know the ALM framing and the calculation: leverage-adjusted duration gap = D_A − (L/A)D_L, and Δsurplus ≈ −[D_A·A − D_L·L]·Δy. Immunize surplus by matching money-durations (D_A·A = D_L·L). Life (long, predictable → duration match, tax-aware, disintermediation risk) vs P&C (short, lumpy → liquidity, larger surplus, more equity capacity). Surplus absorbs losses, so a bigger surplus supports more investment risk.
Quick reference — key formulas
The formulas worth memorizing
Funded ratio = plan assets / PV(liabilities)
Surplus = assets − PV(liabilities)
Spending (rolling avg) = rate × (N-year average MV)
Spending (geometric) = w[Spend_(t−1)(1+infl)] + (1−w)[rate × MV]
Leverage-adjusted duration gap = D_A − (L/A)·D_L
Δ Surplus ≈ −[D_A·A − D_L·L]·Δy   (immunize: D_A·A = D_L·L)
Five DB risks: funded status, sponsor strength, sponsor correlation,
   plan design, workforce characteristics
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. Which SWF type is short-horizon/high-liquidity, and which is very-long/illiquid?
   -> Stabilization fund (short, liquid); savings fund (very long, illiquid).
2. Name the five DB pension risk considerations.
   -> Funded status, sponsor financial strength, sponsor–fund correlation, plan design, workforce characteristics.
3. Assets 1,150M, PV liabilities 1,000M. Funded ratio and surplus?
   -> 115%; surplus +150M (overfunded → more risk capacity).
4. Why must a plan avoid assets correlated with the sponsor's business?
   -> A downturn hits plan assets and the sponsor's ability to contribute at the same time — a double blow to funded status.
5. A young, active workforce implies what for liability duration and risk capacity?
   -> Longer-duration liabilities and more risk capacity.
6. Rolling 3-year average, 5% rate, MVs 900/1,000/1,100. Spending?
   -> 0.05 × 1,000 = 50M.
7. Life vs P&C insurer — how do liabilities and liquidity differ?
   -> Life: long, predictable → duration matching. P&C: short, lumpy → more liquidity and equity capacity.
8. Bank: D_A 5, A 1,000M, D_L 3, L 900M. Leverage-adjusted duration gap?
   -> 5 − 0.9×3 = 2.3.
9. Same bank, rates +100bps. Change in surplus?
   -> −[5,000 − 2,700]×0.01 = −23M (equity 100M → 77M).
10. How does the bank immunize its surplus against rate moves?
   -> Match money-durations: set D_A·A = D_L·L (here, shorten asset duration to 2.7).

readings.title: Trading Costs and Electronic Markets
readings.orientation: Orientation. This reading is unusually calculation-friendly — three cost measures (the effective spread, VWAP, and implementation shortfall) that you must be able to compute, each worked in full below, including the delay/impact/opportunity decomposition of implementation shortfall that the exam loves. Around the math sits the electronic-markets landscape: the explicit/implicit cost taxonomy, market fragmentation, the types of electronic traders, the uses of trading systems, the advantages of low-latency traders, the systemic risks with their regulatory fixes, and the abusive practices surveillance is designed to catch. Ten unique learning outcomes (the source duplicated the first). Nail the three cost calculations — implementation shortfall above all — and know why each benchmark can mislead.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Portfolio Construction
Reading: Trading Costs and Electronic Markets
Orientation. This reading is unusually calculation-friendly — three cost measures (the effective spread, VWAP, and implementation shortfall) that you must be able to compute, each worked in full below, including the delay/impact/opportunity decomposition of implementation shortfall that the exam loves. Around the math sits the electronic-markets landscape: the explicit/implicit cost taxonomy, market fragmentation, the types of electronic traders, the uses of trading systems, the advantages of low-latency traders, the systemic risks with their regulatory fixes, and the abusive practices surveillance is designed to catch. Ten unique learning outcomes (the source duplicated the first). Nail the three cost calculations — implementation shortfall above all — and know why each benchmark can mislead.

LOS 1 — Components of execution costs
LOS: explain the components of execution costs, including explicit and implicit costs
What you must do:  Separate explicit from implicit costs and explain the central impact-vs-timing trade-off.
Total execution cost is the difference between what a trade actually achieves and a frictionless benchmark. It splits into two families:
Table 1 — Execution cost components
Explicit (directly observable)
Implicit (must be estimated)
Commissions and broker fees
Bid-ask spread (the cost of demanding liquidity)
Taxes / stamp duty
Market impact (your own trade moves the price)
Exchange and clearing fees
Delay (slippage) — price drift before execution
—
Opportunity cost — return missed on the unfilled portion
Explicit vs implicit. Explicit costs are invoiced and easy to see; implicit costs are larger and harder to measure, requiring a benchmark price for comparison. For institutional-sized orders, market impact and opportunity cost — not commissions — dominate the total.
The central trade-off. Trading FAST reduces delay and opportunity cost (you get filled before the price moves away) but RAISES market impact (aggressive orders push the price). Trading SLOWLY reduces market impact but raises the risk that the price moves away before you finish (higher delay/opportunity cost). Every execution strategy is a point on this impact-versus-timing curve, and implementation shortfall is the metric that captures the whole trade-off in one number.
Drill: A manager must buy a large, illiquid position and has strong short-term conviction the price will rise today. Should the order be traded aggressively or patiently? (Answer: aggressively — with high alpha decay and rising prices, the opportunity/delay cost of waiting outweighs the extra market impact of trading fast.)
What drives the bid-ask spread
The spread a liquidity provider charges compensates for three distinct costs, and knowing them explains why spreads vary across securities and conditions:
	•	Order-processing costs — the operational cost of quoting and executing (technology, clearing, staffing).
	•	Inventory (holding) costs — the risk and financing cost of holding an unwanted position after filling a trade, until it can be offset.
	•	Adverse-selection costs — the risk of trading against a better-informed counterparty; the market maker loses when the informed side is right.
Adverse selection is the largest and most subtle component: liquidity providers widen spreads when informed trading is more likely, so spreads are wider for less-liquid, more-volatile, and news-sensitive names, and around information events.
Exam focus: Know the explicit/implicit split and the impact-versus-timing trade-off: fast trading lowers opportunity/delay cost but raises market impact, and vice versa. For large orders, implicit costs (impact, opportunity) dominate commissions. Know the three drivers of the spread — order-processing, inventory, and (largest) adverse-selection costs.
LOS 2 — Effective spread and VWAP
LOS: calculate and interpret effective spreads and VWAP transaction cost estimates
What you must do:  Compute the effective spread and a VWAP-based cost, and know the limitations of each.
Effective spread
The effective spread measures the round-trip cost implied by how far execution deviated from the midquote at the time the order arrived. It DOUBLES the one-side deviation so it is comparable to the quoted bid-ask spread:
Effective spread (buy)  = 2 × (execution price − midquote at order)
Effective spread (sell) = 2 × (midquote at order − execution price)
Worked example — effective spread (buy and sell)
Buy: the market is 49.98 / 50.02 (midquote 50.00) when the order arrives; it fills at 50.04.
Effective spread = 2 × (50.04 − 50.00) = 0.08
The 0.08 effective spread EXCEEDS the 0.04 quoted spread, signaling the order had market impact (it walked up the book).
Sell: midquote 50.00 at arrival; the order fills at 49.97.
Effective spread = 2 × (50.00 − 49.97) = 0.06
VWAP
The volume-weighted average price is the average transaction price over an interval, weighted by volume, and is a common execution benchmark — beating VWAP (buying below it or selling above it) suggests good execution:
VWAP = Σ (price × volume) / Σ (volume)
Worked example — computing VWAP and a VWAP cost
Market trades over the interval: 1,000 @ 50.00; 2,000 @ 50.10; 1,500 @ 50.05; 500 @ 50.20.
Numerator = 1,000×50.00 + 2,000×50.10 + 1,500×50.05 + 500×50.20
          = 50,000 + 100,200 + 75,075 + 25,100 = 250,375
VWAP = 250,375 / 5,000 = 50.075
A manager who bought 3,000 shares at an average price of 50.06 beat VWAP:
VWAP cost (buy) = (50.06 − 50.075)/50.075 = −0.03%   (a saving vs VWAP)
Realized spread and price impact
The effective spread has two parts: the price IMPACT (the permanent move that reflects the information in the trade — an adverse-selection cost borne by the liquidity provider) and the REALIZED spread (what the liquidity provider actually keeps after the price has moved):
Price impact (buy) = 2 × (midquote shortly after trade − midquote at trade)
Realized spread = effective spread − price impact
Worked example — decomposing the effective spread
A buy fills at 50.04 against a midquote of 50.00 (effective spread 0.08). Five minutes later the midquote has risen to 50.03.
Price impact    = 2 × (50.03 − 50.00) = 0.06
Realized spread = 0.08 − 0.06 = 0.02
The liquidity provider's 0.08 effective spread was mostly price impact (0.06) — the price moved against them because the buyer was informed — leaving only 0.02 of realized spread actually earned. A high price impact relative to the effective spread signals informed trading and heavy adverse selection.
Watch out: VWAP is easy to GAME (a trader can simply match the volume profile to hit the benchmark), it IGNORES the opportunity cost of shares never filled, and it is a poor benchmark for orders large relative to daily volume (the trade itself moves VWAP). The effective spread, meanwhile, only captures spread/impact around arrival — not delay or missed trades.
Exam focus: Effective spread DOUBLES the deviation from the midquote; if it exceeds the quoted spread, the trade had impact. Be able to compute VWAP from tick data and a VWAP cost. Know VWAP's weaknesses (gameable, ignores unfilled shares, unreliable for large orders) — the reasons implementation shortfall is preferred.
LOS 3 — Implementation shortfall
LOS: describe the implementation shortfall approach to transaction cost measurement
What you must do:  Define implementation shortfall, decompose it (including delay and impact), and compute it.
Definition. Implementation shortfall (IS) is the difference between the return on a hypothetical "paper" portfolio (executed instantly and costlessly at the decision price) and the return on the ACTUAL portfolio. It captures the ALL-IN cost of trading, crucially including the shares that were never filled — the piece VWAP and the effective spread miss.
IS = paper portfolio return − actual portfolio return
In currency terms, IS decomposes into three buckets (with the execution cost further split into delay and trading/impact):
IS = execution cost + opportunity cost + explicit fees
  execution cost   = filled × (execution price − decision price)
     = delay: filled × (arrival − decision)
     + trading/impact: filled × (execution − arrival)
  opportunity cost = unfilled × (end/cancel price − decision price)
Worked example — full implementation shortfall with delay decomposition
Decision to buy 10,000 shares at a decision price of 100.00. By the time the order reaches the market (arrival), the price is 100.05. 7,000 shares fill at an average of 100.15, commission 0.02/share. The remaining 3,000 are cancelled when the price is 100.30.
Paper investment = 10,000 × 100.00 = 1,000,000
Delay cost      = 7,000 × (100.05 − 100.00) = 350
Trading/impact  = 7,000 × (100.15 − 100.05) = 700
  → execution cost = 350 + 700 = 1,050
Opportunity cost= 3,000 × (100.30 − 100.00) = 900
Fees            = 7,000 × 0.02 = 140
Total IS ($)    = 1,050 + 900 + 140 = 2,090
IS (bps)        = 2,090 / 1,000,000 = 0.209% = 20.9 bps
In basis points: delay 3.5 + trading 7.0 + opportunity 9.0 + fees 1.4 = 20.9. Notice the biggest cost (9.0 bps) was the OPPORTUNITY cost of the unfilled 3,000 shares as the price ran away — exactly what VWAP would have ignored.
Exam focus: The reliable calculation. Three buckets: execution cost (filled × move from decision, splittable into delay and impact via the arrival price), opportunity cost (unfilled × move from decision to end), and fees; divide the currency total by the paper investment for the percentage. The opportunity-cost bucket is the whole point of IS — it captures the missed trade that other benchmarks ignore.
Choosing and adjusting the benchmark
Execution benchmarks fall into three groups by timing: PRE-TRADE (the decision price or arrival price — used by implementation shortfall), INTRADAY (VWAP, TWAP — the trade blended into the day), and POST-TRADE (the closing price — appropriate for index funds struck at the close). Arrival-price/IS is the most rigorous because it anchors to the moment of the investment decision and captures the opportunity cost of unfilled shares.
Because a raw implementation shortfall mixes the manager's trading cost with overall market drift, analysts often compute a MARKET-ADJUSTED (index-adjusted) implementation shortfall, subtracting the portion of the price move explained by the market — using the security's beta — to isolate the cost attributable to the trade itself:
Market-adjusted cost = raw arrival cost − beta × (market return over the trade)
Worked example — market-adjusted implementation shortfall
The raw arrival-price cost of a buy was 20.9 bps over the execution window. During that window the market index rose 0.10% (10 bps); the stock's beta is 1.0.
Market-driven portion = 1.0 × 10 = 10 bps
Market-adjusted cost  = 20.9 − 10 = 10.9 bps
About half the raw cost was simply the market rising, not poor execution — so the market-adjusted 10.9 bps better reflects the trader's actual performance. (If the market had FALLEN during a buy, the adjustment would raise the attributed cost.)
LOS 3b — Forecasting transaction costs and market impact
Before trading, managers estimate expected costs with pre-trade cost models that forecast market impact from the order's characteristics. Impact RISES with order size relative to average daily volume, with volatility, and with urgency; it FALLS with liquidity (depth and tighter spreads). Impact is usefully split into a TEMPORARY component (the price concession needed to attract immediate liquidity, which reverses afterward) and a PERMANENT component (the information-driven move that persists).
	•	Larger order relative to average daily volume → more impact.
	•	Higher volatility → more impact and more timing risk.
	•	Greater urgency → more impact (you are demanding liquidity).
	•	Deeper liquidity and tighter spreads → less impact.
These forecasts drive the choice of trading horizon and algorithm — balancing expected market impact against timing risk, the impact-versus-timing trade-off from LOS 1. A large order in an illiquid, volatile name with high urgency has a high forecast cost, arguing for a liquidity-seeking approach and careful sizing.
Illustration — a pre-trade cost estimate and the horizon trade-off
A model estimates, for an order of 20% of average daily volume in a stock with 2% daily volatility:
Spread cost              ≈  5 bps
Temporary impact         ≈ 15 bps  (reverses after trading)
Permanent impact         ≈ 10 bps  (information move, persists)
Forecast cost (fast)     ≈ 30 bps
Trading the same order more slowly over two days might cut impact to ~18 bps total, but the 2% daily volatility now adds meaningful TIMING RISK — the price could drift away before completion. The manager picks the horizon that balances forecast impact against timing risk.
Post-trade cost analysis
After trading, transaction cost analysis (TCA) compares the realized cost (implementation shortfall, market-adjusted where appropriate) to the pre-trade estimate and to the relevant benchmarks. It is used to evaluate broker, algorithm, and venue quality, to identify sources of slippage, and to refine future trading strategy — closing the loop from forecast to execution to review.
Exam focus: Know the three benchmark groups (pre-trade/intraday/post-trade) and the market-adjusted IS calculation (subtract beta × market return to strip out market drift). For pre-trade forecasting, impact rises with size/ADV, volatility, and urgency and falls with liquidity, and splits into temporary (reversing) and permanent (information) components. TCA closes the loop by comparing realized cost to the forecast and benchmarks.
LOS 4 & 5 — Electronic-trading drivers and market fragmentation
LOS: describe factors driving the development of electronic trading systems
LOS: describe market fragmentation
What you must do:  State why electronic trading grew and what fragmentation means for execution.
Drivers of electronic trading. Cheaper and faster computing, the drive to cut trading costs, regulatory changes that opened competition among trading venues, and investor demand for speed and anonymity together shifted trading from floors and phones to screens and algorithms — which in turn spawned high-frequency trading and a proliferation of venues.
Market fragmentation. The same security now trades across many venues — traditional exchanges, alternative trading systems / ECNs, and dark pools. The benefits are competition (lower fees and tighter spreads) and choice. The costs are that liquidity is SCATTERED across venues, so traders need smart order routing to find the best price and satisfy best-execution obligations, and price discovery can be complicated when much volume trades in the dark.
Internalization and payment for order flow. A related feature of fragmented markets is internalization: brokers execute client orders against their own inventory or route them to wholesale market-makers rather than to a public exchange. Retail order flow is often sold to these wholesalers (payment for order flow), who profit from the spread. This can deliver price improvement and low costs for small retail orders, but it concentrates order flow away from public venues, can weaken public price discovery, and creates a best-execution conflict — the broker may route to the highest payer rather than the venue best for the client — which regulators scrutinize.
Exam focus: Fragmentation's trade-off: more competition and lower explicit costs, but scattered liquidity requiring smart order routing, harder best execution, and (with dark trading) weaker price discovery. Drivers of electronic trading: technology, cost, regulation, and demand for speed/anonymity. Know internalization / payment for order flow and its best-execution conflict.
LOS 6 & 7 — Types of electronic traders and trading systems
LOS: identify and contrast the types of electronic traders
LOS: describe characteristics and uses of electronic trading systems
What you must do:  Distinguish the trader types and match trading-system tools to their uses.
Types of electronic traders
	•	News traders — react rapidly to announcements and data releases.
	•	Information-motivated (fundamental) traders — trade on research-based views about value.
	•	Electronic market makers — continuously post bids and offers, earning the spread for providing liquidity.
	•	Arbitrageurs — exploit fleeting price discrepancies across related instruments or venues.
	•	High-frequency / low-latency traders — use speed to make markets, arbitrage, and react to order flow.
	•	Buy-side execution desks — use algorithms to work large parent orders into child orders at low cost.
These traders play different roles in the ecosystem, which is why fragmented, electronic markets can still function well: electronic market makers and much high-frequency activity SUPPLY liquidity and tighten spreads; information-motivated and news traders CONSUME liquidity and drive price discovery; and arbitrageurs keep related prices aligned across venues and instruments. Their interaction determines how quickly information is impounded into prices and how cheaply other participants can trade.
Trading-system tools and their uses
Table 2 — Execution algorithms
Algorithm
Goal / use
VWAP
Trade in line with the volume profile to match the day's VWAP (low urgency)
TWAP
Spread evenly over time when volume is unpredictable
Percentage of volume (POV)
Participate at a set fraction of market volume
Implementation shortfall (arrival price)
Front-load to minimize slippage from the decision/arrival price (urgent)
Liquidity-seeking / dark
Hunt for and take liquidity across venues, including dark pools
Systems also use smart order routing (splitting an order across venues for the best price) and hidden or iceberg orders (displaying only part of a large order to limit information leakage).
Exam focus: Match the algorithm to the goal: VWAP/TWAP track a volume/time schedule (patient); POV trades a set share of volume; implementation-shortfall (arrival-price) algorithms minimize slippage from the decision price for urgent orders; liquidity-seeking algorithms hunt across venues for size.
Order types
Beyond the choice of algorithm, the ORDER TYPE trades execution certainty against price control:
Table 2b — Common order types
Order type
Behavior
Trade-off
Market
Executes immediately at the best available price
Certain execution; uncertain price (impact)
Limit
Executes only at a set price or better
Price control; may not fill
Marketable limit
A limit priced to execute immediately
Fast fill with a price cap
Hidden / iceberg
Displays none / only part of the size
Limits information leakage; lower queue priority
Pegged
Tracks a reference (e.g., the midpoint)
Follows the market; common in dark venues
Immediate-or-cancel
Fills what it can now, cancels the rest
Takes available liquidity without resting
Dark pools
Dark pools are trading venues that do not display quotes; participants trade against hidden liquidity, usually at the midpoint of the visible market. The benefit is that large orders can execute with reduced market impact and anonymity. The risks are adverse selection (trading against better-informed counterparties), information leakage (others "ping" the pool with small orders to detect a large resting order), and reduced pre-trade transparency and price discovery for the wider market. Institutions use dark pools to work large orders but must monitor execution quality carefully.
Exam focus: Match the order type to the need: market = certainty, no price control; limit = price control, fill risk; hidden/iceberg = limits leakage. Dark pools cut impact and give anonymity for large orders but bring adverse-selection and information-leakage risk and weaken price discovery.
LOS 8 — Advantages of low-latency traders
LOS: describe comparative advantages of low-latency traders
What you must do:  Explain what speed buys and how it is achieved.
Low-latency (high-frequency) traders profit almost entirely from being fast. Speed lets them react to new information and quote changes before others, capture fleeting arbitrage opportunities across venues and instruments, and make markets by updating quotes rapidly enough to avoid being "picked off" by better-informed orders. The speed advantage is engineered through co-location (placing servers physically next to the exchange's matching engine), high-speed direct data feeds, and highly optimized hardware and code. Critically, their edge is in the SPEED of processing and order submission, not in fundamental research — a technology advantage, not an analytical one.
Common low-latency strategies
Speed is deployed through a handful of recognizable strategies:
	•	Latency arbitrage — exploiting tiny timing differences in how fast price updates reach different venues, trading against quotes that are momentarily stale.
	•	Electronic market making — posting two-sided quotes to earn the spread and any liquidity rebates, updating faster than others to avoid being adversely selected.
	•	Short-horizon statistical arbitrage — rapid mean-reversion and cross-instrument relative-value trades.
