A manager expecting to invest cash soon can lock in today's yield by buying bond futures (a pre-investment hedge); an issuer planning to borrow can hedge against rising rates by selling futures or using a forward-starting swap. For a pension or insurer, RECEIVE-fixed swaps are a capital-efficient way to LENGTHEN asset duration to match long-dated liabilities — the LDI application from the institutional reading — adding liability-hedging duration without buying the long bonds the firm may not want to hold outright.
The swap-as-bond intuition
A receive-fixed, pay-floating swap is economically LONG a fixed-rate bond and SHORT a floating-rate note; its duration is therefore roughly the duration of the fixed leg minus the (small) duration of the floating leg — a positive number, so receiving fixed ADDS duration. Paying fixed is the reverse (short the fixed bond), which subtracts duration. This is why swaps are a capital-efficient way to lengthen or shorten duration: no bonds change hands, only the net interest exchange, yet the interest-rate exposure shifts as intended.
Swaps versus futures for duration management
For adjusting duration, futures and swaps each have merits. Bond futures are exchange-traded and liquid, ideal for short-term, tactical duration changes and easy to unwind, but they reference a cheapest-to-deliver bond (introducing delivery-option and basis complications) and require daily margin. Interest-rate swaps are over-the-counter and customizable to the exact tenor and notional, well suited to a longer-horizon or precise duration target — such as matching a pension's liability duration — and they avoid the cheapest-to-deliver complications, at the cost of counterparty exposure and lower liquidity. A manager making a large, lasting duration change to match liabilities typically prefers a swap; one making a short tactical tilt prefers futures. Both achieve the same BPV objective, so the calculation framework (the BPV gap divided by the instrument's BPV, or the swap-notional formula) is identical; only the instruments' practicalities differ.
Exam focus: Two calculations: futures N_f = (BPV_target − BPV_portfolio)/BPV_futures (positive = buy to add duration; target BPV 0 = full hedge); swap NP = [(D_target − D_p)×V]/D_swap. RECEIVE-fixed adds duration (it is long a fixed bond, short floating), PAY-fixed reduces it. Futures BPV ≈ BPV(CTD)/conversion factor. Prefer futures for short tactical changes, swaps for large or liability-matching (precise, longer-horizon) duration changes.
LOS 2 & 4 — Modifying equity risk (beta) and diversifying
LOS: demonstrate the use of equity swaps and futures to modify a portfolio's risk exposure to equities
What you must do:  Compute the equity-futures hedge ratio to hit a target beta, and use swaps to switch or diversify exposure.
To change a portfolio's beta using equity index futures, the number of contracts is:
N_f = [(β_target − β_portfolio) / β_futures] × [V_portfolio / (f × multiplier)]
Worked example — beta adjustment with futures
A 50,000,000 portfolio has beta 1.1; the manager wants beta 0.8. The futures beta is 1.0 and one contract is worth f × multiplier = 100,000.
N_f = [(0.8 − 1.1)/1.0] × [50,000,000 / 100,000]
    = (−0.30) × 500 = −150  → SELL 150 contracts
To fully hedge equity risk (target beta 0): N_f = (−1.1/1.0) × 500 = −550 → SELL 550. To raise beta, the sign flips and you BUY contracts.
Worked example — raising beta
A 40,000,000 portfolio has beta 0.90; the manager wants beta 1.20. Futures beta 1.0, contract value 200,000.
N_f = [(1.20 − 0.90)/1.0] × [40,000,000/200,000] = 0.30 × 200 = +60 → BUY 60
Buying 60 contracts lifts the effective beta to 1.20, adding market exposure without buying any stock.
Total-return equity swaps: switching and diversifying
A total-return equity swap exchanges the return on one asset for another (or for a financing rate), reshaping exposure without trading the underlying:
	•	Gain/reduce equity exposure — receive the equity index return and pay a financing rate to add exposure (or the reverse to reduce it).
	•	Switch asset classes — pay the equity return and receive a bond return to shift from equities to bonds synthetically.
	•	Diversify a concentrated position — pay the return on the concentrated stock and receive a diversified index return, gaining diversification WITHOUT a taxable sale of the stock.
Worked example — total-return equity swap
An investor wants 20,000,000 of equity-index exposure without buying stocks. Enter a total-return swap: RECEIVE the index total return on 20,000,000 notional and PAY a financing rate (a reference rate plus a spread) on 20,000,000.
If the index returns 8% and financing is 5%: net = (8% − 5%) × 20,000,000 = 600,000
The investor gains equity exposure funded synthetically; if the index falls they pay the negative return plus financing. The swap replicates a financed equity position — equity risk and counterparty risk, but no stock held.
Drill: An executive holds a large, low-basis position in her employer's stock and wants diversification without triggering capital gains. What swap achieves this? (Answer: a total-return swap where she PAYS the employer-stock return and RECEIVES a diversified index return — synthetic diversification with no sale, though it introduces counterparty risk and may face tax or regulatory limits.)
Assumptions and basis risk in beta hedging
The beta hedge assumes the portfolio's beta versus the futures' index is stable and correctly estimated; if beta is mis-estimated or shifts, the hedge is imperfect (basis risk). The hedge also removes only the portfolio's SYSTEMATIC (market) risk — not its idiosyncratic, stock-specific risk — so a concentrated portfolio remains exposed to company-specific moves even when fully beta-hedged. And because both the portfolio value and its beta drift over time, the number of contracts must be REBALANCED periodically to keep the target beta.
Exam focus: Equity beta: N_f = [(β_target − β_p)/β_f] × [V/(f×mult)] — negative = sell to reduce beta; target beta 0 = full hedge; positive = buy to raise beta. Total-return swaps switch or diversify exposure synthetically: to diversify a concentrated holding, PAY the stock return and RECEIVE an index return (no taxable sale, but counterparty risk). Beta hedging leaves idiosyncratic risk and needs rebalancing (basis risk if beta shifts).
LOS 3 — Managing currency exposure
LOS: demonstrate the use of currency forwards, futures, and swaps to modify a portfolio's currency exposure
What you must do:  Hedge and adjust currency exposure with forwards, futures, and FX swaps.
A domestic investor holding foreign assets is exposed to the foreign currency. To HEDGE, SELL the foreign currency forward (or short currency futures) in the amount of the exposure, locking in the exchange rate. Key tools:
	•	Currency forwards/futures — lock in a future exchange rate to hedge the foreign-currency value of assets; the hedge is rolled as contracts expire.
	•	FX swaps — simultaneously exchange currencies now and reverse later; used to ROLL a maturing hedge and to ADJUST the hedge ratio as the underlying asset value changes.
	•	Cross-currency (basis) swaps — exchange interest and principal in two currencies over time — useful for hedging foreign-currency debt or long-dated exposures.
Because the value of the foreign assets changes over time, a static hedge drifts; the hedge notional must be periodically REBALANCED (often via FX swaps) to keep the hedge ratio on target — a point developed further in the currency-management reading.
Worked example — hedging a foreign holding
A domestic investor holds a foreign-equity portfolio worth FC 10,000,000. To hedge the currency exposure fully, SELL FC 10,000,000 forward against the domestic currency.
At maturity, the gain or loss on the forward offsets the change in the domestic value of the FC 10,000,000 caused by exchange-rate moves — locking in the current rate.
Because the equity value itself fluctuates, the hedged amount drifts away from FC 10,000,000; the investor rebalances the forward (via an FX swap) to the current value. A hedge sized to the INITIAL value leaves residual currency exposure on subsequent gains and losses — the reason hedges must be rolled and resized.
Worked example — hedging a foreign bond (currency + duration)
A domestic investor holds a foreign government bond worth FC 20,000,000 with duration 8, and wants NO currency risk and a duration of 3. Two overlays run together:
Currency: SELL FC 20,000,000 forward → removes the currency exposure
Duration: BPV_current = 8 × 0.0001 × 20,000,000 = 16,000
          BPV_target  = 3 × 0.0001 × 20,000,000 = 6,000
          N_f = (6,000 − 16,000)/50 = −200 → SELL 200 bond futures
An FX forward removes the currency risk while bond futures cut the duration from 8 to 3 — a common combined hedge for a foreign fixed-income holding, and a reminder that currency and rate exposures are managed separately with the appropriate instrument for each.
For a portfolio spread across MANY currencies, the same logic scales into a multi-currency overlay: each material currency exposure is hedged (or partly hedged) with its own forward, and the hedges are rebalanced together as asset values move — often managed centrally as a currency-overlay mandate rather than position by position.
Cross hedges and residual risk
When a liquid hedging instrument does not exist for the exact exposure, a CROSS HEDGE uses a correlated instrument — for example hedging one currency with a closely related one, or a bond with a futures on a similar benchmark. A cross hedge leaves BASIS risk, because the hedge and the exposure do not move one-for-one, and the optimal cross-hedge ratio is the regression (minimum-variance) beta of the exposure on the hedging instrument — a point developed in the currency-management reading.
Exam focus: Hedge foreign-currency exposure by SELLING the foreign currency forward/futures in the amount of the exposure. FX swaps roll maturing hedges and adjust the hedge ratio as asset values change; cross-currency swaps hedge longer-dated interest-and-principal exposures. A static hedge drifts, so rebalance the notional to the current value. Where no exact instrument exists, cross-hedge with a correlated one (minimum-variance hedge ratio), accepting basis risk.
LOS 5 — Asset allocation, rebalancing, and cash equitization
LOS: demonstrate the use of derivatives in asset allocation, rebalancing, and inferring market expectations
What you must do:  Use futures overlays to rebalance, to equitize cash, and to adjust allocations efficiently.
Derivatives let a manager adjust the WHOLE portfolio's allocation quickly and cheaply, as an OVERLAY, without disturbing the underlying holdings or managers:
	•	Rebalancing — restore a drifted allocation by buying futures on the underweight asset and selling futures on the overweight one, far faster and cheaper than trading the physical securities.
	•	Cash equitization — a cash balance is a drag in a rising market; buy equity index futures against the cash to gain equity exposure while keeping the cash for liquidity (turning cash into 'equitized cash').
	•	Tactical tilts — express a short-term allocation view with an overlay, then unwind it, leaving the strategic portfolio intact.
Contracts to equitize cash = cash to equitize / (f × multiplier)
Worked example — equitizing cash
A fund holds 20,000,000 of cash it wants to give equity exposure. Index futures are worth f × multiplier = 200,000 per contract (beta ≈ 1).
Contracts = 20,000,000 / 200,000 = 100 → BUY 100 index futures
The 20M now earns the equity return (plus the cash still earns interest), eliminating the cash drag while the cash remains available. Reverse the trade when the cash is deployed or no longer needed.
Synthetic cash, equity, and bond positions
Combining a holding with futures creates SYNTHETIC positions — the mechanical basis for the overlays above:
	•	Synthetic EQUITY = cash + long equity futures (equitizing cash: the cash earns interest while the futures deliver the equity return).
	•	Synthetic CASH = equity holding + short equity futures (de-equitizing: removes market exposure, leaving roughly the risk-free return).
	•	Synthetic BOND / duration = cash + long bond futures, or adjust an existing portfolio's duration with the BPV overlay.
Worked example — creating synthetic cash (de-equitizing)
A manager holds 50,000,000 in equities but wants to temporarily eliminate market exposure without selling. Sell equity index futures covering 50,000,000; each contract is worth 250,000.
Contracts = 50,000,000 / 250,000 = 200 → SELL 200 contracts
The short futures offset the equity's market moves, so the position now behaves like cash (earning ≈ the risk-free rate). Buy the futures back to restore equity exposure — the exact reverse of equitizing cash.
Worked example — rebalancing with a futures overlay
A 100,000,000 fund has drifted to 70% equity / 30% bonds but targets 60/40. Instead of selling 10,000,000 of stock and buying 10,000,000 of bonds, use an overlay.
Equity: reduce exposure by 10,000,000 → sell equity futures
  contracts = 10,000,000 / 250,000 (per contract) = 40 → SELL 40
Bonds: add 10,000,000 of bond exposure → BUY bond futures (sized by BPV)
The overlay shifts the EFFECTIVE allocation to 60/40 immediately and cheaply, leaving the underlying holdings and managers untouched; it is unwound once the physical portfolio is rebalanced or the tilt is no longer wanted.
Completion overlays
A COMPLETION overlay uses derivatives to correct unintended exposures across a multi-manager fund. If the fund's managers collectively leave it underweight a region, a sector, or a duration bucket, an overlay manager adds the missing exposure with futures and swaps to bring the TOTAL fund to its policy targets. This lets the fund keep its chosen managers while ensuring the aggregate portfolio matches the intended allocation; it is governed centrally, often under a dedicated overlay mandate with explicit risk limits.
Portable alpha
A powerful overlay application is PORTABLE ALPHA: separating the ALPHA (manager skill) from the BETA (market exposure). Invest capital with a skilled manager in one market (the alpha source), use derivatives to SUBTRACT that market's beta, and overlay futures/swaps to ADD the desired market's beta on top — 'porting' the manager's alpha onto a different benchmark. For example, allocate to a skilled bond manager, hedge out the bond beta, and overlay equity-index futures to gain equity beta, so the portfolio earns the equity market return PLUS the bond manager's alpha. This decouples WHERE alpha is generated from the asset-class exposure the investor actually wants.
Inferring market expectations. Derivative prices also REVEAL expectations: fed-funds and interest-rate futures imply the market's expected path of policy rates, and option/volatility prices imply expected volatility — useful market-implied inputs for a manager.
Exam focus: Overlays adjust allocation without trading the underlying: rebalance by buying futures on the underweight/selling the overweight; equitize cash by buying index futures (contracts = cash/(f×mult)) to remove cash drag. Derivative prices (rate futures, option-implied vol) reveal MARKET EXPECTATIONS of rates and volatility.
LOS 6 — Managing volatility exposure
LOS: demonstrate the use of volatility derivatives, including VIX futures and variance swaps
What you must do:  Explain VIX futures and compute a variance-swap payoff.
Volatility is a tradable exposure in its own right. VIX FUTURES give exposure to the market's expected near-term volatility (the VIX index); buying them profits if expected volatility rises — a hedge against equity drawdowns, which coincide with volatility spikes. VARIANCE SWAPS pay off on the difference between REALIZED variance and a strike variance agreed at inception:
Variance-swap payoff = variance notional × (σ²_realized − σ²_strike)
  variance notional = vega notional / (2 × strike volatility)
Worked example — variance swap payoff
A long variance swap has a vega notional of 100,000 and a strike volatility of 20%. Realized volatility over the period turns out to be 25%.
Variance notional = 100,000 / (2 × 20) = 2,500
Strike variance = 20² = 400 ; realized variance = 25² = 625
Payoff = 2,500 × (625 − 400) = 2,500 × 225 = 562,500
The long side gains 562,500 because realized volatility exceeded the strike. Note the CONVEXITY: the vega-notional approximation (100,000 × (25 − 20) = 500,000) understates the true payoff, because variance swaps pay on variance, not volatility.
Worked example — short variance swap (the other side)
A dealer is SHORT the same variance swap (vega notional 100,000, strike vol 20%, variance notional 2,500). Realized volatility turns out to be only 15%.
Strike variance = 400 ; realized variance = 15² = 225
Payoff to the short = 2,500 × (400 − 225) = 2,500 × 175 = +437,500
The short profits because realized volatility fell below the strike. But note the ASYMMETRY: the short's gain is limited (volatility cannot fall below zero) while its loss is unbounded if volatility spikes — the classic short-volatility risk.
A long variance swap (or long VIX futures) is a way to HEDGE a portfolio against volatility spikes and market crashes, or to speculate on volatility directly; the position profits when realized volatility exceeds the level priced in at inception.
VIX futures roll and variance vs volatility swaps
VIX futures have a term structure usually in CONTANGO (longer-dated priced above spot) in calm markets, so a long position that rolls contracts forward incurs a roll COST as the futures converge down toward spot — a persistent drag on a long-volatility hedge held over time. In stress, the curve flips to BACKWARDATION. A VARIANCE swap pays on realized VARIANCE (squared volatility) and is therefore CONVEX in volatility, while a VOLATILITY swap pays linearly on realized volatility; variance swaps are the more common instrument, and their convexity means they gain more than a linear position when volatility spikes — attractive for a crash hedge.
Exam focus: VIX futures = exposure to expected volatility (a crash hedge, since vol spikes when equities fall), but a long roll costs money in contango. Variance swap payoff = variance notional × (realized var − strike var), with variance notional = vega notional/(2×strike vol). Long variance profits when realized vol > strike; the convexity means it pays MORE than the linear vega approximation, and more than a (linear) volatility swap.
Risks and practical considerations of derivative strategies
Derivative overlays are efficient but carry their own risks, which the exam expects you to flag:
	•	Counterparty (credit) risk — OTC forwards and swaps depend on the counterparty's performance; mitigated by central clearing and collateral.
	•	Margin and liquidity risk — futures are marked-to-market daily and require variation margin, so a large adverse move forces cash outflows even on a position that is 'hedged' in economic terms.
	•	Basis risk — the derivative may not move exactly with the exposure being hedged (standardized futures, an imperfect or shifting beta), leaving residual risk.
	•	Rollover (roll) risk — short-dated contracts must be rolled to maintain a longer-horizon exposure, at an uncertain roll cost (for example, VIX contango).
	•	Leverage and operational risk — a small margin controls a large notional, magnifying the impact of errors, so overlays require robust operational controls.
These are why an overlay program needs a LIQUIDITY buffer for margin calls, careful sizing, and continuous monitoring — the efficiency of derivatives comes with real obligations, and a common exam point is that a fully invested fund using futures must still hold cash to meet variation margin.
Exam focus: Flag the risks of overlays: counterparty (OTC), margin/liquidity (daily mark-to-market forces cash outflows), basis (imperfect/shifting hedge), rollover (roll cost), and leverage/operational. Practical must-know: even a 'hedged' futures position needs a cash/liquidity buffer for variation margin.
Quick reference — key formulas
The formulas worth memorizing
BPV = modified duration × 0.0001 × market value
Duration via futures: N_f = (BPV_target − BPV_portfolio) / BPV_futures
  BPV_futures ≈ BPV(CTD)/conversion factor ; receive-fixed swap adds duration
Duration via swap: NP = [(D_target − D_p) × V] / D_swap
Beta via futures: N_f = [(β_target − β_p)/β_f] × [V/(f×mult)]
Equitize cash: contracts = cash / (f × multiplier)
Diversify concentrated stock: PAY stock return, RECEIVE index return (swap)
Variance-swap payoff = variance notional × (realized var − strike var)
  variance notional = vega notional / (2 × strike vol)
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. Portfolio BPV 40,000; target 55,000; futures BPV 80. Contracts?
   -> (55,000 − 40,000)/80 = 187.5 ≈ 188 (buy, to raise duration).
2. Which swap adds duration, receive-fixed or pay-fixed?
   -> Receive-fixed adds duration; pay-fixed reduces it.
3. Raise a 100M portfolio's duration 4→6 with a swap of duration 7. Notional?
   -> [(6−4)×100M]/7 = 28.6M receive-fixed.
4. 50M portfolio, beta 1.1 → 0.8, futures beta 1.0, contract 100,000. Contracts?
   -> [(0.8−1.1)/1.0]×(50M/100k) = −150 (sell 150).
5. Fully hedge that portfolio's equity risk — contracts?
   -> (−1.1/1.0)×500 = −550 (sell 550).
6. How do you hedge a foreign-asset currency exposure?
   -> Sell the foreign currency forward (or short currency futures) in the amount of the exposure.
7. What is an FX swap used for in hedging?
   -> Rolling a maturing hedge and adjusting the hedge ratio as the asset value changes.
8. Swap to diversify a concentrated stock without selling it?
   -> Pay the stock's return, receive a diversified index return (no taxable sale; adds counterparty risk).
9. Equitize 20M of cash with futures worth 200,000 each. Contracts?
   -> 20M/200k = 100 (buy index futures).
10. Variance swap: vega notional 100,000, strike vol 20%, realized 25%. Payoff?
   -> Var notional 2,500; 2,500×(625−400) = 562,500.
11. Why buy VIX futures as a hedge?
   -> Volatility spikes when equities fall, so long VIX futures gain in a crash.
12. What do interest-rate futures prices reveal?
   -> The market's expected path of policy/interest rates.

readings.title: Currency Management: An Introduction
readings.orientation: Orientation. How currency exposure affects a global portfolio and how to manage it. Nine learning outcomes: the effect of currency on return and risk (with the domestic-currency return and variance formulas), the strategic hedging decisions the IPS must make, the spectrum from passive to active management, the active-trading styles (fundamentals, technical, carry, volatility), the mechanics of hedging with forwards and FX swaps (including roll yield), option structures that cut hedging cost, cross and minimum-variance hedges, and the special challenges of emerging-market currencies. The quantitative core — the domestic-currency return, the risk decomposition, and the minimum-variance hedge ratio — is worked below. Learn those three calculations, the hedging spectrum, and the carry trade cold.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Derivatives and Currency Management
Reading: Currency Management: An Introduction
Orientation. How currency exposure affects a global portfolio and how to manage it. Nine learning outcomes: the effect of currency on return and risk (with the domestic-currency return and variance formulas), the strategic hedging decisions the IPS must make, the spectrum from passive to active management, the active-trading styles (fundamentals, technical, carry, volatility), the mechanics of hedging with forwards and FX swaps (including roll yield), option structures that cut hedging cost, cross and minimum-variance hedges, and the special challenges of emerging-market currencies. The quantitative core — the domestic-currency return, the risk decomposition, and the minimum-variance hedge ratio — is worked below. Learn those three calculations, the hedging spectrum, and the carry trade cold.

LOS 1 — Currency effects on portfolio return and risk
LOS: analyze the effects of currency movements on portfolio risk and return
What you must do:  Compute the domestic-currency return and the domestic-currency risk of a foreign asset.
Return. The return to a domestic investor combines the foreign-currency asset return and the currency return MULTIPLICATIVELY:
R_DC = (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) − 1
Worked example — domestic-currency return
A foreign asset returns 8% in its local currency (R_FC = 8%), and the foreign currency appreciates 3% against the domestic currency (R_FX = 3%).
R_DC = (1.08)(1.03) − 1 = 1.1124 − 1 = 11.24%
The 11.24% exceeds the simple sum (11%) by the cross-product (0.08 × 0.03). Currency appreciation adds to the domestic return; depreciation subtracts.
Risk. The variance of the domestic-currency return depends on the asset variance, the currency variance, AND their correlation:
σ²(R_DC) ≈ σ²(R_FC) + σ²(R_FX) + 2 ρ σ(R_FC) σ(R_FX)
Worked example — domestic-currency risk
σ(R_FC) = 15%, σ(R_FX) = 8%, correlation ρ = 0.30 between the asset and currency returns.
σ² = 0.15² + 0.08² + 2(0.30)(0.15)(0.08)
   = 0.0225 + 0.0064 + 0.0072 = 0.0361
σ  = √0.0361 = 19.0%
Currency risk ADDS to total risk; a lower (or negative) asset-currency correlation reduces it, which is why the correlation matters for how much to hedge.
Illustration — two foreign holdings
A portfolio holds two foreign assets. Asset X returns 6% locally with its currency +4%; Asset Y returns 10% locally with its currency −3%.
X domestic return = (1.06)(1.04) − 1 = 10.24%
Y domestic return = (1.10)(0.97) − 1 = 6.70%
Currency helped X (+4%) and hurt Y (−3%); because the two currencies moved oppositely, their currency effects partly OFFSET at the portfolio level — multi-currency diversification in action.
Currency as a separate decision; portfolio diversification
Because R_DC decomposes into the asset return and the currency return, the currency decision can be SEPARATED from the asset decision — a manager may like a foreign asset but not its currency, and hedge the currency while keeping the asset. At the PORTFOLIO level, currency exposures across many countries partially DIVERSIFY (currencies are imperfectly correlated), so the aggregate currency risk is less than the sum of the individual currency risks — one argument against fully hedging every currency. What matters is a currency exposure's contribution to TOTAL portfolio risk, not its standalone volatility.
Worked example — currency depreciation
A foreign asset returns 8% locally, but the foreign currency DEPRECIATES 5%.
R_DC = (1.08)(0.95) − 1 = 1.026 − 1 = 2.6%
The depreciation turned an 8% local gain into a 2.6% domestic gain — a currency contribution of about −5.4%. Unhedged currency exposure can dominate a foreign investment's outcome, for better or worse.
Currency behavior differs by asset class
The case for hedging is generally stronger for BONDS than for EQUITIES. A foreign bond's local-currency return is relatively stable, so the currency can be the DOMINANT source of its domestic-currency risk — making hedging valuable and common. A foreign equity is far more volatile in local terms, so the currency is a smaller share of its total risk, and equities are often left partly or fully unhedged; in some markets foreign equity and currency returns are even negatively correlated, providing a natural partial hedge. This asymmetry is why many global investors hedge their foreign-bond currency exposure much more fully than their foreign-equity exposure — the currency dwarfs the bond's own volatility but is minor next to the equity's.
Exam focus: Two formulas: R_DC = (1+R_FC)(1+R_FX) − 1 (multiplicative, so add the cross-product); and σ²(R_DC) ≈ σ²_FC + σ²_FX + 2ρσ_FCσ_FX. Currency movements add both return and risk; the asset-currency correlation drives the risk contribution and the optimal hedge. Currency is a separable decision, multi-currency exposures partially diversify, and foreign BONDS are hedged more than foreign EQUITIES (currency is a bigger share of bond risk).
LOS 2 — Strategic currency decisions in the IPS
LOS: discuss strategic choices in currency management
What you must do:  State what the IPS must decide about currency and the factors that push toward more or less hedging.
The IPS sets the strategic currency policy: the benchmark (strategic) HEDGE RATIO, the permitted RANGE for active deviation from it, and the rebalancing approach. The factors that push toward MORE hedging: a shorter investment horizon, lower risk tolerance, a higher proportion of foreign assets, a high asset-currency correlation, and low hedging costs. The factors that push toward LESS hedging: a long horizon (currency effects may mean-revert and wash out over time), higher hedging costs, and a belief that currency adds diversification or return.
Illustration — hedging reduces risk
Recall the unhedged domestic-currency risk was 19.0% (from σ_FC 15%, σ_FX 8%, ρ 0.30). If the currency is FULLY hedged, the σ_FX and correlation terms drop out:
Hedged σ ≈ σ_FC = 15.0%
Removing the currency exposure cuts total risk from 19.0% to about 15.0% — the risk-reduction benefit of hedging (a partial hedge lands in between). This is the quantitative basis for the 'hedging as risk reduction' argument.
The case for and against hedging
Whether to hedge is genuinely debated. AGAINST hedging: over long horizons currencies tend to mean-revert, so their effect washes out; hedging costs money and effort; and unhedged foreign currency can add diversification. FOR hedging: currency adds risk without a reliable expected return (many argue currency has roughly zero long-run expected return, which makes the hedge a 'free' reduction in risk — the argument for full hedging as the neutral default), and short-horizon or risk-averse investors cannot afford to wait out currency swings. In practice most investors land in the MIDDLE — a partial strategic hedge with some discretion — because the theoretical case for either extreme is weak and depends on horizon, costs, and correlations.
Choosing the strategic hedge ratio
With no strong currency view and roughly zero expected currency return, theory does not pin down a single best hedge ratio, so investors often anchor to a benchmark such as FULL hedging (the minimum-risk default for a pure risk reducer) or a 50% 'regret-minimizing' hedge that avoids the extremes of being fully exposed if the currency falls or fully hedged if it rises. Practical considerations — costs, the proportion of foreign assets, the correlation with the portfolio, and behavioral regret — then adjust the choice, and the IPS records the resulting benchmark hedge ratio and the discretionary band around it.
Exam focus: The IPS decides the benchmark hedge ratio, the discretionary range around it, and rebalancing. Hedge MORE with a short horizon, low risk tolerance, more foreign assets, and cheap hedging; hedge LESS with a long horizon (mean reversion), high hedging cost, or a diversification/return rationale. The 'free lunch' argument supports full hedging as the neutral default; a 50% hedge minimizes regret; most investors partially hedge.
LOS 3 — The spectrum of currency-management strategies
LOS: describe the continuum of currency management strategies from passive to active
What you must do:  Lay out the passive-to-active continuum and why hedge ratios sit between 0 and 1.
Table 1 — The currency-management continuum
Approach
Description
Passive hedging
Match the benchmark hedge ratio mechanically; no views (often fully hedged)
Discretionary hedging
Mostly hedged, with limited active deviation to add value or cut cost
Active currency management
Take deliberate currency positions to add return within set limits
Currency overlay
A separate specialist mandate manages the currency exposure actively
Currency as an asset class
Pursue currency alpha as a standalone return source
Real-world hedge ratios usually sit BETWEEN 0 and 100%: full hedging removes currency risk but forgoes diversification and incurs cost and rebalancing effort, while no hedging leaves full currency risk. Partial hedging balances risk reduction against cost and any return/diversification benefit, and the exact ratio reflects the IPS factors above.
Currency overlay and benchmarks
When currency is managed actively as a distinct program, it is often delegated to a CURRENCY OVERLAY manager whose mandate and benchmark are set separately from the asset portfolio. The overlay can be a pure hedging mandate (keep the hedge on target) or an active/alpha mandate (add return from currency views), and its performance is measured against a currency benchmark — typically the strategic hedge ratio. Separating the overlay lets specialists manage currency without disturbing the underlying asset managers, the same logic as the derivative overlays in the previous reading.
Exam focus: The continuum runs passive → discretionary → active → overlay → currency-as-asset-class. Hedge ratios typically fall between 0 and 1 because full hedging costs money and forgoes diversification while no hedging bears full currency risk; partial hedging balances the two. An active currency program is often run as a separate overlay mandate benchmarked to the strategic hedge ratio.
LOS 4 — Active currency trading strategies
LOS: describe active currency trading strategies based on economic fundamentals, technical analysis, carry, and volatility
What you must do:  Distinguish the four active styles, with the carry trade worked.
	•	Economic fundamentals — value currencies on fundamentals (purchasing-power parity, current-account and capital flows, relative growth and policy) and buy undervalued, sell overvalued currencies; a longer-horizon, value-driven approach.
	•	Technical analysis — trade on price patterns, trends, momentum, and support/resistance, ignoring fundamentals; a shorter-horizon approach.
	•	Carry trade — borrow (short) a LOW-yield currency and invest (long) a HIGH-yield currency, profiting from the interest-rate differential when the high-yield currency does not depreciate enough to offset it — a bet AGAINST uncovered interest rate parity, which empirically fails short-term.
	•	Volatility trading — use FX options (straddles, strangles, variance/vol products) to trade a view on currency VOLATILITY rather than direction.
Worked example — the carry trade
Borrow (short) the funding currency at 0.5% and invest (long) the higher-yielding currency at 4.5%.
If the exchange rate is unchanged: carry return ≈ 4.5% − 0.5% = 4.0%
The risk: if the high-yield currency DEPRECIATES against the funding currency by more than 4%, the trade loses. Carry trades earn steadily in calm markets but suffer sharp losses in crises (the high-yield currency crashes) — a negatively skewed, 'picking up nickels in front of a steamroller' payoff.
Sources of currency return and the volatility risk premium
Active currency returns come from a few persistent sources: CARRY (the rate differential), VALUE (mean reversion toward fundamental value), MOMENTUM (trends persist), and the VOLATILITY risk premium (implied FX volatility tends to exceed realized, so selling FX options or volatility earns a premium on average). These mirror the factor premia in equities and bonds and underpin systematic currency strategies. Each, however, carries crash risk — carry and short-volatility strategies in particular are negatively skewed, earning steadily and then losing sharply in stress.
Managing and measuring active currency
Active currency positions are taken within the discretionary bands the IPS sets, and their success is judged like any active strategy — on risk-adjusted, benchmark-relative performance (the information ratio of the currency program versus its hedge-ratio benchmark). Because currency-strategy returns, especially carry, are negatively skewed, managers and asset owners must look beyond average return and volatility to the TAIL risk, sizing positions and setting stop-losses accordingly rather than trusting a Sharpe ratio that ignores the skew.
Exam focus: Four styles: fundamentals (value via PPP/flows — buy cheap), technical (trends/momentum, ignore fundamentals), carry (long high-yield, short low-yield — bet against UIP), and volatility (FX options on a vol view). Return sources: carry, value, momentum, and the volatility risk premium (implied > realized). The carry and short-vol strategies have negatively-skewed, crash-prone payoffs, so judge them on tail risk, not just Sharpe.
LOS 5 & 6 — Hedging with forwards and FX swaps; roll yield
LOS: describe how forward contracts and FX swaps are used to adjust hedge ratios and manage currency exposure
What you must do:  Explain hedging and rolling with forwards/FX swaps, the need to rebalance, and roll yield.
Currency exposure is most often hedged with FORWARD contracts: to hedge a long foreign-currency exposure, SELL the currency forward. Because forwards have a fixed maturity, the hedge is rolled forward as each contract expires using an FX SWAP — a simultaneous spot transaction and offsetting forward that extends the hedge to a new date (and can also ADJUST the hedge ratio by changing the forward notional).
Rebalancing. As the foreign asset's value changes, a static hedge drifts off target — a rising asset becomes UNDER-hedged, a falling one OVER-hedged — so the hedge notional must be periodically rebalanced. More frequent rebalancing tracks the target more tightly but costs more in transactions.
Roll yield. Rolling a forward hedge earns or costs the forward premium/discount (roll yield). Hedging a currency trading at a forward PREMIUM incurs a cost (negative roll yield); hedging one at a forward DISCOUNT earns a positive roll yield. Roll yield is tied to the interest-rate differential (covered interest rate parity), so hedging a high-yield currency (typically at a forward discount) tends to earn positive roll yield, and hedging a low-yield currency tends to cost.
Worked example — forward points and covered interest rate parity
Spot = 1.2000 (domestic per foreign). Domestic 1-year rate 4%, foreign rate 1%.
Forward ≈ spot × (1 + r_domestic)/(1 + r_foreign) = 1.2000 × (1.04/1.01) = 1.2356
The foreign currency trades at a forward PREMIUM (1.2356 > 1.2000) because it is the LOWER-yielding currency. A domestic investor hedging by SELLING it forward incurs NEGATIVE roll yield (a cost), since the lower-yielding currency is 'expensive' forward. Hedging a HIGHER-yielding (forward-discount) currency would instead EARN positive roll yield.
Worked example — roll yield and hedge rebalancing
An investor hedges a foreign-currency exposure by selling it forward. The foreign currency is the HIGHER-yielding one, so it trades at a forward DISCOUNT (forward < spot).
Selling the discount currency forward earns positive roll yield ≈ rate differential
If the differential is 3%/yr → rolling the hedge earns ≈ +3% roll yield
Now the foreign asset RISES 10% in value: the original hedge (sized to the old value) now covers too little — the position is UNDER-hedged by about 10%. The investor sells additional forward to rebalance back to the target hedge ratio.
Rebalancing frequency and hedging cost
How often to rebalance a currency hedge is a cost-versus-tracking trade-off. Rebalancing frequently (or with tighter triggers) keeps the hedge ratio close to target and reduces tracking error to the benchmark, but incurs more transaction costs and — for a forward-premium currency — more roll cost. Rebalancing infrequently is cheaper but lets the hedge drift as asset values move, adding basis and tracking risk. Managers choose between calendar-based rebalancing (fixed intervals) and threshold-based rebalancing (act only when the hedge drifts beyond a band). Using OPTIONS rather than forwards can reduce the need to rebalance after adverse moves — the option's payoff auto-adjusts — at the cost of the premium. The right frequency balances the marginal risk reduction against the marginal cost.
Exam focus: Hedge a long foreign exposure by SELLING the currency forward; roll the hedge with FX swaps (which also adjust the hedge ratio). Rebalance because asset-value moves cause over/under-hedging (a rising asset is under-hedged). Roll yield: hedging a forward-premium currency costs (negative), a forward-discount currency earns (positive) — via covered interest rate parity. Rebalancing frequency trades tracking accuracy against transaction and roll costs (calendar vs threshold; options reduce rebalancing needs).
LOS 7 — Reducing hedging costs with options
LOS: describe trading strategies used to reduce hedging costs and modify the risk–return characteristics of a foreign-currency portfolio
What you must do:  Explain how option structures trade cost for less complete protection.
A full forward hedge has no upfront premium but removes ALL currency upside; options keep upside but cost premium. Managers reduce the option cost by giving up some protection:
	•	Buy an out-of-the-money option — cheaper than an at-the-money option, but protection only begins beyond the strike.
	•	Risk reversal — buy a protective option and SELL an option on the other side to fund it (e.g., buy a put, sell a call), cutting or eliminating the net premium in exchange for capping the favorable side.
	•	Spread (e.g., put spread) — buy one option and sell a further-out one, lowering cost but capping the protection.
	•	Seagull / collar variants — combine several options to shape the payoff and reduce cost within a bounded range.
Each structure trades COST against the completeness of protection and the retained upside — the same cost-versus-protection spectrum as equity hedging, applied to currencies.
Worked example — a risk reversal to cut hedging cost
To protect a long foreign-currency exposure cheaply, buy an out-of-the-money PUT (protection) for a premium of 1.0 and sell an out-of-the-money CALL for a premium of 0.8.
Net premium = 1.0 − 0.8 = 0.2   (versus 1.0 for the put alone)
The sold call funds most of the put, cutting the net cost from 1.0 to 0.2 — but it CAPS the gain if the currency appreciates past the call strike. This risk reversal trades away favorable upside for cheaper downside protection.
Exam focus: Full forward hedge = no premium but no upside; options keep upside but cost premium. Cut cost by buying OTM options, using a risk reversal (sell one side to fund the other — net premium falls but the favorable side is capped), a put spread, or a seagull/collar — all trade lower cost for less complete protection.
LOS 8 — Cross hedges, macro hedges, and the minimum-variance hedge ratio
LOS: describe the use of cross hedges, macro hedges, and minimum-variance hedge ratios
What you must do:  Define cross and macro hedges and compute the minimum-variance hedge ratio.
Cross hedge (proxy hedge). Hedge an exposure using a DIFFERENT but correlated currency, when the direct instrument is unavailable, illiquid, or expensive (common for emerging-market currencies hedged with a correlated major currency). It leaves basis risk if the correlation is imperfect.
Macro hedge. Hedge the BROAD currency risk of the whole portfolio with a single instrument — for example a basket or index derivative — rather than hedging each currency individually. Efficient but less precise.
Minimum-variance hedge ratio. The hedge ratio that minimizes the variance of the domestic-currency return is the regression slope (beta) of the asset's domestic-currency return on the currency return — so it hedges the currency risk AND the part of asset risk correlated with the currency:
h* = ρ(R_DC, R_FX) × [ σ(R_DC) / σ(R_FX) ]
Worked example — minimum-variance hedge ratio
Correlation between the asset's domestic-currency return and the currency return ρ = 0.60; σ(R_DC) = 10%; σ(R_FX) = 8%.
h* = 0.60 × (10 / 8) = 0.60 × 1.25 = 0.75
Sell 0.75 (75%) of the currency exposure forward to minimize variance. Because it reflects correlation, h* can EXCEED 1 (hedge more than the exposure) if the asset and currency are strongly positively correlated, or be NEGATIVE if they move oppositely.
Worked example — cross (proxy) hedge
An investor has exposure to a thinly-traded EM currency (EMC) that is expensive to hedge directly, but EMC is 0.85 correlated with a liquid major currency (MJC). Hedge EMC by selling MJC forward instead.
Sell MJC forward, scaled by the beta of EMC to MJC
Basis risk remains ≈ 1 − 0.85² = 28% of EMC's variance (unexplained by MJC)
The proxy hedge removes most of the currency risk cheaply but leaves BASIS risk — the part of EMC's moves not tracked by MJC. If the two diverge (correlations break down, common in an EM crisis), the proxy hedge fails — the key risk of cross hedging.
Residual exposures and the limits of hedging
Even a carefully managed hedge leaves residual exposures. A forward hedge fixes the exchange rate on a fixed NOTIONAL, but the actual foreign-asset value at maturity is uncertain, so the hedge is never perfectly sized — the 'quantity risk' of hedging a variable underlying value. Cross and proxy hedges leave basis risk; option hedges leave the out-of-the-money 'deductible' and the premium cost; and rolling hedges expose the investor to changes in the forward points (roll yield) over time. The practical goal is therefore NOT to eliminate currency risk entirely — usually impossible and expensive — but to reduce it to a level consistent with the IPS, accepting the residual as a managed cost.
Exam focus: Cross/proxy hedge = hedge with a correlated different currency (leaves basis risk); macro hedge = one instrument for the whole portfolio's currency risk. Minimum-variance hedge ratio h* = ρ(R_DC,R_FX) × σ(R_DC)/σ(R_FX) — the regression beta; sell h* forward. It can exceed 1 or be negative depending on the asset-currency correlation. Hedges leave residual risk (quantity risk, basis, option deductible, roll), so the aim is a target level of currency risk, not zero.
LOS 9 — Emerging-market currency challenges
LOS: discuss challenges for managing emerging market currency exposures
What you must do:  List why emerging-market currency exposure is harder and riskier to manage.
Emerging-market (EM) currencies pose distinct problems:
	•	Higher trading costs and lower liquidity — wider bid-ask spreads and thinner markets make hedging more expensive.
	•	Convertibility and capital controls — some currencies are restricted or non-deliverable, so hedging must use non-deliverable forwards (NDFs) or proxy hedges.
	•	Non-normal, negatively-skewed returns — EM currencies are prone to sudden large devaluations and fat-tailed, crash-prone behavior, so standard risk measures understate the danger.
	•	Higher carry but higher tail risk — EM currencies often offer attractive carry, but that carry compensates for the risk of sharp depreciation; EM carry trades are especially crash-prone.
	•	Contagion — EM currency crises tend to spread across countries, so diversification across EM currencies fails just when it is needed.
Watch out: The EM trap: attractive carry and steady gains lull investors into oversized positions, then a devaluation or contagion event inflicts a large, sudden loss. The negative skew and fat tails mean volatility alone understates the risk, and correlations spike toward 1 in a crisis — so 'diversified' EM currency exposure offers little protection when it matters.
Illustration — an emerging-market carry position
An investor funds a carry trade by borrowing at 1% and investing in an EM currency yielding 9% — an 8% carry.
Calm years: earn ≈ 8% carry + any EM appreciation (steady, attractive)
Crisis: EM currency devalues 25% → ≈ −25% + 8% = −17% in weeks
One devaluation wipes out years of accumulated carry. This negative-skew, 'nickels-then-steamroller' pattern is the defining risk of EM carry — and because contagion makes several EM currencies crash together, diversification across them fails exactly when it is needed.
Managing EM currency exposure therefore emphasizes conservative POSITION SIZING, explicit tail-risk limits, and sometimes buying option protection (paying premium for a floor) rather than relying on forwards alone — accepting lower expected carry in exchange for surviving the inevitable stress episodes.
Non-deliverable forwards
Where a currency is not freely convertible or deliverable, hedges use NON-DELIVERABLE FORWARDS (NDFs): contracts settled in a convertible currency (usually USD) for the DIFFERENCE between the agreed forward rate and the actual spot at maturity, with no exchange of the restricted currency itself. NDFs let investors hedge or speculate on a controlled currency, but they carry their own basis risk (the NDF rate can diverge from the onshore rate), counterparty risk, and liquidity risk, and are typically less liquid and wider-spread than deliverable forwards.
Exam focus: EM currency challenges: higher costs and lower liquidity, convertibility/capital controls (use NDFs or proxy hedges), non-normal negatively-skewed (crash-prone) returns, high carry that compensates for tail risk, and contagion that breaks diversification in a crisis. Volatility understates the risk; the payoff is negatively skewed. NDFs cash-settle in a convertible currency the difference between the contracted and spot rates.
Quick reference — key formulas
The formulas worth memorizing
Domestic-currency return: R_DC = (1 + R_FC)(1 + R_FX) − 1
Domestic-currency risk: σ²(R_DC) ≈ σ²_FC + σ²_FX + 2ρσ_FCσ_FX
Hedge a long foreign exposure: SELL the currency forward; roll with FX swaps
Roll yield: forward-premium currency → costs to hedge; discount → earns
Carry trade: long high-yield, short low-yield (bet against UIP)
Minimum-variance hedge ratio: h* = ρ(R_DC,R_FX) × σ(R_DC)/σ(R_FX)
  (regression beta; can exceed 1 or be negative)
Cross/proxy hedge (correlated currency) ; macro hedge (whole portfolio)
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. Asset returns 8% locally; currency appreciates 3%. Domestic-currency return?
   -> (1.08)(1.03) − 1 = 11.24%.
2. σ_FC 15%, σ_FX 8%, ρ 0.30. Domestic-currency risk?
   -> √(0.0225 + 0.0064 + 0.0072) = √0.0361 = 19.0%.
3. Name three factors pushing toward MORE hedging.
   -> Short horizon, low risk tolerance, more foreign assets (also high correlation, low cost).
4. List the currency-management continuum.
   -> Passive → discretionary → active → overlay → currency-as-asset-class.
5. What is a carry trade and what does it bet against?
   -> Long high-yield, short low-yield currency; a bet against uncovered interest rate parity.
6. How do you hedge a long foreign-currency exposure, and how is the hedge rolled?
   -> Sell the currency forward; roll with an FX swap.
7. Does hedging a forward-discount currency earn or cost roll yield?
   -> Earns positive roll yield (a forward-premium currency costs).
8. Name two option structures that reduce hedging cost.
   -> Risk reversal (sell one side to fund the other) and a put spread (also OTM options, seagull).
9. Compute the minimum-variance hedge ratio: ρ 0.60, σ_DC 10%, σ_FX 8%.
   -> 0.60 × 10/8 = 0.75 (sell 75% forward).
10. What is a cross (proxy) hedge?
   -> Hedging an exposure with a correlated DIFFERENT currency (leaves basis risk).
11. Why is volatility a poor risk measure for EM currencies?
   -> Returns are negatively skewed and fat-tailed (crash-prone), so volatility understates tail risk.
12. How do you hedge a non-convertible EM currency?
   -> With non-deliverable forwards (NDFs) or a proxy/cross hedge.

readings.title: Index-Based Equity Strategies
readings.orientation: Orientation. How to gain equity-index exposure and why one might tilt away from pure market-cap weighting. Six learning outcomes (two are duplicates, so five distinct topics): comparing factor-based strategies with cap-weighted indexing, the vehicles for index exposure, the three portfolio-construction methods (full replication, stratified sampling, optimization), the causes and control of tracking error, and the sources of return and risk in an index portfolio. It is a more conceptual reading than the derivatives set, but the exam tests precise distinctions — the replication-method trade-offs and the tracking-error drivers above all. This note builds each with worked illustrations of tracking difference, cash drag, and securities-lending offsets. Learn the three construction methods, the tracking-error causes and fixes, and the cap-weight-versus-factor comparison cold.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Equity Portfolio Management
Reading: Index-Based Equity Strategies
Orientation. How to gain equity-index exposure and why one might tilt away from pure market-cap weighting. Six learning outcomes (two are duplicates, so five distinct topics): comparing factor-based strategies with cap-weighted indexing, the vehicles for index exposure, the three portfolio-construction methods (full replication, stratified sampling, optimization), the causes and control of tracking error, and the sources of return and risk in an index portfolio. It is a more conceptual reading than the derivatives set, but the exam tests precise distinctions — the replication-method trade-offs and the tracking-error drivers above all. This note builds each with worked illustrations of tracking difference, cash drag, and securities-lending offsets. Learn the three construction methods, the tracking-error causes and fixes, and the cap-weight-versus-factor comparison cold.

LOS 1 — Factor-based strategies vs market-cap-weighted indexing
LOS: compare factor-based strategies to market-capitalization-weighted indexing
What you must do:  Contrast pure cap-weighting with factor ('smart beta') strategies and know the factor categories.
Market-cap weighting. A cap-weighted index holds each stock in proportion to its market value — it is the true market portfolio, is macro-consistent (everyone can hold it), self-rebalances as prices move (so it has low turnover and cost), and has ample capacity. Its criticism is that it overweights expensive/overvalued stocks and underweights cheap ones (a 'return drag' argument), giving no deliberate factor exposure.
Factor-based (smart beta) strategies. These weight stocks by something OTHER than market cap to capture a rewarded factor, accepting higher cost, turnover, and active risk in exchange for a targeted exposure. They fall into three groups:
	•	Return-oriented — tilt toward factors expected to earn a premium: value, size, momentum, quality, and dividend yield.
	•	Risk-oriented — target lower risk: low-volatility and minimum-variance strategies.
	•	Diversification-oriented — spread exposure more evenly: equal-weighting, fundamental weighting, and risk parity.
Factor strategies are a middle ground between passive and active: rules-based and transparent like indexing, but taking deliberate factor bets like active management. The trade-off versus cap-weighting is higher expected factor return (if the premium persists) against higher fees, turnover, capacity limits, and periods of underperformance when the factor is out of favor.
Alternative weighting schemes
The diversification- and risk-oriented strategies differ mainly in HOW they weight constituents:
	•	Equal-weighting — every constituent the same weight; tilts toward smaller and value names, with high turnover because it must rebalance as prices move.
	•	Fundamental weighting — weight by fundamentals (sales, earnings, book value, dividends) rather than price; breaking the link to market price gives a value tilt.
	•	Risk-weighting / risk parity — weight to equalize each holding's risk contribution, overweighting lower-volatility names.
	•	Minimum-variance — optimize for the lowest-risk portfolio, concentrating in low-volatility, low-correlation stocks.
All of these break the cap-weight link to price and therefore require periodic REBALANCING — which cap-weighting does not — adding turnover and cost. That extra cost is the practical price of the factor tilt.
The common equity factors
Table 1a — Widely used equity factors
Factor
Definition
Rationale for the premium
Value
Cheap on fundamentals (low P/B, P/E)
Risk premium and/or behavioral mispricing
Size
Smaller-cap stocks
Higher risk and illiquidity premium
Momentum
Recent winners
Underreaction / behavioral persistence
Quality
Profitable, low-debt, stable earnings
Quality firms are safer / mispriced
Low volatility
Lower-risk stocks
The low-volatility anomaly (leverage constraints, behavior)
Dividend yield
High dividend payers
Income and a quality/value proxy
Risks of smart beta. Factor strategies carry risks beyond the market: DATA MINING (a factor may be a historical artifact that fails out of sample), CROWDING (popular factors get bid up, eroding the premium), FACTOR CYCLICALITY (long stretches of underperformance when the factor is out of favor), and higher TURNOVER, cost, and capacity limits. Factor TIMING is notoriously difficult, so most implementations hold factors through the cycle rather than rotating among them.
Risks and criticisms of cap-weighted indexing
Cap-weighting has drawbacks the exam expects you to know. It mechanically OVERWEIGHTS the largest and most richly-valued stocks, so a market dominated by a few mega-caps leaves the index concentrated and heavily exposed to those names; in a bubble, cap-weighting buys MORE of the inflating sector as it rises. It also forces the purchase of newly-added (already-appreciated) names at reconstitution. And as passive ownership has grown, questions have arisen about STEWARDSHIP — whether large passive owners adequately monitor and vote the shares they are obliged to hold. These criticisms are the motivation behind factor and alternative-weighting approaches.
ESG and thematic index strategies
Index strategies increasingly embed ESG and thematic tilts: exclusionary screens (dropping certain sectors), best-in-class ESG weighting, or thematic indexes (clean energy, technology). These remain rules-based like standard indexing but introduce active risk versus the broad market and carry the same trade-offs as factor strategies — potential alignment with values or a theme against higher tracking error, narrower diversification, and possible added cost. They blur the line between 'index' and 'active,' because the index itself now embeds a deliberate tilt.
Measuring factor exposures
A factor strategy's tilts are quantified with a FACTOR MODEL that regresses returns on factor exposures, yielding the portfolio's loading (beta) on each factor. This lets a manager verify that a 'value' fund actually has positive value exposure and intended (or neutral) exposures elsewhere, and lets an investor compare strategies on their true factor content rather than their labels — important because two funds with the same name can carry very different exposures. Return-oriented factors are held for their expected premium; risk-oriented tilts such as low-volatility are held to reduce risk even when their long-run return is similar to the market's — a distinction that shapes what the investor should reasonably expect.
Exam focus: Cap-weighting = the market portfolio: macro-consistent, self-rebalancing, low-cost, high-capacity, but overweights the overvalued (concentration, bubble participation, forced reconstitution buying, stewardship concerns). Factor/smart-beta = rules-based tilts in three families — return- (value/size/momentum/quality/yield), risk- (low-vol/min-variance), diversification-oriented (equal-weight/fundamental/risk parity); quantify tilts with a factor model, not labels. Know smart-beta risks (data mining, crowding, cyclicality, cost) and that ESG/thematic indexes embed active tilts.
LOS 2 & 3 — Approaches to index-based equity exposure
LOS: compare different approaches to index-based equity strategies (vehicles and management)
What you must do:  Compare the vehicles for obtaining index exposure and when each fits.
Table 1 — Vehicles for index exposure
Approach
Features / when to use
Open-end index mutual fund
Simple, pooled, priced once daily; broad access, low cost
Exchange-traded fund (ETF)
Intraday trading, in-kind creation/redemption → tax efficiency and low cost; flexible
Separately managed index account
Direct ownership, customization, tax-lot control; for larger investors
Index derivatives (futures, swaps)
Fast, cheap, temporary exposure; equitize cash, tactical tilts, overlays
ETFs and index mutual funds are the workhorses for most investors; ETFs add intraday liquidity and, through in-kind creation/redemption, greater tax efficiency. Separately managed accounts suit large investors wanting customization and tax control. Index derivatives are best for TEMPORARY or efficient exposure — equitizing cash, expressing a short-term tilt, or overlaying an allocation — rather than a permanent holding, because they must be rolled. Exposure can also be managed INTERNALLY or delegated to an EXTERNAL manager.
Internal vs external management
A final structural choice is whether to run the index portfolio INTERNALLY or hire an EXTERNAL manager. Large asset owners with scale and expertise may index in-house to save fees and retain control; others delegate to a specialist index manager whose trading systems and securities-lending programs deliver low tracking error at low cost. The decision weighs the internal cost and capability against the external fee — and because passive mandates are often so cheap and efficient externally, outsourcing is common even for sizable investors. This mirrors the general buy-versus-build question in asset management.
ETF mechanics and costs
An ETF's price stays close to its net asset value through an ARBITRAGE mechanism: authorized participants create new ETF shares by delivering the underlying basket, or redeem shares for the basket, whenever the ETF trades at a premium or discount to NAV — pushing the price back toward NAV. This in-kind process is also what gives ETFs their tax efficiency, because gains are not realized inside the fund. Costs to weigh across vehicles include the expense ratio, trading costs (the bid-ask spread and any premium/discount for an ETF; none for a mutual fund transacted at NAV), and the tax consequences of the structure. For a large institution, a separately managed account or a direct futures position can be cheaper than any fund wrapper.
Exam focus: Match the vehicle: mutual fund/ETF for broad low-cost access (ETFs add intraday trading and tax efficiency via in-kind creation/redemption, which also keeps price near NAV); SMA for customization and tax-lot control (larger investors); derivatives for temporary/efficient exposure (equitize cash, overlays), since they roll. Weigh expense ratio, trading cost/premium-discount, and tax structure.
LOS 3b — Choosing the index (benchmark)
The starting point for any passive strategy is selecting the index itself, because a fund can only be as good as its benchmark. A good index is rules-based and transparent, investable and replicable, FLOAT-ADJUSTED (weighting only freely tradable shares, not closely-held stakes), broadly representative of the target market, and reconstituted on a clear, predictable schedule. The index-construction choices — the universe covered, the weighting scheme, float adjustment, and reconstitution frequency — directly affect the fund's turnover, capacity, and the exposures the investor ends up holding. The index decision is therefore itself a portfolio decision, not a mere administrative choice.
Exam focus: A good index for passive investing is rules-based, transparent, investable/replicable, float-adjusted, representative, and reconstituted on a clear schedule. The index's construction (universe, weighting, float adjustment, reconstitution) drives the fund's turnover, capacity, and exposures — so selecting the benchmark is a portfolio decision.
LOS 4 — Construction: replication, sampling, optimization
LOS: compare full replication, stratified sampling, and optimization approaches for constructing index-based equity portfolios
What you must do:  Compare the three construction methods on accuracy, cost, and data needs.
Table 2 — Index-construction methods
Method
How it works
Trade-offs
Full replication
Hold ALL constituents at index weights
Lowest tracking error; best for liquid, few-name indexes; costly/impractical for large or illiquid indexes
Stratified sampling
Divide the index into cells (sector, size, style) and hold a representative sample matching cell weights
Cheaper, handles large/illiquid indexes; higher tracking error; no covariance modeling
Optimization
Use a factor/risk model to minimize tracking error subject to constraints
Accounts for covariances → efficient; but model-dependent (misestimation risk) and needs data
The choice is a trade-off among tracking accuracy, cost, and complexity. FULL REPLICATION gives the tightest tracking and is ideal for a liquid index with a manageable number of names (e.g., a large-cap index), but it is expensive or impossible for an index with thousands of illiquid constituents. STRATIFIED SAMPLING and OPTIMIZATION hold a subset that mimics the index's characteristics — sampling by matching cell weights, optimization by explicitly minimizing modeled tracking error using the covariance structure. Optimization is the most sophisticated but depends on the quality of the risk model, so a mis-estimated model can raise realized tracking error.
The risk model behind optimization
Optimization relies on a FACTOR RISK MODEL — a statistical description of how stocks co-move that decomposes each stock's risk into common factor exposures (market, industry, style) and stock-specific risk, with a covariance matrix estimated from history. The optimizer uses this to build the smallest-tracking-error portfolio for a given number of holdings and set of constraints. But the method is only as good as the model: if the estimated covariances or factor exposures are wrong — they are backward-looking and can shift — the realized tracking error can exceed the model's forecast. That model dependency is the key limitation of optimization versus straightforward replication.
Choosing a construction method in practice
In practice the method follows the index's characteristics. A liquid, concentrated index — a large-cap benchmark of a few hundred names — is usually FULLY REPLICATED, since the tracking benefit outweighs the modest cost. A broad or illiquid index — a total-market or small-cap benchmark of thousands of names, some barely traded — is better served by STRATIFIED SAMPLING or OPTIMIZATION, which capture the index's risk characteristics while avoiding the cost and market impact of trading illiquid names. Optimization is preferred when a good risk model exists and the manager wants to control specific factor exposures; sampling is simpler and more transparent. Managers often BLEND the two — fully replicating the liquid large-cap portion and sampling the illiquid tail.
Illustration — holdings vs tracking error
An index has 2,000 constituents:
Full replication (2,000 names): ~0 sampling tracking error, HIGH trading cost
200-name stratified sample: much lower cost, tracking error ≈ 0.5%
500-name optimized sample: tracking error ≈ 0.2% at moderate cost
More names lower tracking error but raise cost; the manager picks the point on this trade-off that meets the tracking-error tolerance at the lowest cost, depending on the index's liquidity and concentration.
Exam focus: Full replication = all names at index weights → lowest tracking error, best for liquid/small indexes, costly for large/illiquid. Stratified sampling = match cell (sector/size) weights with a sample → cheaper, higher tracking error, no covariances. Optimization = risk-model minimization of tracking error using covariances → efficient but model-dependent. More holdings cut tracking error but raise cost — choose the least-cost point that meets the TE tolerance.
LOS 5 — Tracking error: causes and control
LOS: discuss potential causes of tracking error and methods to control tracking error for index-based equity portfolios
What you must do:  List the sources of tracking error and how to reduce each.
Tracking error (the volatility of the portfolio's return relative to the index) arises from any deviation between the portfolio and the index. Its main causes:
	•	Fees and expenses — management fees and administrative costs drag the return below the index.
	•	Transaction costs — commissions and market impact from rebalancing and from index changes.
	•	Cash drag — uninvested cash (for liquidity, dividends awaiting reinvestment) underperforms in a rising market.
	•	Sampling / optimization error — holding a subset that does not perfectly match the index.
	•	Index reconstitution — costs and timing mismatches when the index adds/removes names.
	•	Dividend reinvestment timing and taxes — differences in when dividends are received/reinvested and any withholding taxes.
Setting a tracking-error target
How much tracking error is acceptable depends on the mandate. A pure passive mandate targets the tightest feasible tracking error — often from a few basis points to a few tenths of a percent annually — accepting higher trading cost to minimize deviation. A sampled or 'passive-plus' mandate tolerates more tracking error in exchange for lower cost. The asset owner and manager set an explicit tracking-error BUDGET in the mandate, and the manager then chooses the construction method, number of holdings, rebalancing frequency, and cash management needed to stay within it at the lowest cost. Ongoing reporting compares realized tracking error against that target.
Worked illustration — tracking difference
An index returns 10.0% over the year; the fund returns 9.7%.
Tracking difference = 9.7% − 10.0% = −0.3%
  ≈ fees (0.10%) + transaction/sampling (0.25%) − securities lending (0.05%)
The −0.3% shortfall is mostly fees and trading; securities-lending income partly offsets it. (Tracking ERROR is the volatility of this difference over time, not its level.)
Rebalancing and corporate actions
Beyond scheduled reconstitution, an index portfolio must respond to ongoing CORPORATE ACTIONS — dividends, stock splits, mergers, spin-offs, rights issues — that change index composition or weights, and to the daily cash flows into and out of the fund. Each requires trading that can add tracking error and cost if not handled promptly and cheaply. Efficient index managers automate these adjustments, net trades against inflows and outflows, and use futures to keep incoming cash invested — all to keep the portfolio aligned with the index at minimal cost.
Securities lending and index reconstitution
Two practical levers deserve detail. SECURITIES LENDING — lending index holdings to short-sellers for a fee — generates income that OFFSETS fund costs and can shrink the tracking difference, but it introduces counterparty and collateral-reinvestment risk that must be managed. INDEX RECONSTITUTION — when the index provider adds or removes constituents — forces the fund to trade, incurring costs; and because reconstitution dates are known in advance, it exposes the fund to front-running (the prices of added names rise before the fund buys them). Managers mitigate this with careful, sometimes staggered, trading around reconstitution rather than trading everything on the effective date.
Worked example — total cost of ownership
Two funds track the same index. Fund A: expense ratio 0.05% + trading/other 0.03% − securities-lending income 0.03%. Fund B: expense ratio 0.03% + trading/other 0.06% − lending 0.00%.
Fund A net drag = 0.05 + 0.03 − 0.03 = 0.05%
Fund B net drag = 0.03 + 0.06 − 0.00 = 0.09%
Despite B's lower headline expense ratio, Fund A is cheaper to OWN (0.05% vs 0.09% drag) thanks to lower trading costs and securities-lending income. Total cost of ownership — not the expense ratio alone — drives the tracking difference.
Controlling tracking error: minimize fees and trading costs, use index FUTURES to equitize cash (removing cash drag), rebalance promptly and cost-effectively around index reconstitutions, reinvest dividends quickly, and earn SECURITIES-LENDING income to offset costs. Full replication (where feasible) minimizes sampling error.
Watch out: Distinguish tracking DIFFERENCE (the return gap, portfolio minus index, over a period — a level) from tracking ERROR (the standard deviation of that difference over time — a volatility). Fees create a persistent negative tracking difference; sampling/optimization mismatch creates tracking-error volatility. Cash drag hurts in up markets but helps in down markets — its expected effect is negative because equities rise on average.
Worked example — annualizing tracking error
A fund's MONTHLY active returns (fund minus index) have a standard deviation of 0.15%.
Annualized tracking error = 0.15% × √12 = 0.15% × 3.464 = 0.52%
Tracking error is annualized by multiplying the monthly figure by √12. A 0.52% tracking error means the fund's annual return typically lands within about ±0.52% of the index (one standard deviation).
Exam focus: Causes of tracking error: fees, transaction costs, cash drag, sampling/optimization error, index reconstitution, dividend-timing and taxes. Control by cutting fees/costs, equitizing cash with futures, prompt low-cost rebalancing, fast dividend reinvestment, and securities lending. Know tracking DIFFERENCE (level) vs tracking ERROR (volatility, annualized by ×√12).
LOS 6 — Sources of return and risk
LOS: explain sources of return and risk to an index-based equity portfolio
What you must do:  Decompose the return and identify the risks of an index portfolio.
Sources of return. The dominant source is simply the INDEX return (market beta). Around it, the portfolio's actual return is adjusted by the tracking difference: MINUS fees and transaction costs and cash drag, PLUS any securities-lending income. A well-run index fund's return should closely equal the index return minus a small, stable net cost.
Portfolio return ≈ index return − fees − costs − cash drag + securities-lending income
Sources of risk. The primary risk is the index's own MARKET (systematic) risk — an index fund is fully exposed to equity-market moves and offers no downside protection. On top of that sits TRACKING ERROR (active risk relative to the index) from the causes above. For a FACTOR-based index strategy, there is additional FACTOR risk — the strategy underperforms the cap-weighted market when its chosen factor is out of favor, and it bears the factor's specific drawdowns.
Table 3 — Passive vs factor vs active
Dimension
Cap-weighted passive
Factor (smart beta)
Active
Return source
Market beta
Factor premia
Manager skill (alpha)
Cost / fees
Lowest
Moderate
Highest
Turnover
Low (self-rebalancing)
Moderate (rebalancing)
Variable, often high
Active risk
~None
Moderate (factor risk)
High (idiosyncratic)
Transparency
High
High (rules-based)
Often low
Capacity
Very high
High but factor-limited
Often constrained
Worked illustration — cash drag
A fund holds 2% in cash (earning ≈ 0%) in a year the equity index returns 10%.
Cash drag ≈ 2% × (10% − 0%) = 0.20% underperformance
Holding 2% out of the market cost about 0.20% of relative return. Equitizing the cash with index futures (buying futures on the 2%) would capture the equity return and remove the drag.
The risks of index investing
An index fund's defining feature is also its main risk: it is FULLY exposed to the market and offers NO downside protection — it falls as much as the index in a bear market, by design. It also inherits whatever concentration the index carries, such as a few dominant stocks or sectors. Relative to the benchmark the residual is tracking error, which a well-run fund keeps small. Unlike an active strategy, an index fund makes no attempt to avoid overvalued segments, so investors bear the full consequences of any market-wide mispricing — the flip side of the low-cost, reliable market exposure it provides.
Index investing across asset classes and regions
The same index-based approach extends beyond a single equity market. Investors build global equity exposure by combining regional or country index funds, or by holding one broad global-index fund, and can tilt regional weights actively while indexing within each region. Other asset classes bring their own index challenges — bond indexes are far harder to replicate fully because of the enormous number of issues and thin trading, the theme of the liability-driven and index-based fixed-income reading. Within equities an investor might index the large-cap core, sample the small-cap and emerging-market portions, and add factor or active satellites, assembling a whole-portfolio structure from index building blocks. The unifying idea is that low-cost, rules-based index exposure is the default building block, with active or factor tilts layered on only where justified.
The growth and market impact of passive
Passive investing's rise has prompted debate about its market effects. Proponents note it has driven fees down and improved investor outcomes broadly. Critics raise concerns that heavy indexing could dampen PRICE DISCOVERY (fewer active investors setting prices), amplify INDEX-INCLUSION effects (added stocks jump and deleted stocks fall as passive money follows), and concentrate corporate ownership and voting in a few large index providers. The prevailing view is that markets remain efficient enough for indexing to work well, but these are live governance and market-structure questions the exam may reference.
Why passive? The efficient-markets argument
The case for indexing rests on market efficiency and simple arithmetic: in aggregate, active investors collectively hold the market and, as a group, must underperform the index by their costs (Sharpe's 'arithmetic of active management'). Because identifying in advance the minority of active managers with genuine skill is hard, and their fees are high, capturing the market return cheaply through an index is a rational default for most investors. Active management is justified only where an investor has real reason to expect skill NET of fees — which is why passive investing has grown to command a large share of assets.
The role of index investing
Index investing is the low-cost CORE of most portfolios: it captures the market return reliably, at minimal fee and turnover, with broad diversification and full transparency — and the empirical difficulty of beating the market after costs is the central argument for it. Investors often combine a passive index CORE with active or factor SATELLITES (the core-satellite approach), concentrating their fee budget and active-risk budget where they believe skill exists while holding the rest passively. Index strategies also serve as efficient building blocks for asset allocation and for temporary exposure through ETFs and futures.
Exam focus: Return ≈ index return − fees − costs − cash drag + securities-lending income (market beta dominates). Risks: the index's market risk (full downside exposure, no protection) PLUS tracking error; factor strategies add factor risk (underperform when the factor is out of favor). Cash drag ≈ cash weight × (equity return − cash return). Indexing is the low-cost core; combine with active/factor satellites (core-satellite).
Quick reference — key concepts
The essentials worth memorizing
Cap-weighting = market portfolio: macro-consistent, self-rebalancing, low-cost
Smart beta = return- / risk- / diversification-oriented factor tilts
Vehicles: index fund, ETF (intraday, tax-efficient), SMA (custom), derivatives (temporary)
Construction: full replication (lowest TE) / stratified sampling / optimization (covariances)
Tracking-error causes: fees, costs, cash drag, sampling, reconstitution, dividend timing/taxes
