Exam focus: LDI risks: non-parallel shifts (structural risk), model/measurement error, rebalancing (duration drift), spread/credit risk (non-Treasury assets; liability discounted at a credit rate can diverge from risk-free assets), liquidity, and counterparty risk (derivatives). Duration matching protects only against parallel shifts — monitor curve/key-rate exposures and convexity, and stress-test twists. Monitor via the funded ratio; split hedging vs return-seeking assets by funded status.
LOS 5 — Bond indexes and the challenge of mimicking them
LOS: discuss bond indexes and the challenges of managing a fixed-income portfolio to mimic the characteristics of a bond index
What you must do:  Explain why bond indexes are hard to replicate and the 'bums' problem.
Replicating a bond index is far harder than an equity index:
	•	Enormous number of issues — a broad bond index holds thousands of securities, far more than a stock index, many issued by the same borrower across maturities.
	•	Illiquidity — many bonds trade rarely, so buying the exact index weights is impractical and expensive.
	•	Constant turnover — bonds mature, get called, are downgraded (out of the index), and new issues enter, so the index changes continuously.
	•	The 'bums' problem — a market-cap (issuance-weighted) bond index gives the LARGEST weights to the most heavily-indebted issuers, because they issue the most debt — a perverse risk characteristic, concentrating exposure in the biggest borrowers.
Because full replication is impractical, bond index funds use STRATIFIED SAMPLING / cell matching — matching the index's exposures to duration, sector, credit quality, and key-rate buckets with a manageable subset of liquid bonds (enhanced indexing).
Enhanced indexing and matching key-rate durations
Because full replication is impossible, bond index managers practice ENHANCED INDEXING: they sample the index to match its key risk exposures — effective duration, KEY-RATE durations across the curve, sector and quality weights, and convexity — while substituting more liquid bonds for illiquid ones and taking small, controlled tilts to add value. Tracking error is contained by keeping these exposures close to the index's. Matching KEY-RATE durations, not just overall duration, matters for bonds because the yield curve reshapes: two portfolios with the same overall duration can behave very differently under a twist, so cell/key-rate matching controls the curve risk that a single duration number misses.
Types and construction of bond indexes
Bond indexes span many categories — aggregate (broad investment-grade), government, corporate, high-yield, mortgage-backed, and global — each with its own liquidity and replication challenges. Most are ISSUANCE-weighted, which creates the bums problem; some providers offer alternatively-weighted indexes (GDP-weighted or fundamentally-weighted) to address it. Index-construction rules — the eligible maturity and credit range, the minimum issue size, and the reconstitution frequency — determine turnover and investability, so the manager must understand them both to track the index efficiently and to judge whether it is an appropriate benchmark.
Exam focus: Bond-index replication is hard: thousands of issues, many illiquid; constant turnover (maturities, calls, downgrades, new issues); and the BUMS problem — issuance-weighted indexes overweight the most-indebted issuers. Indexes come in many types (aggregate, government, corporate, high-yield, MBS, global); construction rules drive turnover/investability. Funds use stratified sampling / enhanced indexing — matching duration, KEY-RATE durations, sector, quality, convexity — not full replication.
LOS 6 — Passive bond-market exposure methods
LOS: compare alternative methods for establishing bond market exposure passively
What you must do:  Compare the ways to gain passive bond exposure.
Table 1 — Passive bond-exposure methods
Method
Notes
Full replication
Hold all index bonds — impractical for bonds (too many, illiquid); rarely used
Stratified sampling / enhanced indexing
Match cells (duration, sector, quality, key-rate) with liquid bonds — the standard approach
Bond ETF / index mutual fund
Simple, low-cost pooled exposure; intraday for ETFs
Derivatives (total-return swap, futures)
Receive the index return via a TRS, or use bond futures — efficient, capital-light, must be rolled
The choice trades cost, tracking accuracy, and size. Stratified sampling is the practical workhorse; ETFs/index funds suit smaller investors; a total-return swap or futures gives efficient, temporary, or capital-light exposure. Full replication is essentially never used for broad bond indexes.
Choosing among passive methods
The passive method follows the investor's size, cost sensitivity, and tracking-error tolerance. A SMALL investor is best served by a bond ETF or index fund — instant diversification at low cost, though with the fund's fee and some tracking error. A LARGE institution can run a separately managed stratified-sampling portfolio, controlling cost and customization. A TOTAL-RETURN SWAP delivers the index return with little capital and is easy to unwind, useful for temporary or overlay exposure, but carries counterparty risk and must be rolled. Bond FUTURES provide liquid duration exposure but cover only part of the index (mainly government duration). Most large passive bond mandates combine a sampled physical portfolio with a derivatives overlay to fine-tune duration and manage cash flows.
Exam focus: Passive bond exposure: full replication (impractical for bonds), stratified sampling/enhanced indexing (the standard), bond ETFs/index funds (simple, low-cost — best for small investors), and derivatives (total-return swap to receive the index return, or futures for duration — efficient, must be rolled). Choose by size, cost, and tracking-error tolerance; large mandates blend a sampled portfolio with a derivatives overlay.
LOS 7 — Selecting and justifying a benchmark
LOS: discuss criteria for selecting a benchmark and justify the selection of a benchmark
What you must do:  State the valid-benchmark criteria and match the benchmark to the mandate.
A valid benchmark should be:
	•	Unambiguous — its constituents and weights are clearly defined.
	•	Investable — one could passively hold it.
	•	Measurable — its return can be calculated readily.
	•	Appropriate — consistent with the manager's style/mandate.
	•	Reflective of current investment opinions — the manager has views on its constituents.
	•	Specified in advance — set before the evaluation period.
	•	Accountable / owned — the manager accepts it.
Matching benchmark to mandate. For a LIABILITY-driven mandate, the benchmark is the LIABILITY itself (a liability-relative benchmark) — the portfolio is measured against how well it meets the liabilities, not a market index. For a TOTAL-RETURN mandate, an appropriate market bond INDEX is the benchmark. Justify the choice by the objective: the benchmark must represent what the portfolio is actually trying to achieve.
Liability-relative benchmarking in practice
Measuring against the liability changes the objective from beating a market index to minimizing the volatility of the SURPLUS (assets minus liabilities) and ensuring the liabilities are met. Techniques include surplus optimization (mean-variance on the surplus rather than assets alone) and a hedging/return-seeking split whose hedging sleeve is benchmarked to the liabilities and whose return-seeking sleeve is benchmarked to market indexes. The unifying principle holds: the benchmark must represent the true objective, which for these mandates is the liability, not the market.
Exam focus: Valid-benchmark criteria: unambiguous, investable, measurable, appropriate, reflective of current opinions, specified in advance, accountable/owned. Match to mandate: liability-driven → the LIABILITY is the benchmark (minimize surplus volatility, meet liabilities); total-return → a market bond index. Justify by the objective.
Worked case — LDI for an underfunded pension
Consider a pension with liabilities of PV 500,000,000 (BPV 900,000) and assets of 450,000,000 — a funded ratio of 90%, a 50,000,000 deficit. The reading's tools combine as follows:
	•	Hedging sleeve — allocate most assets to a liability-matched bond portfolio, matching PV and BPV to the liabilities so funded-ratio volatility from rate moves is minimized. With only 450M of assets against 500M of liabilities, a derivatives overlay (receive-fixed swaps or long bond futures) can raise the assets' BPV toward the liabilities' 900,000 without needing the full 500M in bonds — capital-efficient liability hedging.
	•	Return-seeking sleeve — allocate a portion to growth assets (equities, credit) to earn the extra return needed to close the 50M deficit over time.
	•	Monitoring and glide path — track the funded ratio; as it improves, shift assets from return-seeking into the hedging sleeve (a de-risking 'glide path'), locking in the better funding.
This illustrates the whole reading: match BPV to hedge the liabilities, use derivatives for capital efficiency, and split hedging versus return-seeking by funded status, de-risking along a glide path as funding improves.
Comparing the liability strategies
Table 2 — Liability-management strategies at a glance
Strategy
Rate-risk protection
Rebalancing
Cost / flexibility
Cash-flow matching
Immune to ANY shift
None needed
Costly, inflexible
Single-liability immunization
Parallel shifts (bullet best)
Periodic (duration drift)
Moderate
Multiple-liability duration match
Parallel shifts (bracket + dispersion)
Periodic
Flexible, cheaper
Contingent immunization
Floor via safety-net; active upside
Active + switch at trigger
Flexible, with a floor
The progression trades protection for cost and flexibility: cash-flow matching is the safest and most expensive, immunization and duration matching accept parallel-shift protection for lower cost, and contingent immunization layers active upside on top of an immunized floor. The right choice depends on the surplus, the predictability of the liabilities, and risk tolerance.
Quick reference — key conditions
The conditions worth memorizing
Single liability: (1) PV(A) ≥ PV(L), (2) MacDur(A) = horizon, (3) minimize dispersion/convexity
Multiple liabilities: PV(A)=PV(L), BPV(A)=BPV(L), disp(A) ≥ disp(L) AND assets bracket L
BPV = money duration × 0.0001 = mod duration × PV × 0.0001
Cash-flow matching = exact match, no rate risk, no rebalancing, but costly/restrictive
Horizon matching = cash-flow-match near-term + duration-match long-term
Contingent immunization: cushion = assets − PV(liability at safety-net rate)
Immunization protects parallel shifts; low convexity minimizes twist (structural) risk
Bond index: bums problem (issuance-weighted → most-indebted issuers overweighted)
Benchmark: liability-driven → liability; total-return → market index
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. State the three single-liability immunization conditions.
   -> PV(A) ≥ PV(L); Macaulay duration of assets = horizon; minimize dispersion/convexity.
2. Why set Macaulay duration equal to the horizon?
   -> So price risk and reinvestment risk offset for a parallel shift, fixing the horizon value.
3. Bullet or barbell for immunization, and why?
   -> Bullet — lower dispersion/convexity means less exposure to non-parallel (twist) shifts.
4. Give the three multiple-liability duration-matching conditions.
   -> PV(A)=PV(L); BPV(A)=BPV(L); asset dispersion ≥ liability dispersion and assets bracket the liabilities.
5. What is cash-flow matching, and its main drawback?
   -> Building cash flows that exactly match the liabilities (no rate risk); drawback = restrictive and costly.
6. Liability 10M in 5 yrs, safety-net rate 4%, portfolio 9M. Cushion?
   -> PV(L) = 10M/1.04^5 = 8.22M; cushion = 9.0M − 8.22M = 0.78M.
7. When does contingent immunization switch to pure immunization?
   -> When the portfolio value falls to the amount needed to immunize the liability (the cushion is gone).
8. Why can assets and liabilities diverge even with matched duration?
   -> Non-parallel shifts, and spread/credit differences (liabilities discounted at a credit rate vs risk-free assets).
9. What is the bond-index 'bums' problem?
   -> Issuance-weighted indexes overweight the most-indebted issuers (they issue the most debt).
10. How do bond index funds handle the impracticality of full replication?
   -> Stratified sampling / cell matching by duration, sector, quality, and key-rate buckets.
11. Name two ways to get passive bond exposure via derivatives.
   -> A total-return swap (receive the index return) and bond futures.
12. What is the benchmark for a liability-driven mandate?
   -> The liability itself (a liability-relative benchmark), not a market index.
13. Why is a zero-coupon bond at the horizon the ideal immunizing asset?
   -> Its duration equals its maturity, it has no reinvestment risk, and its single cash flow matches the liability (zero dispersion).
14. Why match KEY-RATE durations, not just overall duration, for a bond index?
   -> Two portfolios with equal overall duration can behave very differently under a curve twist; key-rate matching controls curve risk.
15. How is a cash-flow-matched portfolio built?
   -> Backward from the last liability, using each bond's principal and coupons to cover each year's payment.
16. For a pension, what are the hedging and return-seeking portfolios?
   -> The hedging sleeve is matched to the liabilities; the return-seeking sleeve (benchmarked to market indexes) aims to close a deficit or grow surplus.
17. Blend a 5-yr bond (dur 4.5) and 10-yr (dur 8.0) to duration 7.0 — weights?
   -> w(10-yr) = 2.5/3.5 = 0.71; ~71% in the 10-year, 29% in the 5-year.
18. Why does a barbell carry more structural risk than a bullet at the same duration?
   -> Higher dispersion/convexity → greater sensitivity to non-parallel (twist) curve moves.
19. Name the seven valid-benchmark criteria (short).
   -> Unambiguous, investable, measurable, appropriate, reflective of current opinions, specified in advance, accountable/owned.

readings.title: Yield Curve Strategies
readings.orientation: Orientation. How to position a bond portfolio for expected yield-curve moves. Seven learning outcomes: decomposing bond return from yield changes, positioning when your view MATCHES the forwards (no active bet), positioning when it DIVERGES on level/slope/shape, positioning for a change in VOLATILITY (convexity), evaluating sensitivity with KEY-RATE DURATIONS, curve strategies ACROSS CURRENCIES, and the expected-return/risk of a curve strategy. The return decomposition and the duration/convexity price-change formula are the calculation core, worked below, together with the bullet-vs-barbell and key-rate-duration analysis. Learn the return decomposition, the level/slope/shape position map, and the convexity trade-off cold.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Fixed-Income Portfolio Management
Reading: Yield Curve Strategies
Orientation. How to position a bond portfolio for expected yield-curve moves. Seven learning outcomes: decomposing bond return from yield changes, positioning when your view MATCHES the forwards (no active bet), positioning when it DIVERGES on level/slope/shape, positioning for a change in VOLATILITY (convexity), evaluating sensitivity with KEY-RATE DURATIONS, curve strategies ACROSS CURRENCIES, and the expected-return/risk of a curve strategy. The return decomposition and the duration/convexity price-change formula are the calculation core, worked below, together with the bullet-vs-barbell and key-rate-duration analysis. Learn the return decomposition, the level/slope/shape position map, and the convexity trade-off cold.

LOS 1 — Decomposing bond return from yield changes
LOS: describe the factors affecting fixed-income portfolio returns due to a change in benchmark yields
What you must do:  Break total return into its components and apply the duration/convexity price formula.
A bond's total return over a horizon decomposes into these pieces:
	•	Yield income — coupon plus reinvestment income; roughly the annual coupon divided by the current price.
	•	Rolldown return — as the bond ages, it 'rolls down' an upward-sloping curve to a lower yield, so its price rises (assuming the curve is unchanged).
	•	Price change from the rate view — the gain/loss if yields move as expected, via duration and convexity.
	•	Less credit losses and currency effects — for credit and foreign-currency bonds.
Rolling yield = yield income + rolldown return   (return if the curve is UNCHANGED)
ΔPrice% ≈ −MD × Δy + ½ × Convexity × (Δy)²
E(total return) ≈ yield income + rolldown + [−MD × Δy + ½C(Δy)²] − credit − currency
Worked example — return decomposition
Yield income 3.0%, rolldown 0.5%. The manager expects yields to FALL 0.2% (Δy = −0.002). Modified duration 6, convexity 50.
Price change from view = −6 × (−0.002) + ½ × 50 × (−0.002)²
  = 0.012 + 0.5 × 50 × 0.000004 = 0.012 + 0.0001 = 1.21%
E(total return) = 3.0% + 0.5% + 1.21% = 4.71%
The rolling yield (3.5%) is the return if the curve stays put; the +1.21% is the additional gain from the expected rate fall (mostly duration, a sliver from convexity).
From a single bond to the portfolio
The decomposition applies to a PORTFOLIO as well as a single bond, using the portfolio's aggregate duration and convexity (the market-value-weighted averages of its holdings). A portfolio's expected return under a yield-curve view is the sum of its positions' yield income and rolldown plus the price change implied by its duration and convexity — and, when the move is non-parallel, its key-rate durations. This is why active curve management is expressed through the portfolio's DURATION, CONVEXITY, and KEY-RATE profile relative to the benchmark: those three exposures determine how the portfolio's return will differ from the benchmark's under any curve move.
What moves the yield curve
Empirically, yield-curve movements decompose into three factors: LEVEL (a roughly parallel shift, the largest share of variance), SLOPE (short vs long rates diverging — steepening or flattening), and CURVATURE (the middle moving relative to the wings — humps). Level shifts are driven by the general level of rates (policy, inflation expectations); slope changes by the monetary-policy cycle (short rates react to the central bank, long rates to growth/inflation expectations); curvature by shifts in the expected policy path and supply/demand at particular maturities. A curve strategy is ultimately a bet on one or more of these three factors versus what the forwards already imply.
Exam focus: Total return = yield income + rolldown return (+ these two = rolling yield, the unchanged-curve return) + price change from the rate view (−MD×Δy + ½C(Δy)²) − credit losses − currency. Curve moves decompose into LEVEL (parallel, most variance), SLOPE, and CURVATURE; a strategy bets on these vs the forwards. Memorize ΔPrice% ≈ −MD×Δy + ½C(Δy)².
A framework: static vs active curve strategies
Curve strategies range from STATIC (no view, capture the curve as it is) to ACTIVE (express a rate or curve view):
	•	Buy-and-hold / laddered — no view; capture yield income across maturities.
	•	Roll-down (ride the curve) — hold longer bonds to capture rolldown; a bet on curve stability.
	•	Carry — earn the spread between funding and holding, or across markets.
	•	Duration positioning — bet on the LEVEL of rates (lengthen/shorten).
	•	Curve positioning — steepener/flattener (slope) and bullet/barbell/butterfly (shape).
	•	Convexity positioning — bet on the level of interest-rate volatility.
The learning outcomes map onto this framework: LOS 2 covers the static/no-view case, LOS 3 the level/slope/shape active bets, and LOS 4 the volatility bet — all evaluated with the LOS 1 return decomposition.
LOS 2 — When your view matches the forwards
LOS: formulate a portfolio positioning strategy given forward interest rates and an interest rate view that coincides with the market view
What you must do:  Explain why matching the forwards means no active bet adds value.
Forward rates are the market's implied expectation of future rates. If your view EQUALS the forwards (you agree with the market), then no duration or curve bet can be expected to add value: if forward rates are REALIZED, every bond earns the same short-rate return over the horizon, so lengthening or shortening duration changes risk without changing expected return. The right posture is a NEUTRAL one — match the benchmark's duration and curve exposure (buy-and-hold or a passive/laddered structure).
Riding the yield curve. A related idea: 'riding' (rolling down) the curve — buying a longer bond and holding as it rolls down to a lower yield — earns the rolldown return and OUTPERFORMS a short bond ONLY IF the curve stays static, i.e., forwards are NOT realized (rates do not rise as the forwards imply). If forwards are realized, riding the curve earns no extra return. So the rolldown strategy is really a bet that the curve is more stable than the forwards imply.
Worked example — rolldown return
A 5-year bond yields 3.0%; the 4-year point on the upward-sloping curve yields 2.7%. Over one year, if the curve is unchanged, the bond becomes a 4-year bond yielding 2.7%.
Yield declines 0.30% → price rises ≈ duration × 0.30%
If duration ≈ 4: rolldown ≈ 4 × 0.30% = 1.2%
Rolling yield = yield income 3.0% + rolldown 1.2% ≈ 4.2% (unchanged curve)
Rolldown is larger when the curve is STEEP (bigger yield decline as the bond rolls down) and duration is higher. It disappears if forwards are realized (rates rise to match).
Exam focus: If your view = the forwards, no active bet adds expected value: when forwards are realized, all bonds earn the same horizon return, so duration/curve tilts only change risk. Match the benchmark (neutral). 'Riding the curve' (rolldown = yield income + price gain from rolling to a lower yield) beats a short bond only if the curve stays static (forwards NOT realized) — a bet on curve stability, larger when the curve is steep.
LOS 3 — When your view diverges: level, slope, shape
LOS: formulate a portfolio positioning strategy given forward rates and a view that diverges from the market in rate level, slope, and shape
What you must do:  Map each type of divergent view to a position.
Level (parallel) view
If you expect rates LOWER than the forwards imply, LENGTHEN duration (buy long bonds) to gain from the price rise; if you expect rates HIGHER, SHORTEN duration. The bet is on the LEVEL of rates versus the forward-implied level.
Slope view
A slope view is about the short–long spread changing:
	•	Expect a STEEPENER (long rates rise relative to short, curve steepens) → tilt toward the SHORT end: buy short, sell/underweight long (a duration-neutral steepener holds duration constant while shifting weight short).
	•	Expect a FLATTENER (long rates fall relative to short) → tilt toward the LONG end: buy long, sell/underweight short.
Shape / curvature view — bullet vs barbell
A curvature view is expressed with BULLET and BARBELL structures:
	•	Bullet — concentrate maturities at one point (e.g., the middle). Lower convexity; benefits if the curve is stable or the middle outperforms (a less-humped curve).
	•	Barbell — hold short and long maturities, little in the middle. Higher convexity; benefits from increased CURVATURE (the middle cheapening relative to the wings) and from higher volatility.
BUTTERFLY trades formalize the curvature bet: a long-butterfly (long the wings, short the body) profits if curvature increases; the reverse profits if it decreases. Curve trades are often made DURATION-NEUTRAL — total duration matches the benchmark while key-rate exposures are tilted — so the trade isolates the slope/shape view from a level bet.
Worked example — a duration-neutral flattener
A manager expects the curve to FLATTEN (long rates fall relative to short). To bet on slope WITHOUT a level bet, build a duration-neutral flattener.
Buy the long (high 30y-KRD) bond, sell the short (2y-KRD) bond
Size the legs so total effective duration = the benchmark's (no level bet)
If the curve flattens (30y yield falls, 2y roughly unchanged), the long leg gains more than the short leg loses → profit, independent of a parallel move. Matching total duration isolates the slope view from a level bet.
Table 1 — Mapping a view to a position
View
Position
Rates fall (level)
Lengthen duration (buy long)
Rates rise (level)
Shorten duration
Steepener (long up vs short)
Buy short / sell long (tilt short)
Flattener (long down vs short)
Buy long / sell short
Curvature up (wings outperform)
Barbell / long butterfly
Curvature down (body outperforms)
Bullet / short butterfly
Higher volatility
Raise convexity (barbell / buy options)
Lower volatility
Reduce convexity (bullet / sell options)
Exam focus: Divergent views → positions. LEVEL: rates below forwards → lengthen; above → shorten. SLOPE: steepener → buy short/sell long (tilt short); flattener → buy long/sell short. SHAPE: bullet (low convexity, benefits if stable/middle outperforms) vs barbell (high convexity, benefits from curvature/volatility); butterfly trades express curvature. Make curve trades DURATION-NEUTRAL (match total duration, tilt key-rates) to isolate the slope/shape view from a level bet.
LOS 4 — Positioning for a change in volatility
LOS: formulate a portfolio positioning strategy based upon expected changes in interest rate volatility
What you must do:  Link a volatility view to a convexity position and know convexity's cost.
Convexity is the key to a volatility view. The convexity term ½C(Δy)² is POSITIVE for any move and grows with the SIZE of the move, so higher convexity is valuable when yields are volatile:
	•	Expect HIGHER volatility → INCREASE convexity (a barbell, or buy options / long-convexity structures): larger moves in either direction add value through the convexity term.
	•	Expect LOWER volatility → REDUCE convexity (a bullet, or sell options): in a calm market you do not want to pay for convexity you will not use.
Convexity has a cost. For the same duration, a higher-convexity portfolio has a LOWER yield — the market charges for convexity. So the trade-off is direct: BUY convexity (accept lower yield) when you expect volatility; SELL convexity (earn the higher yield) when you expect calm. Buying options is long volatility/convexity; selling options earns premium but is short convexity (dangerous if volatility spikes).
Worked example — convexity in a large move
A bullet (convexity 50) vs a barbell (convexity 90), both duration 6. Yields move 1.0% (Δy = ±0.01), either direction.
Bullet convexity gain = ½ × 50 × 0.01² = 0.25%
Barbell convexity gain = ½ × 90 × 0.01² = 0.45%
The barbell's convexity adds 0.45% vs the bullet's 0.25% — a 0.20% edge for the SAME duration, in EITHER direction. For a large move that edge can outweigh the barbell's yield give-up; for a small move it cannot. Hence: buy convexity (barbell) when you expect big moves, sell it (bullet) when you expect calm.
Using derivatives for curve and volatility positioning
Curve and volatility views are often implemented with DERIVATIVES rather than by trading bonds. Interest-rate FUTURES and SWAPS adjust duration and express steepener/flattener views efficiently (for example, a futures spread between two maturities). OPTIONS on rates or bonds express a volatility view directly: buying options (caps, floors, swaptions, or bond options) is a long-convexity, long-volatility position that gains from large moves, while selling them earns premium but is short convexity. Derivatives let a manager fine-tune the duration, key-rate, and convexity profile precisely and cheaply — adding or removing convexity without wholesale changes to the bond holdings — at the cost of the usual derivative risks (basis, counterparty, rollover).
Exam focus: Volatility view via convexity: expect HIGHER vol → raise convexity (barbell / buy options) — the ½C(Δy)² term rewards big moves in either direction; expect LOWER vol → cut convexity (bullet / sell options) to capture yield. Convexity costs yield. Implement with derivatives — futures/swaps for duration and slope, options/swaptions for volatility/convexity (buy = long convexity, sell = short convexity, earn premium).
LOS 5 — Key-rate durations
LOS: evaluate a portfolio's sensitivity using key rate durations of the portfolio and its benchmark
What you must do:  Use key-rate durations to locate curve exposure and check they sum to effective duration.
KEY-RATE (partial) durations measure a portfolio's price sensitivity to a yield change at a SPECIFIC maturity (say 2y, 5y, 10y, 30y), holding the other points fixed. They pinpoint WHERE on the curve the portfolio is exposed — something a single effective-duration number cannot show. A basic identity:
Σ key-rate durations = effective (total) duration
Worked example — key-rate duration profile
A portfolio's key-rate durations: 2y = 0.5, 5y = 1.0, 10y = 2.5, 30y = 2.0.
Effective duration = 0.5 + 1.0 + 2.5 + 2.0 = 6.0
If the benchmark has the same 6.0 effective duration but a DIFFERENT profile (say more at 5y, less at 30y), the portfolio is overexposed to the long end. A 30y-rate rise would hurt the portfolio more than the benchmark, even though total durations match — a curve (not level) exposure that only key-rate durations reveal.
Key-rate durations are how a manager BUILDS and MONITORS duration-neutral curve trades: match total duration to the benchmark while deliberately tilting the key-rate profile to express a steepener, flattener, or butterfly view, then track the realized curve move against that profile.
Worked example — a duration-neutral steepener via KRDs
Benchmark KRDs: 2y = 2.0, 10y = 4.0 (effective duration 6.0). The manager expects a steepener (10y rises vs 2y) and wants a duration-neutral bet.
Raise the 2y KRD, cut the 10y KRD, keeping the sum at 6.0:
  e.g. 2y = 3.5, 10y = 2.5 → sum = 6.0 (duration unchanged)
If the 10y yield rises and the 2y is unchanged, the portfolio loses LESS at the long end than the benchmark → it outperforms. Total duration matches (no level bet); the KRD tilt (more short, less long) profits from the expected steepening.
Exam focus: Key-rate (partial) durations = sensitivity to a change at a SPECIFIC maturity, holding others fixed; they locate curve exposure that effective duration hides, and they SUM to effective duration. Compare the portfolio's KRD profile to the benchmark's to see over/underexposed points, and TILT the KRD profile (keeping the sum = benchmark duration) to build duration-neutral steepener/flattener/butterfly trades.
LOS 6 — Yield curve strategies across currencies
LOS: discuss yield curve strategies across currencies
What you must do:  Explain inter-market curve trades and their currency dimension.
Curve strategies extend across countries: a manager can take a steepener in one country's curve and a flattener in another, or a relative duration bet between markets, based on differing rate views and cycles. Cross-market curve trades expand the opportunity set (more independent bets, higher breadth) and can diversify, since national curves are imperfectly correlated. Two considerations dominate:
	•	Currency risk — a position in a foreign bond carries currency exposure; the manager decides whether to HEDGE it (isolating the pure rate/curve view) or leave it (adding a currency bet), using the tools from the currency-management reading.
	•	Curve correlation — the diversification benefit depends on how correlated the two curves are; highly correlated curves (e.g., closely-linked economies) offer less diversification and can move together against the position.
Inter-market trades also include CARRY: borrowing in a low-yield-curve currency and investing in a higher-yielding one — profitable if the rate differential is not offset by currency moves (the same carry logic as currency management).
Worked example — an inter-market curve trade
A manager expects Country A's curve to FLATTEN and Country B's to STEEPEN. Position: a duration-neutral flattener in A and a duration-neutral steepener in B, sized to comparable risk.
A: buy long / sell short (flattener) ; B: buy short / sell long (steepener)
Currency: hedge A and B to isolate the pure curve views (or leave for a currency bet)
If A flattens and B steepens as expected, both legs profit. Combining two country curve views raises breadth and can diversify (if the curves are imperfectly correlated), but currency must be managed and correlated curves reduce the diversification benefit.
Exam focus: Across currencies: combine curve views across countries (steepener here, flattener there; relative duration) to add breadth and diversification, since curves are imperfectly correlated. Decide whether to HEDGE the currency (pure rate view) or keep it (currency bet). Watch curve correlation (less diversification if highly correlated). Inter-market carry = borrow low-curve currency, invest higher — a currency-risk bet.
LOS 7 — Expected return and risks of a curve strategy
LOS: evaluate the expected return and risks of a yield curve strategy
What you must do:  Estimate the expected return and identify the risks of a positioning strategy.
Expected return uses the LOS 1 decomposition: yield income + rolldown + the expected price change from the rate view (−MD×Δy + ½C(Δy)²) − credit − currency. A manager evaluates a candidate position by projecting each component under the expected scenario and comparing across strategies.
Worked example — comparing two positions
Under an expected 0.2% fall in yields: a BULLET (duration 6, convexity 50) vs a BARBELL (same duration 6, convexity 90), both with rolling yield 3.5%.
Bullet price change = −6(−0.002) + ½(50)(0.002²) = 0.012 + 0.0001 = 1.21%
Barbell price change = −6(−0.002) + ½(90)(0.002²) = 0.012 + 0.00018 = 1.218%
For a SMALL move the convexity edge is tiny (1.218% vs 1.21%); but the barbell's yield is lower (convexity costs yield), so if the move is small the bullet wins on yield. The barbell only pulls ahead if the move is LARGE (volatility high) — the convexity/yield trade-off in numbers.
Worked example — scenario analysis of a barbell
A barbell (duration 6, convexity 90, rolling yield 3.5%) is evaluated across scenarios (probabilities in parentheses):
Rates −0.5% (30%): 3.5 + [−6(−0.005)+½(90)(0.005²)] = 3.5 + 3.11 = 6.61%
Unchanged (40%): 3.5%
Rates +0.5% (30%): 3.5 + [−6(0.005)+½(90)(0.005²)] = 3.5 − 2.89 = 0.61%
E(return) = 0.30(6.61) + 0.40(3.5) + 0.30(0.61) = 3.57%
Averaging across scenarios gives a probability-weighted expected return (3.57%) and the range (0.61% to 6.61%). The convexity (½×90×0.005² = 0.11%) lifts BOTH the up and down cases relative to a lower-convexity bullet.
Risks. The main risk is the VIEW being wrong — rates or the curve moving opposite to the bet (a duration bet loses if rates rise; a steepener loses if the curve flattens). Other risks: the curve moving in a NON-PARALLEL way the position did not anticipate (revealed by key-rate mismatches), CONVEXITY cost if the expected volatility does not materialize, and for cross-currency trades, CURRENCY risk. Evaluate a strategy by BOTH its expected return and the risk (duration, curve, convexity, currency) it takes on.
Evaluating strategies by scenario analysis
Because a curve can move in many ways, managers evaluate a candidate position with SCENARIO ANALYSIS: project the total return under several yield-curve scenarios (parallel up/down, steepener, flattener, butterfly, unchanged), using the return decomposition for each. This shows not just the expected return under the base case but the DISTRIBUTION of outcomes and the position's worst case, revealing hidden curve or convexity risks. Comparing candidate strategies across the same scenarios — weighted by the manager's probabilities — identifies the position with the best risk-adjusted expected return for the view.
Risks and execution of curve trades
Curve trades carry risks beyond a simple directional loss. A duration-neutral curve trade is exposed to the curve moving in an UNEXPECTED way — a flattener loses if the curve steepens, and is also hurt if the whole curve shifts in a non-parallel manner the KRD tilt did not anticipate. Butterfly and barbell trades depend on convexity that only pays if volatility materializes. Execution matters too: curve trades often involve multiple legs (buying and selling several bonds or futures), so transaction costs and liquidity at each maturity affect the net result, and the position must be rebalanced as durations and KRDs drift. A trade that looks attractive under the base-case scenario can be poor once costs, financing, and the full scenario distribution are considered.
Exam focus: Expected return = the LOS 1 decomposition under the expected scenario. Evaluate with SCENARIO ANALYSIS across parallel/steepener/flattener/butterfly/unchanged cases to see the outcome distribution and worst case. Risks: the view being wrong, unanticipated non-parallel shifts (key-rate mismatch), convexity cost if volatility does not appear, currency risk, and execution/rebalancing costs across multiple legs. Judge by expected return AND risk taken.
Worked case — positioning for a lower, flatter curve
Suppose a manager expects rates to FALL and the curve to FLATTEN over the next year, both by more than the forwards imply. The reading's tools combine as follows:
	•	Level — because rates are expected below the forwards, LENGTHEN duration above the benchmark to profit from the price rise.
	•	Slope — because a flattener is expected, tilt the KEY-RATE profile toward the LONG end (buy long, sell short), capturing the slope view on top of the level view.
	•	Convexity — if the move is also expected to be LARGE (high volatility), add convexity (a barbell or long options) to gain from the size of the move; if the move is expected to be smooth, keep a bullet to avoid the yield give-up.
	•	Evaluation — run scenario analysis (down-and-flatter as the base case, plus up, steepener, and unchanged scenarios) to confirm the probability-weighted expected return beats alternatives and to see the worst case.
The resulting portfolio has longer duration than the benchmark, a long-tilted key-rate profile, and convexity chosen for the expected volatility — a clean expression of 'lower and flatter' whose return decomposes into yield income, rolldown, the duration/convexity gain from the expected move, and (for any foreign legs) currency. Its main risk is the view being wrong: rising rates or a steepening curve would reverse the duration and slope gains.
Curve strategies at a glance
Table 2 — Strategy, view, and key risk
Strategy
View it expresses
Key exposure / risk
Duration positioning
Level of rates vs forwards
Duration (level) risk
Steepener / flattener
Curve slope change
Curve slope risk
Bullet vs barbell
Curvature / volatility
Convexity, curvature risk
Roll-down (ride curve)
Curve stability (forwards not realized)
Curve rising as forwards imply
Convexity (options)
Interest-rate volatility
Convexity cost if calm
Cross-currency
Relative curves across markets
Currency + curve-correlation risk
Each strategy isolates a different feature of the curve — level, slope, shape, or volatility — and each carries the corresponding risk if that feature moves against the view. Duration-neutral construction and scenario analysis keep the intended bet clean and its downside visible.
Quick reference — key formulas
The formulas worth memorizing
Rolling yield = yield income + rolldown return (unchanged-curve return)
ΔPrice% ≈ −MD × Δy + ½ × Convexity × (Δy)²
E(return) ≈ yield income + rolldown + [−MD×Δy + ½C(Δy)²] − credit − currency
View = forwards → no active bet (match benchmark); rolldown bets on curve stability
Level: rates < forwards → lengthen; rates > forwards → shorten
Slope: steepener → buy short/sell long; flattener → buy long/sell short
Shape: bullet (low convexity) vs barbell (high convexity); butterfly for curvature
Volatility: expect ↑ vol → raise convexity (barbell/buy options); ↓ vol → cut it
Convexity costs yield; Σ key-rate durations = effective duration
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. Name the components of a bond's total return.
   -> Yield income + rolldown (= rolling yield) + price change from the rate view − credit − currency.
2. Yield income 3%, rolldown 0.5%, Δy −0.2%, MD 6, C 50. Total return?
   -> 3 + 0.5 + [−6(−0.002)+½(50)(0.002²)] = 3 + 0.5 + 1.21 = 4.71%.
3. If your view equals the forwards, what position adds value?
   -> None — match the benchmark; when forwards are realized, all bonds earn the same horizon return.
4. When does riding the curve (rolldown) beat a short bond?
   -> Only if the curve stays static (forwards NOT realized) — it is a bet on curve stability.
5. You expect rates lower than the forwards imply. Duration action?
   -> Lengthen duration (buy long bonds).
6. Position for an expected steepener?
   -> Tilt short: buy short, sell/underweight long (duration-neutral if isolating slope).
7. Bullet vs barbell — which has more convexity, and who benefits from higher volatility?
   -> Barbell has more convexity and benefits from higher volatility/curvature.
8. Expect higher rate volatility — convexity action?
   -> Increase convexity (barbell / buy options).
9. What is the cost of holding higher convexity?
   -> Lower yield for the same duration (convexity is priced).
10. KRDs 0.5, 1.0, 2.5, 2.0. Effective duration?
   -> 6.0 (key-rate durations sum to effective duration).
11. Why use key-rate durations rather than just effective duration?
   -> They show WHERE on the curve the exposure is, enabling duration-neutral curve trades.
12. In a cross-currency curve trade, what is the extra risk decision?
   -> Whether to hedge the currency (pure rate view) or keep it (currency bet).
13. Benchmark KRDs 2y=2.0, 10y=4.0. Tilt for a duration-neutral steepener?
   -> Raise 2y, cut 10y keeping the sum at 6.0 (e.g., 2y=3.5, 10y=2.5).
14. Bullet C=50 vs barbell C=90, Δy=±1.0%. Convexity gains?
   -> ½(50)(0.01²)=0.25% vs ½(90)(0.01²)=0.45% — a 0.20% barbell edge for a large move.
15. Which three exposures drive a portfolio's return vs its benchmark under a curve move?
   -> Duration, convexity, and the key-rate-duration profile.
16. How is a volatility view implemented with options?
   -> Buy options (caps/floors/swaptions) = long convexity/volatility; sell = short convexity, earn premium.

readings.title: Fixed-Income Active Management: Credit Strategies
readings.orientation: Orientation. The densest fixed-income reading: how to manage a credit (spread) portfolio. Eleven learning outcomes: spread risk considerations, the spread measures and why OAS is best, bottom-up and top-down credit approaches, liquidity and tail risk, CDS strategies, positioning for a spread view (with duration times spread), international and structured credit, and the analytical tools. The calculation core is DURATION TIMES SPREAD (DTS) and the EXPECTED-EXCESS-RETURN decomposition, both worked below. Learn the spread measures (OAS), DTS, the excess-return formula, and the spread-view positioning cold — they anchor most of the reading's exam questions.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Fixed-Income Portfolio Management
Reading: Fixed-Income Active Management: Credit Strategies
Orientation. The densest fixed-income reading: how to manage a credit (spread) portfolio. Eleven learning outcomes: spread risk considerations, the spread measures and why OAS is best, bottom-up and top-down credit approaches, liquidity and tail risk, CDS strategies, positioning for a spread view (with duration times spread), international and structured credit, and the analytical tools. The calculation core is DURATION TIMES SPREAD (DTS) and the EXPECTED-EXCESS-RETURN decomposition, both worked below. Learn the spread measures (OAS), DTS, the excess-return formula, and the spread-view positioning cold — they anchor most of the reading's exam questions.

LOS 1 — Risk considerations for spread-based portfolios
LOS: describe risk considerations for spread-based fixed-income portfolios
What you must do:  Identify the components of credit/spread risk and the role of spread duration.
A spread-based portfolio earns extra yield for bearing credit (spread) risk, which has several components:
	•	Default (credit) risk — the issuer fails to pay; the expected loss = probability of default × loss given default.
	•	Credit migration (downgrade) risk — a rating downgrade widens the spread and lowers the price even without default.
	•	Spread (market) risk — spreads widen for macro reasons (a weaker credit cycle), lowering prices across the board.
	•	Liquidity risk — credit bonds trade infrequently, so exiting can be costly, especially in stress.
Price change from a spread move ≈ − spread duration × Δspread
Spread duration measures a bond's price sensitivity to a change in its SPREAD (as opposed to the risk-free rate). It is the key exposure to manage in a credit portfolio, and it interacts with the spread level itself — the basis for duration times spread (LOS 8). Note that spreads and risk-free rates often move OPPOSITELY (spreads widen as rates fall in a flight to quality), so a credit bond's empirical (regression-based) rate duration can differ from its analytical duration.
The spread-income-vs-loss trade-off
At its core, credit investing trades SPREAD INCOME against expected LOSSES. The spread compensates for expected default losses (POD × LGD), a liquidity premium, and a risk premium for the uncertainty of those losses. A credit position adds value only if the spread earned EXCEEDS the losses realized — which is why the expected-excess-return decomposition (LOS 8) subtracts both spread-change effects and expected credit losses from spread income. In benign periods spread income dominates and credit outperforms; in a downturn losses and widening dominate and credit underperforms sharply — the source of the negative skew.
Exam focus: Credit/spread risk = default risk (POD × LGD) + migration/downgrade risk + spread (market) risk + liquidity risk. Spread duration measures price sensitivity to spread changes: ΔPrice ≈ −spread duration × Δspread. Credit trades spread income against expected losses; it adds value only if spread earned > losses realized. Spreads and rates often move oppositely (flight to quality), so empirical duration differs from analytical.
LOS 2 — Spread measures and why OAS is best
LOS: discuss the advantages and disadvantages of credit spread measures and explain why option-adjusted spread is most appropriate
What you must do:  Compare the spread measures and justify OAS.
Table 1 — Credit spread measures
Measure
What it is
Limitation
Benchmark / G-spread
Yield over an interpolated government yield
Ignores the shape of the curve and optionality
I-spread
Yield over the swap rate
Same; a single point, no curve/option adjustment
Z-spread
Constant spread over the SPOT curve to price the bond
Ignores embedded options (overstates spread for callables)
OAS
Z-spread MINUS the option cost (option-adjusted)
Model-dependent (needs a rate-volatility model)
Why OAS is best. OAS removes the value of embedded options from the spread, so it measures the compensation for CREDIT and liquidity risk alone — making it COMPARABLE across bonds with and without options, and across the whole curve. A callable bond's Z-spread is inflated by the option the investor is short; OAS strips that out. The drawback is that OAS depends on an interest-rate/volatility MODEL, so different assumptions give different OAS values.
Worked example — OAS vs Z-spread for a callable
A callable bond has a Z-spread of 150 bp. A model values the embedded call option at 40 bp.
OAS = Z-spread − option cost = 150 − 40 = 110 bp
The investor is SHORT the call (the issuer can call the bond away), so 40 bp of the Z-spread is compensation for the option, not credit. The 110 bp OAS is the true credit/liquidity spread, comparable to an option-free bond's — using the 150 bp Z-spread would overstate the credit compensation.
Exam focus: Spread measures: G-spread (over government), I-spread (over swaps), Z-spread (constant spread over the spot curve — ignores options), OAS (Z-spread minus option cost). OAS is best because it is option-adjusted → comparable across option and non-option bonds and over the whole curve; its drawback is model dependence (needs a volatility model).
LOS 3 & 4 — Bottom-up and top-down credit approaches
LOS: discuss bottom-up and top-down approaches to credit strategies
What you must do:  Contrast issuer-level credit selection with macro credit allocation.
Bottom-up credit builds the portfolio from ISSUER-level analysis: assess each issuer's creditworthiness (the four Cs — capacity, collateral, covenants, character), estimate its fair spread, and buy bonds whose spread is WIDE relative to that fair value or to peers (relative value). The manager compares issuers on spread (OAS) per unit of credit risk and selects the most attractive, expecting their spreads to tighten or at least to compensate for the risk.
Top-down credit starts from MACRO views: where we are in the CREDIT CYCLE, the expected path of default rates, and therefore whether to add or reduce credit risk overall. It allocates across SECTORS (cyclical vs defensive), QUALITY (investment-grade vs high-yield), and the overall credit BETA of the portfolio, positioning for the expected direction of spreads. Many managers blend the two — a top-down risk posture with bottom-up security selection within it.
The credit cycle and relative value
Top-down credit positioning tracks the CREDIT CYCLE. In an EXPANSION, defaults fall and spreads tighten, favoring MORE credit risk (down in quality, longer spread duration, cyclical sectors); as the cycle PEAKS and turns, spreads widen and defaults rise, favoring LESS risk (up in quality, shorter spread duration, defensive sectors). Timing the turn is hard, so managers watch leading indicators — issuance quality, corporate leverage, spreads versus history, and macro data. Bottom-up RELATIVE VALUE complements this: comparing a bond's OAS to its peers, its own history, and its fundamentals to find spreads that are wide (buy) or tight (avoid) for the risk, and monitoring each spread's expected path.
Exam focus: Bottom-up = issuer-level analysis (four Cs: capacity, collateral, covenants, character), buy wide-spread/undervalued credits on relative value. Top-down = macro credit-cycle view (expansion → add risk/down in quality/cyclical; peak/turn → reduce/up in quality/defensive) driving sector, quality (IG/HY), and overall credit-beta allocation. Often blended: top-down posture + bottom-up selection.
LOS 5 — Liquidity risk in credit markets
LOS: discuss liquidity risk in credit markets and how it can be managed
What you must do:  Explain why credit is illiquid and how to manage it.
Credit markets are far LESS liquid than government-bond markets: they trade over-the-counter, a single issuer has many different bonds (fragmenting liquidity), and dealer inventories (and thus market-making capacity) have shrunk since the financial crisis. Liquidity dries up further in stress, exactly when investors want to sell. Management techniques:
	•	Hold liquid instruments — larger, newer (on-the-run) issues and a cash/liquidity buffer for redemptions.
	•	Use liquid derivatives and index products — CDS indexes (CDX/iTraxx) and liquid ETFs give credit exposure that trades even when individual bonds do not.
	•	Size positions to tradability — limit holdings of illiquid names and account for bid-ask and market-impact costs.
Illustration — the cost of illiquidity
An illiquid corporate bond has a bid-ask spread of 1.0% (buy at 100.5, sell at 99.5), versus 0.05% for a liquid on-the-run Treasury.
Round-trip trading cost ≈ 1.0% (corporate) vs 0.05% (Treasury)
In stress the corporate's bid-ask can widen to several percent, or the bond may not trade at all. Illiquidity is a real, sometimes large, cost that must be weighed against the extra spread — and it is worst exactly when the manager needs to sell.
Exam focus: Credit is illiquid: OTC, fragmented (many bonds per issuer), shrunken dealer inventories, and liquidity vanishes in stress. Manage with liquid on-the-run bonds and a cash buffer, CDS indexes/ETFs for liquid exposure, and position sizing to tradability (account for bid-ask/impact, which widens sharply in stress).
LOS 6 — Tail risk in credit portfolios
LOS: describe how to assess and manage tail risk in credit portfolios
What you must do:  Explain credit's negative skew and how to assess/manage the tail.
Credit returns are NEGATIVELY SKEWED with fat tails: the upside is capped (you can only earn the spread) while the downside is large (default losses), so occasional severe losses dominate the risk. Normal-distribution measures (standard deviation, ordinary VaR) UNDERSTATE this tail. Assessment and management:
	•	Assess with scenario and stress testing — model spread-widening and default scenarios, and use CONDITIONAL VaR (expected shortfall) rather than standard deviation or VaR alone.
	•	Diversify and tilt to quality — spread issuer and sector exposure and reduce concentration in the riskiest credits.
	•	Hedge the tail — buy protection via CDS or index products, or options, to cap the loss from a systemic widening or default wave.
Illustration — expected vs unexpected loss
A bond has a 2% annual probability of default and a 60% loss given default.
Expected annual credit loss = 2% × 60% = 1.20%
But the DISTRIBUTION is skewed: in 98% of years the loss is zero, and in the rare default year the loss is 60% — far above the 1.20% average. The tail (a default) dominates the risk even though the expected loss is modest, which is why expected-loss numbers must be paired with tail measures.
Watch out: Because credit is negatively skewed, a strategy can look excellent on average-return and volatility measures for years, then suffer a large loss in a default/widening event — the same 'picking up nickels' trap as the carry trade. Volatility and VaR understate the danger; scenario analysis, expected shortfall, and tail hedges are needed. Correlations also SPIKE toward 1 in a crisis, so diversification helps least when it is needed most.
Exam focus: Credit returns are negatively skewed (capped upside, large default downside), so SD/VaR understate tail risk. Assess with scenario/stress testing and conditional VaR (expected shortfall). Manage by diversifying, tilting to quality, reducing concentration, and buying tail hedges (CDS/index/options). Correlations spike in crises.
LOS 7 — Credit default swap strategies
LOS: discuss the use of credit default swap strategies in active fixed-income portfolio management
What you must do:  Explain CDS mechanics and the main CDS strategies.
A CREDIT DEFAULT SWAP transfers credit risk: the protection BUYER pays a periodic premium and receives a payout if the reference entity defaults (a SHORT-credit position); the protection SELLER receives the premium and pays on default (a LONG-credit position). CDS uses:
	•	Adjust credit exposure efficiently — add credit risk by selling protection, or reduce/short it by buying protection, WITHOUT trading the underlying bonds.
	•	Express relative value — sell protection on a favored credit and buy protection on a disliked one (a long-short credit pair).
	•	Curve and index trades — trade CDS at different maturities (credit-curve views) or CDS indexes (CDX/iTraxx) and their tranches for macro credit beta.
	•	Basis trades — exploit the CDS-BOND BASIS, the difference between the CDS spread and the cash-bond spread, when they diverge.
CDS is liquid, allows SHORTING credit (hard to do with bonds), and lets a manager separate the credit decision from the cash-bond position — an efficient, capital-light way to implement credit views.
Worked example — a CDS protection payout
An investor buys 10,000,000 notional of 5-year CDS protection at a spread of 200 bp (paying 200,000/year). The issuer defaults with a recovery rate of 40%.
Payout = notional × (1 − recovery) = 10,000,000 × (1 − 0.40) = 6,000,000
The protection buyer receives 6,000,000 (net of premiums paid). Before default the CDS also marks to market: if the credit's spread WIDENS, the protection buyer's position GAINS — the basis for using CDS to express spread views, not just default bets.
Exam focus: CDS: buy protection = short credit (pay premium, gain on default/widening); sell protection = long credit (receive premium). Default payout = notional × (1 − recovery). Uses: adjust exposure without trading bonds, relative-value long/short pairs, curve and index (CDX/iTraxx) trades, and CDS-bond basis trades. Liquid, allows shorting credit, capital-light.
LOS 8 — Positioning for a credit spread view (DTS)
LOS: discuss portfolio positioning strategies to implement a specific credit spread view
What you must do:  Position for tightening/widening and compute DTS and expected excess return.
Positioning follows the spread view: expect spreads to TIGHTEN → ADD credit risk (buy credit, sell protection, go DOWN in quality, EXTEND spread duration); expect spreads to WIDEN → REDUCE credit risk (sell credit, buy protection, UP in quality, SHORTEN spread duration). The right measure of spread exposure is DURATION TIMES SPREAD:
DTS = spread duration × spread
DTS is used instead of spread duration alone because higher-spread bonds have proportionally MORE spread volatility — a given percentage spread change hits them harder — so DTS captures true spread risk. Position and budget spread risk by DTS.
Spread-curve positioning and empirical duration
A spread view can also concern the credit CURVE, not just its level: if short-dated spreads are expected to tighten more than long-dated (a credit-curve flattener), overweight short-maturity credit; the reverse for a steepener. And because credit spreads tend to move OPPOSITELY to risk-free rates (spreads widen when rates fall in a flight to quality), a credit bond's EMPIRICAL duration — estimated by regressing its price on Treasury yields — is often LOWER than its analytical duration, because the spread partly offsets the rate move. Managers use empirical duration when hedging credit bonds' rate exposure, since it better reflects how the bonds actually behave.
Worked example — duration times spread
A bond has a spread duration of 5 and a spread of 200 bp (2.00%).
DTS = 5 × 2.00% = 10 (equivalently 5 × 200 = 1,000 bp-years)
If spreads on this bond widen 10% (from 200 to 220 bp, i.e. +20 bp), the loss ≈ spread duration × Δspread = 5 × 0.20% = 1.0%. DTS captures that higher-spread bonds move proportionally more.
E(excess return) ≈ (spread × t) − (Δspread × spread duration) − (t × POD × LGD)
Worked example — expected excess return
Spread 2.00% (annual, t = 1). Expected spread widening Δspread = +0.20% with spread duration 5. Probability of default 2%, loss given default 60%.
Spread income = 2.00%
Spread-change loss = 0.20% × 5 = 1.00%
Expected credit loss = 2% × 60% = 1.20%
E(excess return) = 2.00 − 1.00 − 1.20 = −0.20%
Despite a 2% spread, the expected excess return is NEGATIVE (−0.20%) once the expected widening and default losses are subtracted — the spread does not compensate for the risk under this view.
Constructing the credit portfolio
A full credit process combines the pieces: a TOP-DOWN view sets the overall credit beta and the sector/quality tilts (how much DTS to run and where), a BOTTOM-UP process selects the specific issuers within that budget on relative value, LIQUIDITY and TAIL-risk limits constrain concentration and illiquid holdings, and CDS or index products fine-tune the exposure and hedge the tail efficiently. The manager budgets spread risk by DTS, projects the expected excess return of the resulting portfolio, and stress-tests it — assembling a portfolio whose spread exposure reflects the view while its liquidity and tail risk stay within the mandate.
Exam focus: Spread view: tighten → add credit (buy, sell protection, down in quality, extend spread duration); widen → reduce (sell, buy protection, up in quality, shorten). Budget spread risk by DTS = spread duration × spread (higher-spread bonds move proportionally more). E(excess return) ≈ (spread×t) − (Δspread×spread duration) − (t×POD×LGD). Combine top-down beta/tilts with bottom-up selection, within liquidity/tail limits.
LOS 9 — International credit
LOS: discuss considerations in constructing and managing portfolios across international credit markets
What you must do:  List the considerations in cross-border credit.
	•	Currency risk — foreign credit carries currency exposure; hedge it to isolate the credit view or leave it as a currency bet.
	•	Differing credit cycles and spreads — regions are at different points in the cycle, creating relative-value opportunities across markets.
	•	Legal and covenant differences — bankruptcy regimes, creditor rights, and covenant quality vary by jurisdiction, affecting recovery and risk.
	•	Liquidity differences — some markets are far less liquid than others.
	•	Emerging-market credit — higher yields but higher default, political, and tail risk, and thinner liquidity.
Illustration — hedged vs unhedged foreign credit
A US investor buys a euro-denominated corporate bond yielding 3.0% (a 150 bp spread over Bunds).
Unhedged: return also depends on EUR/USD — depreciation can wipe out the spread
Hedged: sell EUR forward → isolates the credit/rate return; hedge cost = rate differential
Hedging separates the credit view from a currency bet; most credit managers hedge the currency to keep the position a pure credit trade unless they hold a currency view.
Exam focus: International credit considerations: currency risk (hedge to isolate the credit view, or keep as a currency bet), differing credit cycles/spreads (relative value), legal/covenant and bankruptcy-regime differences (recovery), liquidity differences, and emerging-market credit (higher yield, higher default/political/tail risk).
LOS 10 — Structured instruments as an alternative
LOS: describe the use of structured financial instruments as an alternative to corporate bonds
What you must do:  Explain how structured products serve credit portfolios and their risks.
STRUCTURED instruments — asset-backed securities (ABS), mortgage-backed securities (RMBS/CMBS), collateralized loan obligations (CLOs), covered bonds, and CDOs — are alternatives (or complements) to corporate bonds. Their appeal:
	•	Diversification — exposure to different collateral (consumer loans, mortgages, corporate loans) uncorrelated with corporate credit.
	•	Targeted risk via tranching — senior/mezzanine/equity tranches let an investor pick a precise point on the risk-return spectrum.
	•	Yield pickup — often a higher yield than similarly-rated corporates, compensating for complexity and liquidity.
The main structured types
The principal structured categories differ by collateral and cash-flow rules. ABS are backed by consumer receivables (auto loans, credit cards); RMBS and CMBS by residential and commercial mortgages, carrying PREPAYMENT (and extension) risk as borrowers refinance; CLOs by portfolios of corporate loans, tranched from senior (low-risk) to equity (first-loss); COVERED BONDS are issued by banks with dual recourse to the issuer AND a ring-fenced cover pool. Each is TRANCHED so losses hit the equity tranche first and the senior tranche last, letting an investor choose a risk level — but the senior tranches' safety depends on correlation assumptions about the collateral, which is exactly where 2008 losses concentrated.
Watch out: Structured products add COMPLEXITY and model risk: MBS carry prepayment (and extension) risk, tranche values depend on correlation assumptions that failed badly in 2008, and many are less liquid than corporates. The higher yield is compensation for these — do not treat a highly-rated tranche as risk-equivalent to a same-rated corporate bond.
Exam focus: Structured instruments (ABS, RMBS/CMBS, CLOs, covered bonds, CDOs) offer diversification (different collateral), targeted risk via tranching (senior/mezz/equity), and yield pickup. Risks: complexity/model risk, prepayment/extension (MBS), correlation risk in tranches (2008), and lower liquidity.
LOS 11 — Analytical tools
LOS: describe key inputs, outputs, and considerations in using analytical tools to manage fixed-income portfolios
What you must do:  Know the inputs, outputs, and limits of credit-portfolio analytics.
Inputs: security characteristics, spreads/OAS, rate and spread durations, key-rate durations and DTS, ratings and default/recovery estimates, and yield-curve and spread scenarios.
Outputs: portfolio-level risk measures (effective and spread duration, DTS, VaR/CVaR), scenario and stress-test returns, exposures versus the benchmark (sector, quality, curve), and tracking error.
Considerations: the outputs are only as good as the model assumptions and data (garbage in, garbage out); models rely on historical relationships that can break; and quantitative output must be combined with JUDGMENT, not followed blindly. Understand each model's assumptions and limitations before relying on its numbers.
Scenario and stress analytics in the process
In practice the analytical tools are used iteratively: the manager builds a candidate portfolio, runs the risk engine to see its duration, spread duration, DTS, and benchmark-relative exposures, then STRESS-tests it against spread-widening, default, and rate scenarios to check the tail. The outputs flag unintended concentrations or curve exposures, and the manager adjusts positions and re-runs until the risk profile matches the mandate. The tools do not make the decision — they quantify the consequences of the manager's views and constraints, which the manager then judges against the expected excess return.
Exam focus: Analytics inputs: security data, spreads/OAS, rate & spread durations, key-rate/DTS, ratings, scenarios. Outputs: duration/spread-duration/DTS, VaR/CVaR, scenario returns, benchmark-relative exposures, tracking error. Considerations: garbage in/garbage out, models rely on breakable historical relationships, combine with judgment; know each model's assumptions/limits. Used iteratively — build, measure, stress-test, adjust.
Worked case — positioning for spread tightening
Suppose a manager, on a top-down view that the economy is entering an expansion and spreads will tighten, wants to add credit risk. The reading's tools combine:
	•	Top-down — raise the portfolio's credit beta and DTS above the benchmark; overweight cyclical sectors, shift somewhat down in quality (toward high-yield), and extend spread duration to capture the expected tightening.
	•	Bottom-up — within that budget, select issuers whose OAS is wide relative to peers and fundamentals (relative value), avoiding names with deteriorating credit.
	•	Implementation — add exposure efficiently by SELLING CDS protection (or buying a CDX index) rather than sourcing many illiquid bonds — faster and more liquid.
	•	Risk control — cap concentration and illiquid holdings, keep a liquidity buffer, and buy some tail protection (index puts or protection on the weakest names) so a surprise widening does not inflict an outsized loss; monitor DTS and stress-test.
The resulting portfolio has higher DTS than the benchmark, a cyclical/lower-quality tilt, and CDS-based exposure for liquidity — a clean expression of 'spreads will tighten.' Its expected excess return is the spread income and expected tightening gain minus expected credit losses; its main risk is the view being wrong (a downturn instead), which the tail hedges and quality limits are meant to cushion.
Positioning for a spread view — summary
Table 2 — Credit expectation and action
Expectation
Action
Spreads tighten
Add credit: buy, sell protection, down in quality, extend spread duration (raise DTS)
Spreads widen
Reduce credit: sell, buy protection, up in quality, shorten spread duration (cut DTS)
Credit-curve flattener
Overweight short-maturity credit (short spreads tighten more)
Higher default / tail risk
Diversify, tilt to quality, buy tail protection (CDS/index/options)
Need liquid exposure
Use CDS indexes (CDX/iTraxx) or liquid ETFs
The unifying measure is DTS: adding credit risk means raising the portfolio's duration times spread, reducing it means cutting DTS — always weighed against the expected excess return and the tail.
Quick reference — key formulas
The formulas worth memorizing
Spread P&L: ΔPrice ≈ − spread duration × Δspread
Expected credit loss = probability of default × loss given default
DTS = spread duration × spread (the true spread-risk measure)
E(excess return) ≈ (spread×t) − (Δspread×spread duration) − (t×POD×LGD)
OAS = Z-spread − option cost (best: option-adjusted, comparable)
Spread view: tighten → add credit / extend DTS ; widen → reduce / shorten
CDS: buy protection = short credit ; sell protection = long credit
Credit is negatively skewed → use CVaR/stress, not just SD/VaR
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. Name the components of credit/spread risk.
   -> Default risk (POD×LGD), migration/downgrade risk, spread (market) risk, liquidity risk.
2. Price change if spread duration is 5 and the spread widens 0.50%?
   -> ≈ −5 × 0.50% = −2.5%.
3. Why is OAS the preferred spread measure?
   -> It strips out embedded-option value → comparable across option/non-option bonds and the whole curve.
4. What is a Z-spread's main limitation vs OAS?
   -> It ignores embedded options (overstates the spread for callables).
5. Bottom-up vs top-down credit in one line each.
   -> Bottom-up = issuer-level selection (four Cs, relative value); top-down = macro credit-cycle sector/quality/beta allocation.
6. Two ways to manage credit liquidity risk.
   -> Hold liquid on-the-run bonds + a cash buffer; use CDS indexes/ETFs (also size to tradability).
7. Why do SD and VaR understate credit risk?
   -> Credit returns are negatively skewed with fat tails; use scenario/stress testing and conditional VaR.
8. Buying CDS protection is a long or short credit position?
   -> Short credit — you pay premium and gain if the entity defaults/widens.
9. Compute DTS: spread duration 5, spread 200 bp.
   -> 5 × 2.00% = 10 (5 × 200 = 1,000 bp-years).
10. Spread 2.00%, Δspread +0.20% × SD 5, POD 2% × LGD 60%. Expected excess return?
   -> 2.00 − 1.00 − 1.20 = −0.20%.
11. To position for expected spread TIGHTENING, what do you do?
   -> Add credit risk: buy credit, sell protection, go down in quality, extend spread duration (raise DTS).
12. Name two risks of structured (tranched) products.
   -> Complexity/model risk and correlation risk in tranches (also prepayment for MBS, lower liquidity).
13. Why is a credit bond's empirical duration often lower than its analytical duration?
   -> Spreads move oppositely to rates (flight to quality), so the spread partly offsets the rate move.
14. CDS protection on 10M, recovery 40% — default payout?
   -> 10M × (1 − 0.40) = 6,000,000.
15. In an expansion phase of the credit cycle, how do you position?
   -> Add credit risk: down in quality, longer spread duration, cyclical sectors (tightening expected).
16. Should a credit manager usually hedge the currency of a foreign bond?
   -> Yes — hedging isolates the pure credit trade, unless the manager holds a currency view.

readings.title: Trade Strategy and Execution
readings.orientation: Orientation. How to turn an investment decision into executed trades at the lowest cost. Nine learning outcomes: the MOTIVATIONS to trade and how they set urgency, the INPUTS to a trading strategy, the execution BENCHMARKS, recommending a strategy, the ALGORITHM classes, the market MICROSTRUCTURE of each asset class, measuring trade COST (implementation shortfall), evaluating execution, and trade GOVERNANCE. The calculation core is the IMPLEMENTATION-SHORTFALL decomposition, worked below. Learn the urgency-driven strategy logic, the three algorithm classes, the benchmark types, and the implementation-shortfall calculation cold.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Trading and Performance
Reading: Trade Strategy and Execution
Orientation. How to turn an investment decision into executed trades at the lowest cost. Nine learning outcomes: the MOTIVATIONS to trade and how they set urgency, the INPUTS to a trading strategy, the execution BENCHMARKS, recommending a strategy, the ALGORITHM classes, the market MICROSTRUCTURE of each asset class, measuring trade COST (implementation shortfall), evaluating execution, and trade GOVERNANCE. The calculation core is the IMPLEMENTATION-SHORTFALL decomposition, worked below. Learn the urgency-driven strategy logic, the three algorithm classes, the benchmark types, and the implementation-shortfall calculation cold.

LOS 1 — Motivations to trade and urgency
LOS: discuss motivations to trade and how they relate to trading strategy
What you must do:  Link each trade motivation to its urgency and the resulting strategy.
The MOTIVATION for a trade determines its URGENCY, which drives the strategy:
	•	Profit-seeking (alpha-driven) — acting on new information or a decaying signal → HIGH urgency; trade quickly before the alpha erodes, accepting market impact.
	•	Risk-management / hedging — adjusting risk exposure → moderate-to-high urgency.
	•	Cash-flow driven (client inflows/outflows, dividends) — lower urgency; cost-minimizing, patient trading.
	•	Rebalancing — scheduled and predictable → low urgency, cost-sensitive.
	•	Liquidity-motivated (raising cash) — can be urgent if cash is needed quickly.
The trader's dilemma. Every trade balances MARKET IMPACT (trading fast moves the price against you) against TIMING/OPPORTUNITY RISK (trading slowly risks the price moving away before you finish). Urgent, alpha-driven trades pay for immediacy to avoid opportunity cost; patient, cost-driven trades trade slowly to minimize impact. Matching urgency to this trade-off is the heart of trade strategy.
Alpha decay and the cost frontier
The urgency decision can be framed as a trade-off frontier. Trading FAST incurs high market impact but low timing risk and captures the alpha before it decays; trading SLOW incurs low impact but high timing risk and may miss the alpha. Plotting expected cost (impact + timing) against the trading horizon gives an efficient frontier: the optimal speed depends on the alpha's DECAY rate and the trader's aversion to timing uncertainty. A fast-decaying signal or a risk-averse trader chooses a faster (higher-impact) schedule; a slow signal and a patient trader choose a slower one. This is the conceptual basis for the arrival-price (implementation-shortfall) algorithm, which solves this trade-off explicitly.
Exam focus: Motivation sets URGENCY, which sets strategy. Alpha-driven (decaying signal) → urgent, aggressive (accept impact to avoid opportunity cost). Cash-flow/rebalancing → low urgency, patient (minimize impact). The trader's dilemma: market impact (fast) vs timing/opportunity risk (slow). Optimal speed depends on alpha decay and risk aversion — a cost frontier the arrival-price algo solves.
LOS 2 — Inputs to selecting a trading strategy
LOS: discuss inputs to the selection of a trading strategy
What you must do:  List the inputs the trader weighs to choose a strategy.
	•	Order characteristics — side, SIZE relative to average daily volume (ADV), and urgency.
	•	Security characteristics — liquidity, volatility, bid-ask spread, and market cap.
	•	Market conditions — current volatility, liquidity, and news/events.
	•	Risk/urgency of the order — how fast the alpha decays.
	•	Cost trade-off — expected market impact versus timing risk.
	•	Constraints — client instructions, regulatory rules, and any restrictions.
A large order relative to ADV in an illiquid, volatile name is hard to trade cheaply — it forces a slower schedule or a search for hidden liquidity; a small order in a liquid name can be executed quickly at low cost. The trader combines these inputs to choose the venue, timing, and algorithm.
Estimating market impact
A key input is the PRE-TRADE estimate of market impact, produced by cost models that predict impact from order SIZE relative to ADV, the security's VOLATILITY and SPREAD, and the planned trading rate. Larger relative size, higher volatility, and wider spreads all raise expected impact. The trader compares this estimate across strategies — a slower schedule lowers impact but raises timing risk — to pick the lowest expected-cost approach for the order's urgency. These estimates also become the benchmark against which realized cost is later judged: a realized cost far above the pre-trade estimate signals a poor execution or unexpected conditions.
Exam focus: Inputs: order (side, size vs ADV, urgency), security (liquidity, volatility, spread, cap), market conditions, alpha decay, the impact-vs-timing cost trade-off, and constraints. A PRE-TRADE impact estimate (from size vs ADV, volatility, spread, trading rate) guides the choice and becomes the yardstick for evaluating realized cost. Size relative to ADV and liquidity are central.
LOS 3 — Execution benchmarks
LOS: compare benchmarks for trade execution
What you must do:  Compare the pre-trade, intraday, and post-trade benchmarks.
Table 1 — Trade-execution benchmarks
Benchmark
Type
Use
Decision / arrival price
Pre-trade
Total cost vs the decision (implementation shortfall)
VWAP (volume-weighted avg price)
Intraday
Judge execution vs the day's average price
TWAP (time-weighted avg price)
Intraday
Even trading over time; low signaling
Closing price
Post-trade
Index funds priced at the close
The right benchmark depends on the objective. ARRIVAL (decision) price measures the total cost of implementing the decision — the basis for implementation shortfall — and is best for alpha-driven trades. VWAP judges whether the trade beat the day's average, suiting patient orders spread over the day. The CLOSING price suits index funds that value at the close. Choosing a benchmark that matches the trade's motivation is essential, because a strategy optimized for one benchmark (e.g., VWAP) can score poorly on another (e.g., arrival price).
Worked example — VWAP vs arrival-price benchmark
An order arrives at a decision price of 50.00. Over the day the VWAP is 50.30, and the trader executes at an average of 50.25.
Vs VWAP: 50.25 − 50.30 = −0.05 → BEAT VWAP by 5 cents
Vs arrival: 50.25 − 50.00 = +0.25 → implementation shortfall of 25 cents
Same trade, two verdicts: it looks good on VWAP but costly on arrival price, because the price trended up during the day. The benchmark must match the objective.
Exam focus: Benchmarks: arrival/decision price (pre-trade — total cost vs the decision, basis of implementation shortfall, best for alpha trades); VWAP and TWAP (intraday — vs the day's average / even over time); closing price (post-trade — index funds). Match the benchmark to the motivation; a trade can beat VWAP yet show a large arrival-price shortfall if the price trended.
LOS 4 — Recommending a trading strategy
LOS: recommend and justify a trading strategy given relevant facts
What you must do:  Match the strategy to urgency, size, and market conditions, and justify it.
The recommendation follows from the motivation and inputs:
	•	Urgent / alpha-driven — an aggressive, front-loaded ARRIVAL-PRICE (implementation-shortfall) strategy to capture the alpha before it decays, accepting market impact.
	•	Low-urgency / cost-sensitive — a passive SCHEDULED strategy (VWAP/TWAP or a low participation rate) to minimize market impact, accepting timing risk.
	•	Large order in fragmented/illiquid liquidity — a LIQUIDITY-SEEKING strategy (including dark venues) to source size without signaling.
