Justify the recommendation by the impact-versus-timing trade-off: high urgency or fast alpha decay tilts toward speed (impact) despite the cost; low urgency and a large, illiquid order tilts toward patience (timing risk) to save impact cost. Order size relative to ADV, volatility, and spread refine the choice.
Worked example — recommending a strategy
Case A: urgent news, BUY a small position (2% of ADV) in a liquid large-cap before the market reacts.
→ Arrival-price / aggressive algo: alpha decays fast, small+liquid = low impact, speed dominates
Case B: SELL a large position (40% of ADV) in an illiquid small-cap for a client redemption, no urgency.
→ Liquidity-seeking (dark venues) over several days: huge relative size makes impact the dominant cost
The recommendation flips with urgency and size-vs-ADV: fast for urgent/small/liquid, patient and stealthy for large/illiquid.
Exam focus: Recommend by urgency: alpha-driven/urgent → arrival-price (front-loaded, accept impact); low-urgency → scheduled (VWAP/TWAP/low POV, accept timing risk); large/illiquid → liquidity-seeking (incl. dark). Justify via the impact-vs-timing trade-off, order size vs ADV, volatility, and spread — the recommendation flips with urgency and size.
LOS 5 — Trading algorithm classes
LOS: describe factors that typically determine the selection of a trading algorithm class
What you must do:  Describe the three algorithm classes and what drives the choice.
	•	Scheduled (VWAP / TWAP / POV) — trade to a schedule or a percent-of-volume participation rate; predictable and low-signaling. Best for LOW-urgency, larger orders where minimizing impact matters more than speed.
	•	Liquidity-seeking (opportunistic) — search across venues (lit and dark) and trade aggressively when liquidity or price is favorable. Best when liquidity is FRAGMENTED or the order needs to find size.
	•	Arrival-price (implementation-shortfall) — FRONT-LOAD trading to minimize deviation from the arrival price, balancing impact against timing risk. Best for URGENT/alpha-driven orders.
What determines the class. The choice is driven by URGENCY (alpha decay), order SIZE relative to ADV, LIQUIDITY, VOLATILITY, and the bid-ask SPREAD — ultimately the trade-off between market impact and timing risk. High urgency and high volatility push toward arrival-price (trade now); low urgency and large size push toward scheduled; fragmented liquidity pushes toward liquidity-seeking.
Worked example — a percent-of-volume (POV) order
Buy 500,000 shares of a stock trading 5,000,000 shares/day (10% of ADV), using a 20% POV algorithm (participate in 20% of market volume until filled).
If the market trades 5,000,000 that day: algo capacity ≈ 20% × 5,000,000 = 1,000,000
→ the 500,000 fills in roughly half a day
Higher POV (30%) fills faster but with more impact; lower POV (10%) is stealthier but slower and risks the price moving. POV ties the trade to actual liquidity, but a large order relative to ADV still takes time and signals — hence very large orders also use dark venues.
Exam focus: Three algorithm classes: SCHEDULED (VWAP/TWAP/POV — predictable, low-signaling, low-urgency/large orders; POV participates in a set % of volume); LIQUIDITY-SEEKING (opportunistic across venues incl. dark — fragmented liquidity/find size); ARRIVAL-PRICE (implementation-shortfall — front-loaded, urgent/alpha). Choice driven by urgency, size vs ADV, liquidity, volatility, spread — the impact-vs-timing trade-off.
LOS 6 — Market microstructure by asset class
LOS: contrast key characteristics of equity, fixed income, options/futures, OTC derivatives, and spot currency markets for trade implementation
What you must do:  Contrast the market structure of each asset class.
Table 2 — Market structure by asset class
Market
Structure
Implication
Equity
Exchange + dark pools, order-driven, electronic
Transparent, continuous, generally liquid
Fixed income
OTC / dealer, quote-driven (RFQ)
Less transparent/liquid; electronic growing
Options & futures
Exchange-traded, electronic, cleared
Liquid for standard contracts; transparent
OTC derivatives (swaps)
Bilateral/dealer, negotiated (SEFs, clearing)
Less liquid; customized; counterparty risk
Spot currency
OTC, dealer/electronic, decentralized
Very liquid, 24-hour, no central exchange
The structures differ on TRANSPARENCY, LIQUIDITY, and the trading MECHANISM (order-driven exchanges vs quote-driven/RFQ dealer markets), which changes how a trade is best executed. Exchange-traded, order-driven markets (equity, listed derivatives) support algorithmic execution against a visible order book; dealer/RFQ markets (much of fixed income and OTC derivatives) require requesting quotes from dealers and reward relationships and discretion; spot FX is deeply liquid but decentralized.
Trading mechanisms and venues
The mechanism matters as much as the asset class. ORDER-DRIVEN markets (exchanges) match buyers and sellers against a central limit order book, with continuous, transparent prices — well suited to algorithmic execution. QUOTE-DRIVEN (dealer) markets, common in fixed income and OTC derivatives, require requesting quotes (RFQ) from dealers who make prices from inventory — rewarding relationships and discretion. DARK POOLS let large orders trade without displaying size, reducing signaling and impact for block trades. SMART ORDER ROUTERS split an order across lit and dark venues to find the best price and liquidity. The trader chooses the mechanism and venues to fit the order's size, urgency, and the asset class's structure.
Exam focus: Equity = exchange/dark, order-driven, transparent, liquid. Fixed income = OTC/dealer, quote-driven (RFQ), less transparent/liquid. Options/futures = exchange-traded, cleared, liquid for standard contracts. OTC derivatives = bilateral/negotiated (SEFs/clearing), less liquid, counterparty risk. Spot FX = OTC, decentralized, very liquid, 24-hour. Mechanism matters: order-driven (exchange/algos) vs quote-driven (RFQ/dealers); dark pools hide block size; smart routers split across venues.
LOS 7 — Measuring trade cost: implementation shortfall
LOS: explain how trade costs are measured and determine the cost of a trade
What you must do:  Decompose and compute implementation shortfall.
IMPLEMENTATION SHORTFALL (IS) is the total cost of executing a decision — the difference between the value of a hypothetical 'paper' portfolio transacted at the DECISION price and the actual executed portfolio. It has three components:
IS = execution cost + opportunity (unfilled) cost + fees
Execution cost = (execution price − decision price) × shares filled
Opportunity cost = (final price − decision price) × shares NOT filled
Worked example — implementation shortfall
Order: BUY 10,000 shares, decision price 100 (order value 1,000,000). 8,000 shares fill at an average of 100.20; the price then moves to 100.50 and the remaining 2,000 go unfilled. Commissions are 0.01/share on the filled shares.
Execution cost = (100.20 − 100) × 8,000 = 0.20 × 8,000 = 1,600
Opportunity cost = (100.50 − 100) × 2,000 = 0.50 × 2,000 = 1,000
Fees = 0.01 × 8,000 = 80
IS = 1,600 + 1,000 + 80 = 2,680
IS in bps = 2,680 / 1,000,000 = 0.268% = 26.8 bps
The 26.8 bps total splits into execution (16 bps), opportunity (10 bps), and fees (0.8 bps). The unfilled portion cost 10 bps because the price ran away — the price of NOT completing the order.
Decomposing implementation shortfall
IS is often broken into finer components for diagnosis. DELAY (slippage) cost is the price move between the decision and when trading actually begins — the cost of not acting immediately. MARKET IMPACT is the price move caused by the trading itself (a temporary component that reverts plus a permanent one). TIMING/opportunity cost covers price moves during and after the trading window, including the unfilled portion. Plus explicit costs (commissions, fees, taxes). Separating these tells the manager whether cost came from acting too slowly (delay), trading too aggressively (impact), or the market moving (timing) — each pointing to a different fix.
Exam focus: Implementation shortfall = paper-portfolio return (at decision price) minus actual return = total execution cost. IS = execution cost [(exec − decision price) × filled] + opportunity cost [(final − decision price) × unfilled] + fees. Finer split: delay (slippage), market impact (temporary + permanent), timing/opportunity, and explicit costs. Express in bps as total cost / (decision price × order shares). Worked: 1,600 + 1,000 + 80 = 2,680 = 26.8 bps.
LOS 8 — Evaluating trade execution
LOS: evaluate the execution of a trade
What you must do:  Explain how execution quality is judged.
Execution is evaluated with TRADE COST ANALYSIS (TCA): compare the realized cost against the chosen benchmark (arrival price → implementation shortfall; or VWAP), and against the PRE-TRADE cost estimate. TCA decomposes IS into delay, market impact, timing, and opportunity components to see WHERE cost arose, and compares the result to expectations and peer/venue data. The evaluation asks whether the STRATEGY and ALGORITHM matched the order's urgency and the market conditions — a low-urgency order that paid high impact by trading too fast, or an urgent order that lost alpha by trading too slowly, indicates a mismatch.
Good evaluation is done consistently and over many trades, not just one, since any single trade's cost is noisy; systematic underperformance versus benchmarks signals a process problem (poor algo/venue choice or information leakage).
TCA metrics and reporting
Trade cost analysis reports several metrics: implementation shortfall (vs arrival price), performance versus VWAP/TWAP, realized-versus-estimated cost, and the decomposition into delay, impact, timing, and fees. Because a single trade's cost is dominated by market noise, TCA is most informative AGGREGATED over many trades and compared to peer and venue benchmarks — revealing whether a broker, algorithm, or venue systematically adds or destroys value. Firms use TCA both to improve execution (adjusting algorithm and venue choices) and to demonstrate best execution to clients and regulators.
Exam focus: Evaluate with TCA: compare realized cost to the benchmark (arrival price/IS or VWAP) and to the pre-trade estimate; decompose IS into delay/impact/timing/opportunity; check the strategy/algorithm matched urgency and conditions. Assess over MANY trades (single-trade cost is noisy) vs peer/venue data; persistent underperformance signals a process problem. TCA both improves execution and demonstrates best execution.
LOS 9 — Trade governance
LOS: evaluate a firm's trading procedures, including processes, disclosures, and record keeping
What you must do:  List the elements of good trade governance.
Good trade governance ensures trades serve the CLIENT'S interest and meet the firm's fiduciary and best-execution duties. Its elements:
	•	A documented trade policy — written policies and procedures for how orders are handled and routed.
	•	Best-execution obligation — seeking the best result for the client, monitored via TCA.
	•	Fair allocation — orders and fills allocated fairly across clients (no favoritism).
	•	Disclosure — of trading practices and conflicts (soft dollars, order-routing arrangements, payment for order flow).
	•	Record-keeping — complete, auditable records of orders, executions, and decisions.
	•	Periodic review — regular monitoring and review of execution quality and broker/venue selection.
Best execution and conflicts
Best execution is the duty to seek the most favorable terms REASONABLY available for the client — not merely the lowest commission, but the best total result (price, impact, speed, and likelihood of execution) given the order. Governance must manage and DISCLOSE conflicts that can compromise it: SOFT DOLLARS (using client commissions to buy research, which can inflate commissions), PAYMENT FOR ORDER FLOW (routing orders to a venue that pays the broker, which may not give the best price), and directing trades to affiliated brokers. Firms address these with written best-execution policies, ongoing TCA monitoring, fair-allocation rules, full disclosure, and independent review — ensuring the client's interest, not the firm's, drives execution.
Exam focus: Good trade governance: a documented trade policy, a best-execution obligation (best TOTAL result — price/impact/speed/likelihood, not just commission; monitored by TCA), fair allocation across clients, disclosure of practices/conflicts (soft dollars, order routing, PFOF, affiliated brokers), complete record-keeping, and periodic review — all to serve the client and meet fiduciary duty.
Worked case — executing a large, urgent order
Suppose a manager decides to buy 1,000,000 shares of a mid-cap stock (25% of ADV) on a strong, fast-decaying alpha signal. The reading's tools combine:
	•	Motivation & urgency — the alpha decays quickly, so urgency is HIGH; but 25% of ADV is large, so pure aggression would cause heavy impact. The trader balances the two.
	•	Strategy & algorithm — use an ARRIVAL-PRICE (implementation-shortfall) algorithm, front-loaded to capture much of the alpha early, supplemented by LIQUIDITY-SEEKING routing (including dark pools) to source size without signaling the full order.
	•	Benchmark — measure against the ARRIVAL price, since the goal is to implement the decision at low total cost (VWAP would merely reward matching the day, not the objective).
	•	Execution & evaluation — after trading, run TCA: decompose the implementation shortfall into delay, impact, timing, and fees, and compare to the pre-trade estimate to judge whether the aggressive-but-stealthy approach was right.
	•	Governance — ensure the order was handled per policy, allocated fairly if pooled across clients, routed for best execution, and fully recorded.
The result trades some market impact (from front-loading) for reduced opportunity cost (capturing the decaying alpha), sources size through dark venues to limit signaling, and is judged on arrival-price implementation shortfall — a clean expression of 'urgent but large.'
Explicit vs implicit trading costs
Trading costs fall into two broad categories. EXPLICIT costs are directly observable: commissions, fees, taxes, and the bid-ask spread paid. IMPLICIT costs are not invoiced but are often larger: MARKET IMPACT (the price move caused by the order), DELAY/slippage (the price move before trading begins), and OPPORTUNITY cost (the return foregone on the unfilled portion). Implicit costs dominate for large orders in less-liquid names, and they are precisely what implementation shortfall captures — which is why sophisticated cost measurement focuses on the implicit costs, not just the visible commission. Reducing total cost therefore means managing the implicit components — through algorithm and venue choice and trade sizing — not merely negotiating a lower commission.
Algorithm classes — summary
Table 3 — Class, mechanism, and best use
Class
Mechanism
Best for
Scheduled (VWAP/TWAP/POV)
Trade to a schedule or % of volume
Low-urgency, larger orders; minimize impact
Liquidity-seeking
Opportunistic across lit/dark venues
Fragmented liquidity; sourcing block size
Arrival-price (IS)
Front-loaded vs the arrival price
Urgent / alpha-driven orders
The class follows the impact-vs-timing trade-off: scheduled algorithms minimize impact at the cost of timing risk, arrival-price algorithms do the reverse, and liquidity-seeking algorithms hunt for size when liquidity is scattered. Urgency, order size versus ADV, and liquidity pick the class.
Quick reference — key concepts
The essentials worth memorizing
Motivation → urgency → strategy; trader's dilemma = impact (fast) vs timing (slow)
Inputs: order size vs ADV, liquidity, volatility, spread, urgency, constraints
Benchmarks: arrival/decision (IS), VWAP/TWAP (intraday), close (post-trade)
Algo classes: scheduled (VWAP/TWAP/POV), liquidity-seeking, arrival-price (IS)
Markets: equity/listed = order-driven exchange; FI/OTC = dealer/RFQ; FX = OTC liquid
IS = execution cost + opportunity (unfilled) cost + fees
  Execution = (exec − decision)×filled ; Opportunity = (final − decision)×unfilled
IS in bps = total cost / (decision price × order shares)
Evaluate with TCA over many trades; governance = policy, best-ex, disclosure, records
Rapid self-test
Cover the answers and check yourself
1. Which motivation creates the highest urgency, and why?
   -> Alpha-driven trades (a decaying signal) — trade fast before the edge erodes.
2. State the trader's dilemma.
   -> Market impact (trading fast moves the price) vs timing/opportunity risk (trading slow lets the price move away).
3. Name three inputs to selecting a trading strategy.
   -> Order size vs ADV, security liquidity/volatility/spread, and urgency (also market conditions, constraints).
4. Which benchmark underlies implementation shortfall?
   -> The arrival/decision price.
5. Which benchmark suits an index fund pricing at the close?
   -> The closing price.
6. For an urgent, alpha-driven order, which algorithm class?
   -> Arrival-price (implementation-shortfall), front-loaded.
7. What is a scheduled (POV) algorithm best for?
   -> Low-urgency, larger orders — predictable, low-signaling participation.
8. Contrast equity and fixed-income market structure.
   -> Equity = order-driven exchange/dark, transparent, liquid; FI = OTC/dealer, quote-driven (RFQ), less transparent/liquid.
9. Compute execution cost: buy 8,000 filled at 100.20, decision 100.
   -> (100.20 − 100) × 8,000 = 1,600.
10. 2,000 unfilled, final price 100.50, decision 100. Opportunity cost?
   -> (100.50 − 100) × 2,000 = 1,000.
11. With 80 in fees, total IS and in bps on a 1,000,000 order?
   -> 1,600 + 1,000 + 80 = 2,680 = 26.8 bps.
12. Name three elements of good trade governance.
   -> Documented policy/best-execution, disclosure of conflicts (soft dollars/routing), and record-keeping (also fair allocation, review).
13. A trade beats VWAP but shows a large arrival-price shortfall. What happened?
   -> The price trended (up for a buy) during the day — good vs the average, costly vs the decision price.
14. What does a percent-of-volume (POV) algorithm do?
   -> Participates in a set percentage of market volume until filled — higher POV fills faster with more impact.
15. What drives a pre-trade market-impact estimate?
   -> Order size vs ADV, the security's volatility and spread, and the planned trading rate.
16. Does best execution mean the lowest commission?
   -> No — the best TOTAL result (price, impact, speed, likelihood), not just the lowest commission.
17. What is delay (slippage) cost within implementation shortfall?
   -> The price move between the decision and when trading actually begins — the cost of not acting immediately.
18. Why assess execution over many trades rather than one?
   -> A single trade's cost is dominated by market noise; systematic underperformance across trades signals a process problem.
19. Name two conflicts trade governance must disclose.
   -> Soft dollars and payment for order flow (also directing trades to affiliated brokers).
20. For a large, illiquid, non-urgent sale, which algorithm class?
   -> Liquidity-seeking (including dark venues) over time — impact is the dominant cost, so trade stealthily.
21. Distinguish explicit from implicit trading costs.
   -> Explicit = commissions/fees/taxes/spread (observable); implicit = market impact, delay, opportunity cost (often larger).
22. Which cost category does implementation shortfall mainly capture?
   -> The implicit costs (impact, delay, opportunity) plus explicit fees — the full cost of the decision.
23. What is a dark pool used for?
   -> Trading large orders without displaying size, reducing signaling and market impact for blocks.
24. What does a smart order router do?
   -> Splits an order across lit and dark venues to find the best price and available liquidity.

readings.title: Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct
readings.orientation: Orientation. CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct — structure of the Professional Conduct Program, disciplinary process, and ethical responsibilities under each Standard. Two learning outcomes.
readings.content:
CFA® Level III — Study Notes
Topic Area: Ethical and Professional Standards
